unit.name: Closed-System Transfer Device (CSTD) System
unit.cfj: Prevent release of hazardous drug vapors and aerosols during drug transfer operations

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a candidate is accepted when its true hazardous-drug release exceeds the permissible threshold
rows.plain: Avoid accepting a product or material when the amount of hazardous medicine that could escape is actually above the allowed limit. This helps protect people from unsafe exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a candidate whose true release exceeds the permissible threshold institutionalizes exposure across every future use; highly relevant and severe due to systemic scope.
rows.sat_rat: Because no harmonized, mandatory pass-fail threshold exists and vendor tests use inconsistent methods, false acceptance of leaky candidates remains a well-documented recurring risk. Current CSTD evaluations rely on vendor-supplied data and draft protocol testing that takes weeks, but the delay is secondary to the underlying uncertainty about acceptance. Judging whether a candidate truly stays below a release threshold demands scarce industrial-hygiene and analytical-chemistry expertise that most purchase influencers lack. Independent verification testing is expensive, so most buyers accept manufacturer claims rather than fund confirmatory studies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recommended candidate leaves worker exposure above the defined protection limit
rows.plain: Make sure the recommended choice does not expose workers to more than the allowed safe amount of danger. This protects workers from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: A recommended candidate leaving exposure above the protection limit is a selection error that guarantees chronic overexposure; relevant and severe due to systemic reach.
rows.sat_rat: With inconsistent thresholds and surrogate-to-drug uncertainty, recommended candidates can still leave exposure above protective limits. Linking a recommendation to actual worker exposure limits requires exposure modeling that extends evaluation timelines. Translating containment data into worker-protection compliance needs toxicology and OEL expertise that is scarce. Validating exposure below protection limits demands biomonitoring or extensive sampling that many sites cannot afford.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a worker-relevant hazardous-drug vapor or aerosol release remains undetected
rows.plain: Make sure a worker’s exposure to a dangerous medicine mist or vapor does not go unnoticed. Early detection helps protect workers from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected worker-relevant release means exposure continues unnoticed; relevant to the detection layer and severe because harm accrues silently.
rows.sat_rat: Established surrogate methods miss short-lived and low-concentration vapor releases, so undetected releases remain a chronic gap. Detecting undetected releases requires sensitive surrogate sampling campaigns that add days to weeks per candidate. Setting up wipe and air sampling capable of catching worker-relevant releases requires specialized hygienist judgement rarely available in-house. Comprehensive sampling and lab analysis to rule out missed releases is costly and often trimmed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which airborne hazardous-drug exposure exceeds applicable occupational limits during sterile fill-finish activities
rows.plain: Keep workers' exposure to airborne dangerous medicines within the allowed safety limits during sterile product filling. This helps protect their health.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding occupational exposure limits during fill-finish is precisely what the containment job must prevent; the degree of exceedance directly scales harm, making both proxies high.
rows.sat_rat: CSTDs plus engineering controls reduce most releases but validated closed-system performance under real fill-finish conditions is inconsistent, leaving recurring exceedance risk. Real-time airborne HD monitoring is rare, so exposure excursions during fill-finish are typically detected long after the fact, if at all. Keeping airborne HD below occupational limits depends heavily on skilled containment design and expert industrial-hygiene judgement that most facilities cannot staff continuously. Achieving low airborne levels requires substantial capital in isolators, CSTDs and ventilation, and many sites under-invest.

rows.source_job: Contain Spills at the Point of Failure
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous drug material escaping beyond the transfer-failure point before containment
rows.plain: Keep as much hazardous medicine as possible from escaping past the point where a transfer fails. This protects people and the surrounding area from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Material escaping beyond a transfer-failure point before containment defines the magnitude of a breach; central with high severity when failures occur.
rows.sat_rat: Once a CSTD fails, there is little engineered containment of the material that escapes the failure point, making this poorly served. When a transfer failure occurs, escape happens faster than any manual response can react. Limiting escape beyond a failure point depends heavily on operator vigilance and spill-response skill. Spill kits and containment mats are cheap but reactive materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous-drug release remains undetected under proposed use conditions
rows.plain: Make sure dangerous medicine does not escape into the surrounding area during the proposed use. This protects people from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Release remaining undetected under proposed use conditions means silent chronic exposure; relevant to the detection layer and severe because unrecognized harm compounds.
rows.sat_rat: Standard vendor packages report only tested conditions, leaving release under unexamined use conditions chronically undetected. Undetected release by definition surfaces only later, so no timely signal exists during evaluation. Detecting concealed release paths requires deep aerosol/vapor testing expertise rarely present on the buying team. Surrogate marker testing to expose hidden release is expensive and inconsistently commissioned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous drug vapor or aerosol entering the operator breathing zone during transfer
rows.plain: Keep as much hazardous drug mist or vapor as possible out of the worker’s breathing area during transfer, so the worker does not inhale it.
rows.imp_rat: Vapor/aerosol entering the operator breathing zone is the terminal exposure event the CSTD exists to prevent; both relevance and severity are near-maximal since inhalation causes direct occupational harm.
rows.sat_rat: NIOSH-tested CSTDs demonstrably reduce breathing-zone vapor to very low levels, so this core need is well served by standard practice. Modern CSTDs contain vapor in real time during transfer so there is no delay in protection once connected. Airtight membrane and needle-free designs handle vapor containment mechanically, reducing dependence on operator technique. CSTD consumables add per-transfer cost but this is a mature, budgeted expense in hazardous drug programs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous vapors or aerosols escaping containment during drug transfer
rows.plain: Prevent hazardous drug mist or vapor from escaping the protective enclosure during transfer, so workers nearby are not exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Escape of vapors/aerosols from containment is the core failure mode the CFJ addresses; highly central with severe health consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CSTDs plus USP make containment highly consistent, though seal failures and mis-engagements still occasionally breach. CSTDs act instantly at the point of transfer so there is no time lag in containment once the device is engaged. Mature CSTD hardware makes leak-free transfer largely mechanical, reducing dependence on operator finesse though correct engagement still matters. CSTD consumables add per-transfer cost but this is an accepted line item on well-run floors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a containment breach remains undetected during transfer
rows.plain: Make sure a failure in the protective enclosure is noticed during transfer, so hazardous material does not escape without anyone knowing.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected containment breach during transfer means unrecognized ongoing release; relevant to detection and severe due to silent exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Most CSTD breaches give no active alarm, so undetected breaches remain a real recurring gap. Undetected breaches persist until noticed, which can be a prolonged period during transfer. Detection relies on operator observation with no engineered breach indicator, demanding constant attention. No dedicated detection instrumentation is typically deployed, keeping cost low but capability absent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an airborne hazardous-drug exposure excursion remains undetected
rows.plain: Make sure an unusually high exposure to hazardous drug mist or vapor is detected, so people can be protected quickly.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected airborne exposure excursion means silent overexposure; relevant to detection and severe due to unrecognized dose.
rows.sat_rat: Airborne HD is odorless and invisible and no cheap reliable continuous sensor exists, so many excursions go entirely undetected. Detection typically relies on periodic wipe sampling with lab turnaround of days, so excursions remain unseen in real time. Interpreting surface-wipe data to infer airborne excursions requires specialist industrial-hygiene expertise not always on hand. Frequent sampling and real-time sensors are expensive, so surveillance is sparse.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous vapor or aerosol forced across the transfer boundary by vial pressure
rows.plain: Keep hazardous mist or vapor from being pushed across the transfer boundary by pressure inside a vial. This helps protect workers and the surrounding area when hazardous medicines are prepared.
rows.imp_rat: Vial pressure forcing vapor/aerosol across the boundary is the primary physical driver of CSTD failure; highly central and severe as it defeats containment.
rows.sat_rat: Engineered pressure balancing reliably limits vapor forced across the boundary, though performance varies by device design and drug volatility. Automatic pressure equalization happens during transfer with no separate step delaying the operator. The device manages vial pressure so the worker no longer relies on skilled incremental venting. Pressure-equalization mechanisms raise device cost modestly within accepted budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous vapor or aerosol released from the fluid path during dose transfer
rows.plain: As doses are moved, as little harmful vapor or fine mist as possible should escape from the liquid-handling path. This helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Minimizing hazardous vapor/aerosol released from the fluid path during dose transfer is the exact CFJ; relevance is maximal and severity is high because releases directly expose workers to carcinogenic drugs.
rows.sat_rat: NIOSH-vetted CSTDs plus BSC/isolator practice make fluid-path release during transfer uncommon, though seal-integrity variation persists. Mature CSTDs add negligible time to transfers once the operator is trained, so containment during transfer does not slow the job. Standard membrane-to-membrane or needle-free CSTDs contain the fluid path with minimal operator finesse required. CSTD components add per-dose consumable cost but are widely budgeted as standard hazardous-drug practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that decision-relevant vapor or aerosol release remains undetected during qualification testing
rows.plain: Avoid missing an important release of vapor or fine spray during testing. Detecting it helps show whether the equipment protects people and the surrounding area as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected release during qualification lets an unsafe device pass; relevant to the verification job and severe since the miss propagates to all deployments.
rows.sat_rat: Published CSTD test methods vary in detection limits and no single approach guarantees that a decision-relevant release will be caught, leaving a real undetected-release risk. Detection sensitivity depends on analytical method turnaround which existing surrogate-gas or titanium-tetrachloride methods provide within a test campaign, so time is not the binding constraint. Correctly configuring and interpreting detection methods still leans heavily on experienced containment testers who know where subthreshold releases hide. High-sensitivity analytical detection adds instrumentation and consumable expense that many evaluations trim, weakening detection margins.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transfer method is accepted despite hazardous-drug release exceeding containment intent
rows.plain: Avoid approving a transfer method if it lets more hazardous medicine escape than the containment system is meant to allow. This helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a transfer method despite release exceeding containment intent is a qualification-gate error that permits ongoing exposure; relevant and severe because a systemic bad decision affects all future transfers.
rows.sat_rat: No standardized acceptance threshold ties vendor containment data to a defensible pass/fail, so methods with marginal release still slip through with uncomfortable frequency. Reaching an accept/reject decision takes weeks of dossier review, but the delay is not the core failure here. Judging whether release exceeds containment intent demands scarce toxicology and containment-engineering expertise that few purchase influencers can independently apply. A wrong acceptance carries enormous downstream cost in recalls and exposure liability, poorly bounded by current review budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous vapor or aerosol entering the worker breathing zone during preparation
rows.plain: Limit the amount of dangerous drug mist or vapor that reaches the air a worker breathes while preparing it. This helps reduce the worker’s exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Vapor/aerosol entering the worker breathing zone during preparation is a direct exposure endpoint; central to the job with serious health severity.
rows.sat_rat: The engineering-control hierarchy reliably keeps breathing-zone exposure low, with residual risk mainly from technique lapses. Containment engineering works passively during preparation so it costs the worker no added time. Combined CSTD and ventilated cabinet require training but keep the breathing zone protected without moment-to-moment vigilance. The layered controls (cabinet plus CSTD plus PPE) are costly but standard and reimbursed as baseline practice.

rows.source_job: Contain Spills at the Point of Failure
rows.stmt: Minimize the time from transfer failure onset until released hazardous drug material is contained
rows.plain: Contain dangerous drug material as soon as a transfer starts to fail. This limits how far the material can spread and reduces harm to people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Time from failure onset to containment sets total released mass during a breach; relevant to limiting harm and severe when breaches occur.
rows.sat_rat: There is no automated failure-containment mechanism, so response time is highly variable. Time from failure onset to containment depends on human detection and reaction, which is inherently slow. Fast containment requires trained, practiced spill response that not all operators reliably possess. Spill response uses low-cost materials but consumes operator time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that workers remain in an affected area during an airborne hazardous-drug exposure excursion
rows.plain: Help workers leave or avoid an affected area quickly when a dangerous drug gets into the air. This reduces the chance that they breathe in harmful material.
rows.imp_rat: Workers remaining in an affected area during an excursion prolongs exposure; relevant to harm mitigation and severe due to cumulative dose, though it is a response rather than prevention.
rows.sat_rat: Because most HD excursions are invisible and undetected, the mechanism to remove workers rarely triggers, so this need is poorly served. Without real-time alarms, workers often stay in an affected area for extended periods before any evacuation is triggered. Recognizing an invisible vapor excursion and deciding to evacuate relies on worker vigilance and training that is inconsistently applied. Continuous area monitoring and alarm systems that would prompt evacuation are costly and seldom deployed for HD vapors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which challenge conditions underrepresent credible worst-case transfer-system use
rows.plain: Make sure testing reflects the most believable difficult ways the transfer system might be used. This gives more confidence that the system will work safely in real situations.
rows.imp_rat: Challenge conditions underrepresenting worst-case use lets devices pass that fail in reality; relevant to qualification validity and severe because it systematically permits release.
rows.sat_rat: Standard test methods commonly use idealized conditions that underrepresent aggressive real-world use, so this gap is chronic and poorly served. Defining worst-case challenge scenarios up front takes protocol-development time but is a one-time effort. Identifying credible worst cases demands deep process and containment expertise that few reviewers possess. Building worst-case rigs and fixtures costs money that lean evaluations often avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous drug vapor or aerosol escaping during transfer-path separation
rows.plain: Prevent as much dangerous medicine vapor or mist as possible from escaping when two connected parts are separated. This helps protect people from breathing or touching the medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Vapor/aerosol escaping during transfer-path separation targets the exact CFJ at a known critical step; central with high inhalation-exposure severity.
rows.sat_rat: CSTDs are specifically designed and tested to prevent vapor/aerosol escape at separation, so this is well served. Containment during separation is instantaneous and adds no separation time. The closed membrane interface removes reliance on operator care during the separation step. Separation containment is inherent to the consumable already purchased.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualified runs in which hazardous vapor or aerosol release exceeds acceptance limits
rows.plain: Keep harmful vapors or fine airborne droplets from exceeding the allowed amount during tested operations. This protects people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Percentage of qualified runs exceeding acceptance limits directly measures containment failure rate; central to demonstrating the job and severe as each exceedance means real release.
rows.sat_rat: Because acceptance limits and methods differ across protocols, the fraction of runs exceeding limits is inconsistently characterized and comparability is weak. Reporting pass/fail against limits is quick once runs complete, so timing rarely blocks this need. Judging whether an exceedance is real requires trained interpretation of borderline results. Additional replicate runs to confirm exceedances raise testing cost but are routinely budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous drug escaping while a transfer-path obstruction is cleared
rows.plain: Lower the chance that dangerous medicine escapes while someone clears a blockage in the transfer route. This helps protect workers and prevent spills.
rows.imp_rat: Drug escaping while clearing an obstruction is release during a hands-on high-risk task; relevant and severe.
rows.sat_rat: There is no engineered containment during obstruction clearing, so escape is a real and variable risk. Clearing an obstruction is a manual, time-consuming task with drug potentially escaping meanwhile. Safely clearing obstruction requires experienced judgement to avoid releasing drug. No special tooling; only PPE and cleanup materials involved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of missed short-lived releases during connection transfer disconnection or disposal
rows.plain: Reduce how often brief releases are missed while equipment is being connected, disconnected, moved, or discarded. This helps people recognize and control short exposures that may otherwise go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Missed short-lived releases across steps mean transient peaks go unrecorded; relevant to detection completeness and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Transient spikes during connect/disconnect/disposal are routinely missed by time-averaged sampling, a well-known weakness. Catching short-lived releases requires high-frequency, event-synchronized sampling that lengthens test setup. Timing sampling to transient events during connection and disposal needs experienced hands. High-time-resolution monitoring equipment is expensive and seldom deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of pressurized hazardous drug ejecting toward the worker during reconstitution or withdrawal
rows.plain: Lower the chance that pressurized dangerous medicine sprays toward a worker while it is being mixed or drawn out. This helps prevent injury and exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Pressurized ejection toward the worker during reconstitution is a violent, high-dose exposure event; central to containment and severe because it can splash concentrated drug onto skin/eyes.
rows.sat_rat: Balanced-pressure devices greatly cut ejection events, though high-pressure or oily drugs still occasionally spray if technique falters. Pressure-equalized withdrawal takes about the same time as conventional technique once mastered. Pressure-equalizing CSTDs reduce reliance on the manual pressure-balancing skill that traditional venting demanded. The pressure-management components add consumable cost but are routine spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that trapped pressure causes sudden hazardous drug discharge during flow restoration
rows.plain: Make sure trapped pressure cannot suddenly force out harmful medicine when flow is started again. This protects people and the surrounding area from an unexpected discharge.
rows.imp_rat: Trapped pressure causing sudden discharge on flow restoration is a violent release event; central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD pressure equalization helps but trapped-pressure discharge during restoration still occurs unpredictably, leaving a meaningful gap. Sudden discharge on flow restoration is instantaneous, offering no reaction time. Managing trapped pressure requires operator awareness and controlled restoration technique. Pressure-equalization features add no separate cost beyond the device.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous-drug vapor or aerosol releases remain below protocol detection capability
rows.plain: Make sure any harmful medicine fumes or fine particles released during testing are detectable by the agreed testing method. This prevents a release from going unnoticed simply because the test cannot detect it.
rows.imp_rat: Releases below protocol detection capability can go unrecorded; relevant to detection adequacy and moderately severe because low-level exposure accrues.
rows.sat_rat: Protocol detection limits routinely sit above meaningful release levels, so genuine releases go unreported. Improving protocol detection capability adds method-development time before testing can begin. Judging whether protocol sensitivity is adequate requires analytical-chemistry skill in short supply. More sensitive protocols raise per-sample analytical cost significantly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that candidate hazardous-drug releases remain below the reported detection capability
rows.plain: Make sure dangerous drug releases are not so small that the test falsely suggests there was no release. This allows different options to be compared using results that can actually be detected.
rows.imp_rat: Candidate releases below reported detection capability can pass despite real hazard; relevant to measurement adequacy and severe because it masks exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Many candidate releases fall below reported method detection capability, so real releases are frequently reported as non-detects. Confirming releases are above detection limits requires repeated low-level analysis that slows evaluation. Interpreting detection-limit adequacy demands analytical-method expertise not commonly held by buyers. Lower detection limits require costlier instrumentation and longer analytical runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which challenge-material release behavior diverges from hazardous-drug release behavior in sterile fill-finish handling
rows.plain: Make sure test materials are released in a way that reflects how dangerous drug material could be released during sterile filling and finishing. This helps give a realistic picture of the remaining risk to workers.
rows.imp_rat: Challenge-material release behavior diverging from the drug in fill-finish invalidates results; relevant to representativeness and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Surrogate behavior diverges from drug behavior under fill-finish conditions, a rarely-closed representativeness gap. Characterizing surrogate-vs-drug behavior specifically for fill-finish adds analytical time. This requires niche expertise combining surrogate science and fill-finish process knowledge. Such targeted characterization studies are expensive and infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incompletely engaged transfer connection is accepted as secure before fluid movement
rows.plain: Prevent a partly connected transfer fitting from being treated as safely secured before liquid starts moving. An insecure connection could leak or allow contamination.
rows.imp_rat: An incompletely engaged connection accepted as secure before flow is a direct leak precursor; central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Click-lock feedback helps but there is no engineered interlock preventing flow through an unsecured connection, so errors persist. Acceptance of an insecure connection is immediate at the connection step. Determining security before flow relies on operator confirmation rather than a lockout. No incremental cost for this judgement step.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transient containment breaches missed during connection, transfer, or disconnection
rows.plain: Reduce the number of short-lived leaks or openings that go unnoticed during joining, moving, or separating equipment. Finding these problems quickly helps prevent contamination from escaping or entering.
rows.imp_rat: Transient containment breaches missed during connection/transfer/disconnection hide short peak exposures; relevant to detection completeness and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Transient breaches during connect/transfer/disconnect are largely missed today, an essentially unserved need. Transient breaches occur and vanish faster than any manual check. Catching momentary containment breaches exceeds unaided human perception. No affordable standard detection technology is in routine use.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the acceptance threshold falls below measurement capability under defined transfer conditions
rows.plain: Set the allowed limit high enough that the measuring method can actually detect whether the limit has been exceeded. A limit below the method’s ability to measure cannot reliably protect against a problem.
rows.imp_rat: An acceptance threshold below measurement capability makes qualification meaningless; relevant to test validity and severe as it certifies unmeasured hazard.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance thresholds are commonly set below what methods can reliably measure, making pass results scientifically meaningless. Aligning acceptance thresholds with measurement capability is a definitional step that adds little duration once decided. Setting a threshold that is actually measurable demands metrology and statistical judgement that is scarce. There is little direct cost to defining the relationship, but hitting an ambitious threshold costs more in sensitive testing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which hazardous peak release is obscured by averaging across transfer events
rows.plain: Avoid hiding a dangerous burst of released material by averaging results from several transfers. The largest release should still be visible so the risk can be judged accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Averaging that obscures peak release hides the most hazardous moments; relevant to measurement fidelity and severe because peaks drive acute exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Standard protocols report averaged concentrations that obscure hazardous peak releases, a persistent methodological flaw. Resolving peaks rather than averages requires more granular sampling that adds time. Recognizing when averaging masks peaks requires exposure-science judgement that is scarce. Peak-resolved measurement needs more samples and analysis, raising cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which protocol challenge conditions differ from actual sterile fill-finish transfer conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the test conditions closely match the real conditions used to move clean product into its containers. Otherwise, the test may not show what will happen in actual production.
rows.imp_rat: Protocol challenge conditions differing from real fill-finish transfer undermine qualification validity; relevant to representativeness and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Standard challenge conditions diverge from actual fill-finish operations, so results poorly predict real containment. Replicating fill-finish conditions in testing adds setup and validation time. Reproducing realistic sterile fill-finish transfer conditions demands specialized process knowledge. Building representative test rigs is costly and rarely fully realized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which challenge-substance release behavior differs from hazardous-drug release behavior
rows.plain: Make testing with a substitute substance behave as much as possible like testing with the dangerous medicine itself. This makes the test a more dependable guide to how the medicine may spread or escape.
rows.imp_rat: Challenge substance behaving unlike the real drug invalidates qualification; relevant to test representativeness and severe because passing may not reflect real containment.
rows.sat_rat: Common surrogates (e.g., titanium tetrachloride, isopropanol) behave differently from actual hazardous drugs, creating systematic representativeness error. Selecting and characterizing a representative surrogate adds upfront study time. Choosing a challenge substance that mimics drug release behavior requires deep physicochemical expertise. Better surrogate characterization studies add cost but are often skipped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which evaluation conditions diverge from worker-relevant sterile fill-finish handling conditions
rows.plain: Make testing conditions as similar as possible to the real conditions workers face when handling sterile products. This helps ensure the results reflect actual risks to workers.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation conditions diverging from worker-relevant handling weaken exposure estimates; relevant to test relevance and severe because it misrepresents real exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Test conditions commonly fail to represent real handling, weakening the validity of conclusions. Matching evaluation to worker-relevant handling conditions extends preparation time. Defining worker-relevant conditions requires task-analysis expertise that is scarce. Recreating realistic handling scenarios adds cost that is often trimmed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of released hazardous-drug vapor and aerosol mass excluded from quantification
rows.plain: Measure as much of the harmful drug vapor and spray released into the air as possible. Complete measurements give a clearer picture of the remaining risk to workers.
rows.imp_rat: Released mass excluded from quantification biases qualification low; relevant to measurement validity and severe because underestimation hides real hazard.
rows.sat_rat: Standard surrogate methods systematically exclude a portion of released mass, especially vapor phase, leaving a persistent quantification gap. Capturing the full released mass requires multi-phase sampling that lengthens each test cycle. Ensuring both vapor and aerosol fractions are quantified needs analytical expertise many teams lack. Full mass-balance quantification is resource-intensive and often reduced to partial sampling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incompletely engaged source-to-destination junction is accepted as sealed
rows.plain: Make sure the connection between where liquid starts and where it goes is fully joined before treating it as sealed. This helps prevent leaks or outside contamination.
rows.imp_rat: An incompletely engaged junction accepted as sealed guarantees a leak path at flow start; central to connection integrity and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Many connectors give audible/tactile click feedback but incomplete engagement can still be mistaken for sealed, leaving a moderate gap. An incomplete junction is accepted instantly unless verification is performed. Confirming full engagement relies on operator tactile/visual judgement more than positive feedback. No added cost for verification beyond time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of low-level hazardous vapor or aerosol releases unrecognized during preparation
rows.plain: Detect small releases of dangerous vapor or fine mist during preparation instead of overlooking them. Early detection helps people respond before the release becomes more serious.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecognized low-level releases during preparation allow chronic low-dose exposure; relevant to detection sensitivity and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Low-level releases routinely go unrecognized because no standard real-time detection exists at the bench, a chronic gap. Recognizing low-level releases in real time is not a matter of time but of perception. Detecting invisible vapor/aerosol demands expertise the operator does not realistically have without instrumentation. Continuous monitoring instruments are expensive and rarely deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual hazardous liquid released during and after fluid-path separation
rows.plain: Release as little leftover dangerous liquid as possible while separating and afterward disconnecting a fluid line. This reduces the risk of exposure and spills.
rows.imp_rat: Residual hazardous liquid released during/after separation is a common CSTD leak; central and severe due to concentrated residue exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Residual liquid at disconnection is much reduced but not eliminated, leaving small recurring droplet-release risk. Dry-disconnect separation adds no wait, keeping residual-liquid handling fast. Self-sealing membranes reduce the delicate technique previously needed at separation. Self-sealing connectors carry standard consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of hazardous-drug vapor or aerosol releases during sterile fill-finish activities
rows.plain: Reduce how often dangerous medicine fumes or fine sprays escape during sterile filling and finishing work. This helps protect workers from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Frequency of vapor/aerosol releases during fill-finish is a direct measure of the CFJ outcome; central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CSTDs and closed processing substantially and consistently reduce release frequency, making this a comparatively well-served need. Releases occur in seconds during transfers, faster than any current monitoring can flag. Reducing release frequency depends on consistent aseptic and containment technique that varies by operator. CSTDs and isolators represent a proven investment many facilities have made to cut releases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sampling locations miss hazardous-drug release plumes near transfer interfaces
rows.plain: Place samples where they can detect dangerous drug particles released near points where materials are transferred. This helps reveal contamination that could harm people or spread to other areas.
rows.imp_rat: Sampling locations missing release plumes near interfaces cause false-negative qualification; relevant to measurement placement and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Poorly placed sensors frequently miss localized plumes, and there is little standardized guidance to prevent this. Placing samplers correctly is a setup activity that does not greatly extend duration. Correctly locating sampling points at release plumes near interfaces depends heavily on hygienist expertise and experience. Adding more sampling locations increases sampling and analysis cost modestly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vial-closure puncture creates a hazardous drug escape path
rows.plain: Prevent a punctured medicine container from creating a route for dangerous medicine to leak or escape. This protects people nearby from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Vial-closure puncture creating an escape path is a core CSTD failure mode at the vial interface; highly relevant and severe as it releases vapor at the source.
rows.sat_rat: Coring or off-axis puncture is uncommon with proper adapters but still creates an escape path occasionally. Vial-access spikes engage quickly without slowing the job. Guided vial adapters reduce the skill needed to puncture the stopper cleanly. Vial adapters add per-vial consumable cost that is standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether workers are breathing hazardous-drug vapor during sterile fill-finish activities
rows.plain: Find out quickly whether workers are breathing vapor from hazardous medicines during sterile production. This allows action to be taken promptly if anyone may be exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty about whether workers are inhaling drug delays protective response; relevant to mitigation and moderately severe.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous drug escaping into the sterile fill area during transfer
rows.plain: Keep dangerous medicine from escaping into the clean filling area while it is being moved. This protects the work area and the people in it.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous drug escaping into the sterile fill area during transfer combines occupational exposure and product-contamination risk; central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Combined engineering controls and CSTD keep drug escape into the fill area low, though sterile-area integration is less mature than for hood compounding. Containment operates continuously during transfer without adding process time in the sterile fill area. Closed system reduces reliance on aseptic operator technique to prevent drug escape into the environment. Isolator/hood combined with CSTD represents a significant but standard capital and consumable outlay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous vapor or aerosol released into the work area during fluid-path separation
rows.plain: Keep dangerous fumes or fine droplets from spreading into the work area when parts carrying liquid are separated. This reduces the chance that people will breathe in or touch the medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Release into the work area during fluid-path separation is a core containment failure at a known high-risk step; central with serious contamination consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Membrane dry-seal separation is generally clean, but residual droplets and design differences still allow occasional release. Dry-disconnect designs let separation occur immediately without a wait for depressurization. Self-sealing connectors reduce the technique needed to avoid release at disconnection. Dry-disconnect connectors add cost but are standard in CSTD kits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a connection seal fails during diluent injection or drug withdrawal
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the seal at a connection breaks while liquid is added to or medicine is taken from a container. This helps prevent leaks and keeps the dangerous medicine contained.
rows.imp_rat: A connection seal failing during injection/withdrawal is a direct breach at the moment of drug flow; central and severe as it produces immediate release.
rows.sat_rat: Seal failure during injection/withdrawal is infrequent with proper engagement but can occur under overpressure or misconnection. Sealed injection/withdrawal proceeds at normal pace without added time. Locking connectors lower reliance on the worker maintaining seal by hand pressure. Sealed connectors are accepted routine cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of performing a drug transfer with a damaged sterile-boundary component
rows.plain: Check that every part forming the clean, sealed liquid path is in good condition before transferring medicine. This helps prevent loss of cleanliness during the transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Performing transfer with a damaged sterile-boundary component risks both breach and contamination; relevant and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Visual inspection catches gross damage but subtle defects still pass, so this is only moderately served. A damaged component is used in real time unless caught during setup inspection. Catching damaged sterile-boundary components depends on operator inspection skill. Discarding suspect components wastes consumable cost but is affordable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous drug contacting the operator during transfer-path intervention
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous medicine from touching the worker while parts of its transfer route are being worked on. This protects the worker from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Operator contact during transfer-path intervention is a direct exposure event during a manual fix; relevant and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Manual transfer-path intervention exposes the operator to contact risk that PPE only partially mitigates. Intervention on the transfer path is a manual task with no time-based protection. Avoiding contact during intervention leans on operator care and PPE technique. Only PPE materials are consumed, at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which hazardous-drug vapor and aerosol releases are underestimated during handling tasks
rows.plain: Estimate as accurately as possible how much dangerous medicine vapor and fine spray may escape during handling. This helps people take the right precautions.
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating release during handling tasks biases safety decisions low; relevant to assessment validity and severe because it understates real exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Underestimation is common because sampling data are sparse and models are conservative-in-name only, so confidence in release estimates is low. Assessing whether releases are underestimated is a periodic analytical exercise, not time-critical. Correctly estimating true release magnitude demands scarce exposure-modeling and IH expertise that most facilities lack. Comprehensive characterization sampling is costly and often truncated to save money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible worst-case transfer conditions omitted from acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Leave out fewer difficult but believable transfer situations from the rules used to judge whether the transfer is acceptable. This helps ensure the process is tested under the conditions most likely to cause trouble.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting worst-case conditions from acceptance criteria leaves real failure modes unqualified; relevant to test completeness and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria routinely omit credible worst-case conditions because no accepted standard enumerates them, leaving systematic blind spots. Enumerating worst-case conditions in acceptance criteria is front-loaded effort, not an ongoing delay. Deciding which worst-case transfer conditions belong in criteria requires scarce containment-engineering judgement. Covering more conditions expands test matrices and cost, which pressures teams to omit some.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that internal pressure forces hazardous drug past a connection seal during preparation
rows.plain: Lower the chance that pressure inside the equipment pushes a hazardous drug through a connection seal during preparation. This helps keep the drug contained and protects people nearby.
rows.imp_rat: Internal pressure forcing drug past a seal during preparation is a direct breach mechanism; central to containment and severe as it produces uncontrolled release.
rows.sat_rat: Engineered seals mostly withstand internal pressure, but overpressurization from over-injection of diluent can still breach them. Sealed connections hold under pressure without demanding extra operator time. Locking connectors reduce dependence on the worker judging safe pressure before injecting. Robust seal designs add component cost that is treated as normal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible hazardous vapor or aerosol release pathways omitted from qualification
rows.plain: Make sure testing considers every believable way that dangerous vapor or spray could escape. This helps a buyer choose systems that are properly checked for safety.
rows.imp_rat: Credible release pathways omitted from qualification leave unverified leak routes; relevant to test coverage and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification often misses less obvious release pathways because there is no exhaustive pathway checklist, so omissions recur. Mapping release pathways is a design-review task done once per protocol rather than a recurring time sink. Comprehensive pathway identification depends on rare expertise in device mechanics and hazardous-drug behavior. Testing every pathway adds fixtures and runs that budgets frequently curtail.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous liquid leaking through transfer interfaces during preparation
rows.plain: Limit the amount of dangerous liquid that leaks from connection points while equipment is being prepared. This helps protect workers from contact with it.
rows.imp_rat: Liquid leaking through transfer interfaces during preparation is a direct containment breach; highly relevant with serious contamination severity.
rows.sat_rat: Liquid leakage through interfaces is uncommon with proper CSTD connection, though loose or cross-threaded couplings still leak occasionally. Leak-resistant interfaces impose no meaningful time penalty in routine transfers. Luer-lock and dry-membrane interfaces lessen the manual care needed to prevent liquid leaks. Sealed interfaces are standard consumables with accepted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transfer starts before the connection is fully engaged
rows.plain: Make sure a transfer cannot begin until the connection is completely attached. This helps stop material from escaping during the transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Transfer starting before full engagement causes release at flow onset; central to prevention and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Positive-locking designs cut premature transfer, but partial engagement can still slip past inattentive operators. Confirming full engagement adds a brief moment. Click-lock connectors give tactile feedback that reduces reliance on operator judgement of engagement. Locking features add small cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of hazardous drug discharge after containment fails
rows.plain: If a failure lets a dangerous medicine escape, stop the release as quickly as possible. This limits how long people and the surrounding area are exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of discharge after containment fails sets total released mass; relevant to limiting breach harm and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Once containment fails, discharge duration hinges on prompt recognition which is inconsistent, leaving this need weakly served. Spill response time depends on detection, which can be slow. Stopping ongoing discharge quickly relies on trained emergency response. Spill kits are inexpensive and readily stocked.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible vapor and aerosol release pathways omitted from the qualification protocol
rows.plain: Make sure the testing plan includes every realistic way that harmful fumes or fine droplets could escape. This helps show whether the equipment can contain them safely.
rows.imp_rat: Release pathways omitted from the qualification protocol leave leak routes unverified; relevant to test coverage and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Protocols consistently leave out credible pathways because standards are incomplete, making this a recurring reliability gap. Adding pathways to a protocol is a planning activity, not a schedule bottleneck. Anticipating all vapor and aerosol pathways requires specialized failure-mode knowledge. Broader pathway coverage increases protocol scope and expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous vapor or aerosol dispersed beyond the preparation location before confinement
rows.plain: Keep as much harmful gas or mist as possible from spreading away from where preparation happens before it is contained. This helps protect people and nearby areas.
rows.imp_rat: Vapor/aerosol dispersed beyond the preparation location before confinement widens exposure; central to the job and severe because it affects broader personnel.
rows.sat_rat: BSC/isolator airflow reliably confines most emissions near the source, with residual dispersal risk during hand movements. Cabinet and CSTD confine emissions in real time without slowing preparation. Containment is largely passive, requiring less operator vigilance to prevent dispersal. The primary engineering controls are costly but standard infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible hazardous-drug escape pathways omitted from the assessment
rows.plain: Reduce the number of believable ways that a dangerous drug could escape that are left out of the risk review. This helps ensure important sources of harm are considered.
rows.imp_rat: Credible escape pathways omitted from the assessment leave hazards unaddressed; relevant to hazard coverage and severe.
rows.sat_rat: No standardized pathway checklist exists, so credible escape routes are routinely omitted from the assessment. Pathway enumeration is front-loaded analysis that can extend the assessment timeline. Identifying all credible escape pathways depends heavily on expert hazard-analysis skill that is scarce and error-prone. Omissions are cheap to make but catastrophic later, with weak cost signals during review.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which cumulative exposure from repeated tasks and multiple facility locations is underestimated
rows.plain: Avoid underestimating the total exposure a person receives from repeating tasks in different places. This helps give a more accurate picture of the risk.
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating cumulative multi-task, multi-site exposure biases long-term risk low; relevant to assessment validity and severe for chronic health outcomes.
rows.sat_rat: Cumulative exposure across sites and repeated tasks is almost never systematically tracked, so it is routinely underestimated. Cumulative-exposure aggregation is done infrequently rather than under time pressure. Summing exposure across tasks and locations requires epidemiological and IH modeling skill rarely available in-house. The data collection and analysis to capture cumulative dose is resource-intensive and usually skipped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual hazardous drug is expelled under pressure during transfer-path separation
rows.plain: Prevent leftover dangerous medicine from being forced out under pressure when workers separate the connected transfer parts. This helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Residual drug expelled under pressure at separation is a common CSTD leak point; central to the job and severe due to concentrated release near the operator.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-equalizing CSTDs substantially prevent pressurized expulsion, though residual events still occur with high-volume or gas-forming drugs. Pressure-equalization design manages residual pressure at the moment of separation without slowing the operator. Built-in equalization removes the operator's need to manually vent or judge pressure before separating. The pressure-management components are integral to the device cost and require no extra spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous-drug transfers begun with an incomplete or insecure connection
rows.plain: Begin transfers of dangerous medicines only when the connection is complete and secure. This helps protect workers from leaks or exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Transfers begun with an incomplete or insecure connection are a leading cause of leaks; central to prevention and severe because they cause release at flow start.
rows.sat_rat: Luer-lock and dry-connect designs reduce bad connections, yet unverified or partial engagements still slip through without independent checks. A quick tactile or audible click on connection gives near-immediate confirmation of engagement on modern devices. Proper connection still depends on operator technique and attention, so training gaps produce insecure connections. Locking connectors add modest cost but do not materially strain budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel skip containment-critical checks before initiating a hazardous-drug transfer
rows.plain: Make sure workers do not miss the safety checks needed to contain a dangerous medicine before moving it. These checks help prevent harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Skipping containment-critical checks before transfer is a procedural precursor to breach; relevant to prevention and severe because it enables release.
rows.sat_rat: Paper or memory-based checks are inconsistently applied, so skipped checks remain a common latent failure without enforced gating. Pre-transfer checks add little time but are easy to rush under production pressure. Completing containment-critical checks relies entirely on operator discipline and memory, which is inherently error-prone. Checklists are cheap, so cost is not the barrier to compliance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible hazardous-drug release pathways omitted from containment criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the safety rules cover every believable way that a dangerous medicine could escape. Missing an escape route could leave people or the area unprotected.
rows.imp_rat: Release pathways omitted from containment criteria leave leak routes uncontrolled; relevant to criteria completeness and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria derivation is unstandardized, so relevant release pathways are commonly left uncovered. Deriving containment criteria adds analysis effort but is not primarily time-bound. Translating release pathways into criteria requires specialized containment expertise that limits completeness. Missing pathways in criteria carry deferred rather than immediate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous vapor or aerosol released while fluid movement is restored
rows.plain: Keep the release of dangerous vapor or fine spray as small as possible when movement of the fluid is restarted. This helps protect nearby people from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Vapor/aerosol released while restoring fluid movement targets a specific release window; central to the CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Release while restoring flow is generally low with sealed systems, with minor residual risk at reconnection. Restoring flow is not slowed by containment features. Sealed path limits reliance on operator care during restoration. Standard consumable cost.

rows.source_job: Contain Spills at the Point of Failure
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator contact with released hazardous drug material during containment
rows.plain: Workers should be protected from touching or coming into contact with dangerous medicine that has spilled or escaped. This reduces the chance of harm during cleanup and containment.
rows.imp_rat: Operator contact with released material during containment is direct exposure; relevant to response safety and severe due to skin/eye dose.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PPE substantially reduces but does not eliminate contact during hands-on containment, giving moderate protection. Contact during containment happens in the moment of response with no protective delay. Avoiding contact depends on PPE discipline and careful technique rather than the device. PPE (double gloves, gowns) is a routine low-cost consumable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sealing-interface damage remains undetected before fluid movement
rows.plain: Any damage where two parts are joined should be found before liquid is moved through them. This helps prevent leaks or contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected sealing-interface damage before flow means a breach starts hidden; relevant to prevention and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Small sealing-surface damage frequently escapes visual detection before use, a recurring reliability gap. Undetected sealing damage persists until fluid movement reveals it, with no pre-check delay. Detecting sealing-interface damage requires trained visual inspection with no instrumented aid. Inspection adds no material cost but consumes attention.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from a hazardous vapor or aerosol release to recognition by floor personnel
rows.plain: People working nearby should recognize a release of dangerous vapor or fine spray as quickly as possible. Early warning gives them more time to move away and get help.
rows.imp_rat: Time from release to recognition by floor personnel governs how long exposure continues undetected; relevant to mitigation and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Without continuous monitoring, many small releases go unrecognized until routine surface sampling, so detection is inconsistent. Detection of a vapor or aerosol release today relies on smell, visible spill or later wipe sampling, so recognition can lag badly. Recognizing a subtle release demands vigilant, experienced staff since most hazardous vapors are invisible and odorless. Real-time vapor monitoring is rarely deployed because sensors are costly and not standard on floors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a hazardous-drug release cannot be distinguished from environmental variation
rows.plain: People need to be able to tell whether a dangerous medicine has escaped, rather than mistake it for a normal change in the surroundings. This makes it easier to find and respond to exposure risks.
rows.imp_rat: A release indistinguishable from environmental variation escapes detection; relevant to measurement discrimination and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Low-level releases are frequently indistinguishable from ambient contamination variability, undermining confidence in results. Distinguishing release from background variation requires replicate baseline runs that extend timelines. Separating signal from environmental noise demands statistical and analytical expertise rarely present. Extra baseline and control sampling adds cost per evaluation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of transfer-component failures while hazardous drugs are flowing
rows.plain: Reduce how often parts used to move hazardous medicines fail while those medicines are flowing. This helps protect the people operating the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Component failures while hazardous drugs are flowing produce immediate release; central to reliability of containment and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Validated single-use CSTD components fail rarely, though defects and over-pressurization still cause occasional in-flow failures. Component failures during flow are brief events but detection and stopping flow takes reaction time. Single-use validated components reduce reliance on operator judgment about part integrity. Single-use disposables cost more but prevent costlier contamination events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual hazardous drug spraying toward the operator during disconnection
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that leftover dangerous medicine sprays toward the worker when a connection is taken apart. This helps protect the worker from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Residual drug spraying toward the operator during disconnection is a direct exposure event at a high-risk step; central and severe due to potential skin/eye contact.
rows.sat_rat: Dry-disconnect membranes reliably prevent residual spray during normal disconnection, a mature and effective safeguard. Dry-disconnect action occurs at normal disconnection speed with no time penalty. Spray toward the operator is prevented mechanically rather than by careful operator maneuvering. Anti-spray dry-disconnect features are embedded in standard device cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the pressure differential exceeding the containment limit during fluid transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the pressure difference becomes too large during liquid transfer. Keeping it within the safe limit helps prevent loss of containment or other problems.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure differential exceeding the containment limit is the failure condition that produces release; central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed CSTDs hold pressure within containment limits reliably during typical transfers, though extreme volumes can challenge them. Pressure differential exceeding limits happens transiently during transfer without warning time. Pressure control is engineered so the operator needs little judgement here. Pressure-management design is integral to device cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a cracked connection component opens a containment path during transfer
rows.plain: Prevent a cracked connector from creating a leak or opening during the transfer. This helps keep the material safely contained.
rows.imp_rat: A cracked connection component opening a containment path is a direct device defect defeating containment; central with serious release severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cracked components are rare with quality devices but remain a hard-to-detect failure path when they occur. Component integrity requires no added handling time. Pre-assembled robust components reduce the operator's need to inspect for cracks. Higher-grade plastics raise unit cost modestly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that foreign material on mating surfaces prevents a complete seal
rows.plain: Keep dirt or other unwanted material off the surfaces that join together. Clean joining surfaces are needed to form a complete seal.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material preventing a complete seal is a direct precursor to leakage; central to connection integrity and severe as it causes release.
rows.sat_rat: Foreign material occasionally defeats seals; detection depends on vigilance so control is only moderate. Inspecting mating surfaces adds a short step. Detecting foreign material before sealing relies on operator attentiveness and aseptic technique. No added material cost beyond routine cleaning supplies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from containment breach onset to operator recognition
rows.plain: Notice a failure in a protective barrier as soon as it begins during operation. Early recognition gives workers more time to respond before contamination or harm occurs.
rows.imp_rat: Time from breach onset to operator recognition governs exposure duration; relevant to mitigation and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Without engineered detection, recognition timing is inconsistent and often late. Recognition of a breach depends on human notice, which is inherently delayed with no active alarm. Rapid recognition requires attentive, experienced operators watching for subtle signs. No detection instrumentation cost is typically incurred, leaving the gap unaddressed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of liquid hazardous drug leaking through transfer junctions during transfer
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous liquid medicine from leaking at the points where containers or tubes connect. This helps keep the worker from being exposed to the medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Liquid leaking through transfer junctions is a direct containment breach; central to the job and severe because liquid drug is high-concentration contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Validated dry-connect CSTD junctions rarely leak liquid when properly seated, making this a well-served need. Leak-free dry-disconnect junctions engage instantly without added transfer time. Locking luer and membrane-to-membrane interfaces reduce the finesse needed to achieve a liquid-tight seal. Sealed connectors are a standard consumable cost already accepted in the workflow.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about hazardous vapor or aerosol containment during a live transfer
rows.plain: Shorten the time people are unsure whether dangerous fumes or fine particles are safely contained while material is being moved. Fast, clear confirmation helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty about containment during a live transfer delays protective action; moderately relevant and severe since prolonged uncertainty extends potential exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that released hazardous liquid crosses the preparation boundary before confinement
rows.plain: Contain any released dangerous liquid before it crosses the boundary around the preparation area. This helps keep the spill from spreading.
rows.imp_rat: Released liquid crossing the preparation boundary before confinement spreads contamination; central to containment with serious severity.
rows.sat_rat: Enclosed workspaces reliably keep liquid within the boundary, though splashes near the sash edge occasionally escape. Physical containment prevents boundary crossing continuously with no time cost. The cabinet enclosure does the confinement so little operator skill is engaged for this specifically. Enclosure and spill-control materials are budgeted standard costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a procedural deviation remains undetected during a live hazardous-drug transfer
rows.plain: During a transfer of dangerous medicine, notice quickly if any required step is missed or changed. This helps prevent unsafe handling and contamination.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected procedural deviation during a live transfer can silently cause release; relevant to prevention and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Live deviations frequently go undetected because there is no in-process verification beyond the operator themselves. A deviation may persist for the whole transfer before anyone notices it in real time. Detecting a live deviation demands a second trained observer, which is rarely present during routine transfers. Continuous supervision or monitoring is costly, so it is seldom funded.

rows.source_job: Contain Spills at the Point of Failure
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous drug residue remaining in joints after visible spill removal
rows.plain: Remove as much dangerous medicine residue as possible from cracks and connections after a visible spill is cleaned up. Residue left behind could expose people later.
rows.imp_rat: Residue remaining in joints after visible spill removal is hidden persistent contamination; relevant to decontamination completeness and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Hidden joint residue routinely survives visible spill cleanup, a known and poorly closed gap. Residue in joints persists after visible cleanup until deliberate decontamination. Effective joint decontamination requires methodical technique that operators vary in applying. Decontamination agents are inexpensive but must be applied repeatedly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of operator presence in an affected area after a containment breach
rows.plain: Reduce how long workers remain in an affected area after containment is broken. This lowers their exposure to possible hazards.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of operator presence in an affected area after a breach governs dose accumulation; relevant to mitigation and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Because breaches are often unrecognized, exit timing is inconsistent and operators may linger unknowingly. After a breach, evacuation depends on staff noticing and reacting, so operator dwell time in the affected zone is often prolonged. Knowing when and how fast to evacuate depends on trained recognition of an invisible hazard. Spill kits and PPE are inexpensive and available, so cost is not limiting.

rows.source_job: Contain Spills at the Point of Failure
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contained hazardous drug material escapes while the failure area is stabilized
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that dangerous medicine escapes while a damaged area is being made safe. This helps protect workers and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Contained material escaping while the failure area is stabilized is a secondary breach during response; relevant to harm control and severe if it occurs, though it is a follow-on event.
rows.sat_rat: There is no engineered guarantee that contained material stays contained during stabilization, so outcomes vary with operator response. Stabilizing a failure area takes appreciable manual time during which escape can continue. Preventing further escape while stabilizing relies on trained spill-response judgement rather than device features. Secondary containment materials are inexpensive but manual.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an established junction separates under line movement or transfer forces
rows.plain: Make sure a connected joint does not come apart when the line is moved or materials are transferred through it. A separation could cause a leak or break the closed path.
rows.imp_rat: An established junction separating under line movement is a direct in-use breach; central and severe as it releases during flow.
rows.sat_rat: Luer-lock and mechanical latching connectors reliably resist separation under normal line forces, making this well served. Junction separation under load happens suddenly without warning time. Locking connectors reduce the technique needed to keep junctions secure. Locking features are part of standard connector cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from detecting an exposure excursion to restoring airborne hazardous-drug exposure below applicable limits
rows.plain: Shorten the time it takes to bring dangerous drug vapors or particles back below safe limits after an exposure problem is found. This reduces the time people may be exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Time to restore exposure below limits after an excursion determines total dose; relevant to mitigation and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Established spill-response SOPs exist yet actual restoration time is highly variable because it hinges on when the excursion is even noticed. Once an excursion is recognized, spill procedures and ventilation purge can restore levels, but detection-to-restoration cycles are slow and manual. Effective rapid response depends on trained spill teams whose readiness varies across shifts. Decontamination materials and downtime add cost but are broadly available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous residue remaining on external connection surfaces after disconnection
rows.plain: Leave as little dangerous drug residue as possible on the outside of connection points after they are separated. This helps prevent the residue from spreading to people or nearby surfaces.
rows.imp_rat: Residue on external connection surfaces after disconnection is a common surface-contamination source; relevant and moderately severe as a dermal-transfer path.
rows.sat_rat: Residue on external connection surfaces is commonly detected in wipe sampling, indicating incomplete control. Wiping external surfaces after disconnection adds a small step. Clean disconnection to leave little residue depends on operator care. Wipes and deactivation agents are inexpensive routine supplies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous residue transferred from external surfaces to the worker during preparation
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of harmful medicine residue that gets from outside surfaces onto a worker while they prepare materials. This helps limit the worker’s exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Residue transferred from external surfaces to the worker during preparation is a dermal exposure path; relevant and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Residue transfer to workers is still detectable in biomonitoring, showing this remains imperfectly controlled. PPE handling and deactivation add modest time. Avoiding transfer of surface residue to the worker relies on trained handling technique. PPE and deactivation wipes are standard recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of continued hazardous-drug transfer after procedural nonadherence is detected
rows.plain: Shorten the time that harmful medicine continues to be moved after someone notices that the required procedure was not followed. This helps limit further exposure and unsafe handling.
rows.imp_rat: Continued transfer after nonadherence is detected prolongs release once a problem is known; relevant to response discipline and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Continued transfer after detection is inconsistent because there is no automatic stop and reliance is on manual reaction. Once a deviation is noticed, stopping transfer is fast, but the detection-to-stop gap depends on the operator. Deciding to halt requires judgment about whether the deviation is containment-critical. Stopping wastes some in-process product but the cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that verification activities release hazardous-drug vapors or aerosols into the worker environment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that checking the controls releases harmful medicine fumes or fine droplets into the workplace. This helps protect workers while they confirm that safety measures work.
rows.imp_rat: Verification activities themselves releasing vapor/aerosol create exposure during testing; relevant and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Verification activities are a recognized weak point where closed-system integrity is often broken, making releases here frequent. Verification steps like priming or check-weighing happen quickly but can release vapor before any control reacts. Avoiding release during verification depends on careful technique that varies among operators. Closed verification tooling exists but adds cost and is inconsistently adopted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the maximum acceptable hazardous vapor and aerosol release under qualified conditions
rows.plain: Make the highest acceptable amount of dangerous gas or spray released during approved testing clear and specific. This helps people judge whether the equipment is safe enough.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in the maximum acceptable release undermines every pass/fail decision; relevant to qualification clarity and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: No universally accepted maximum acceptable release exists for CSTDs, so thresholds remain ambiguous and decisions inconsistent. Setting a numeric release threshold does not add meaningful time once a value is chosen. Defining a defensible maximum requires toxicological and analytical judgement that is not widely held. Ambiguous thresholds cause costly re-testing but defining them is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of hazardous drug transfer beyond the preparation location on contaminated gloves, garments, or work items
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous medicine from being carried outside the preparation area on dirty gloves, clothing, or equipment. This protects people and nearby areas from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated gloves/garments carrying drug beyond the prep area spreads exposure to others; relevant and moderately severe as secondary contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Surface contamination studies repeatedly find drug on gloves and surfaces, so this need remains only partially controlled. Glove changes and wipe-downs add modest time to the workflow. Preventing surface transfer relies on disciplined donning/doffing and hand-hygiene technique. Frequent glove changes and gowns are recurring consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of worker-exposure points omitted from the facility risk inventory
rows.plain: List every place where workers could be exposed to danger in the facility. This helps direct protective measures to all the areas that need them.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting worker-exposure points from the risk inventory leaves hazards unmanaged; relevant to hazard identification and severe because unlisted risks are unmitigated.
rows.sat_rat: Risk inventories routinely miss transient or informal exposure points, so omissions are a persistent reliability gap. Building the exposure-point inventory is an upfront and periodic task, not time-sensitive. Identifying every exposure point requires experienced walkthroughs and process knowledge that is unevenly distributed. Thorough inventory work costs assessor time but is not capital-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of containment test runs exceeding the predefined hazardous-drug release limit
rows.plain: Reduce how often tests show that too much hazardous medicine has escaped its enclosure. This helps protect people and confirms that the testing method gives trustworthy results.
rows.imp_rat: Rate of test runs exceeding the release limit measures qualification failure; relevant to verifying the job and severe since exceedances reflect real release capability.
rows.sat_rat: Established surrogate test protocols give a repeatable exceedance rate metric, though inter-lab variability still leaves recurring gaps. Containment test runs are batchable and results return within a defined study window. Interpreting run-level exceedances against a limit is a comparatively bounded task once a protocol like USP >/ surrogate methods is adopted. Repeated failing runs waste study material and lab time, a real but contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure while checking a suspected containment breach
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a worker is exposed while checking a suspected leak or failure in the protective enclosure. The check should protect the person as well as the process.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure while checking a suspected breach is direct dose during investigation; relevant to response safety and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure during breach investigation is only partially mitigated by PPE, leaving real residual risk. Checking a suspected breach is a manual action with no protective time delay. Safe checking depends on operator caution and PPE use rather than any device aid. Only PPE is consumed during a breach check.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of vial-pressure spikes during fluid transfer
rows.plain: Prevent the pressure inside a small medicine container from suddenly rising during liquid transfer. This helps avoid spills, leaks, or damage to the container.
rows.imp_rat: Vial-pressure spikes during transfer drive aerosolization and leaks; central to the mechanism and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure spikes are largely damped by equalizing devices but can still occur with rapid diluent injection. Continuous equalization avoids stop-start delays from pressure spikes. Automatic balancing reduces reliance on incremental manual venting skill. The equalization mechanism is standard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous-drug residue remaining on external transfer surfaces after transfer activities
