rows.plain: Keep as little hazardous medicine as possible on the outside surfaces used to move it after the transfer is finished. This helps protect workers from contact with the medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Residue on external surfaces after transfer activities is a primary chronic surface-exposure source; relevant and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: CSTDs reduce external residue substantially, but residual surface contamination is still commonly detected in wipe sampling. Wipe-down after transfer takes little added time within the workflow. Effective decontamination depends on operator thoroughness and technique. Deactivation and cleaning agents are inexpensive commodities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unprotected personnel entering active hazardous-drug transfer areas
rows.plain: Keep people who are not wearing the required protection out of areas where dangerous medicines are being moved. This helps protect them from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Unprotected personnel entering active transfer areas exposes people without PPE; relevant to access control and severe if release is present.
rows.sat_rat: Access control relies on procedure and signage rather than interlocks, so unprotected entry into active transfer areas still happens. Restricting entry is procedural and does not add meaningful delay when signage is in place. Preventing unprotected entry depends on staff awareness and adherence rather than any physical barrier. Barriers, signage and PPE are low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification requirements with ambiguous hazardous-release thresholds
rows.plain: Use qualification requirements that clearly state what level of a dangerous release is unacceptable. This makes it easier to tell whether a system is safe and suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous release thresholds in qualification requirements weaken decisions; relevant to test clarity and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Many requirements still carry vague release thresholds because industry lacks a common quantitative standard. Clarifying thresholds is a one-time documentation act, not a delay. Writing unambiguous thresholds demands regulatory and analytical fluency that is scarce. Ambiguity drives rework costs while clarification itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of released hazardous drug entering seams, penetrations, or inaccessible surfaces before confinement
rows.plain: Keep released dangerous medicine from getting into cracks, openings, or hard-to-reach surfaces before it is contained. This makes cleanup safer and helps prevent hidden contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Released drug entering seams and inaccessible surfaces creates persistent reservoirs; relevant to lasting contamination and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Drug that reaches seams and inaccessible surfaces is hard to remove and frequently persists, a poorly controlled pathway. Cleaning seams and penetrations adds meaningful time to decontamination. Reaching contamination in crevices demands diligent, skilled cleaning effort. Deactivation supplies are cheap but labor is significant.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of dispersion permitted among repeated containment results used for acceptance
rows.plain: Make sure repeated containment tests give results that are close to one another. This makes the results more dependable when deciding whether the system passes.
rows.imp_rat: Permitting excessive dispersion among acceptance results lets inconsistent devices pass; relevant to acceptance rigor and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Protocols rarely specify permissible dispersion among accepted results, leaving acceptance inconsistent. Deciding allowable dispersion is a protocol decision, not a time drain. Setting defensible dispersion limits requires statistical judgement that is uncommon. Tighter dispersion allowances demand more replicates and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual vial overpressure at disconnection
rows.plain: Reduce the extra pressure left inside a vial when it is disconnected. This helps prevent a sudden release of air or liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Residual vial overpressure at disconnection is the direct driver of aerosol release at separation; central to the containment mechanism and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Balanced-pressure CSTDs leave little overpressure at disconnection, though residual pressure varies by drug and device. Pressure equalization occurs during use so disconnection needs no waiting. The device manages residual pressure, reducing operator judgement at disconnect. Pressure-equalizing designs add accepted component cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual pressure differential when the transfer connection is separated
rows.plain: Reduce the pressure difference before disconnecting the transfer connection. This helps prevent a sudden release or movement of product and air.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pressure differential at separation drives aerosol expulsion; central to the containment mechanism and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-equalizing CSTDs consistently reduce residual differential at separation, a well-served design goal. Residual pressure is neutralized at separation without added time. Automatic equalization removes the need for operators to manually assess residual pressure. Equalization is built into the purchased consumable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfer connections made with contaminated mating interfaces
rows.plain: Connections between fluid lines should not be made when the joining surfaces are dirty or contaminated. This helps keep the fluid clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Connections made with contaminated mating interfaces risk both leak and product contamination; relevant and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Capped or protected mating surfaces help, but interface contamination before connection remains an intermittent, hard-to-verify problem. Contaminated mating interfaces are used in the moment with no time buffer to catch them. Avoiding contaminated interfaces relies on operator inspection and clean handling. Protective caps and clean handling add minor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of dispersion in vapor and aerosol containment results across repeated qualified runs
rows.plain: Keep the results for containing airborne droplets and particles nearly the same across repeated approved tests. This makes it easier to trust that the system contains these materials reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Dispersion in containment results across runs signals inconsistent performance; relevant to reliability characterization and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Containment results commonly scatter across runs and there is no standard bound on acceptable dispersion, so consistency is weak. Running repeated qualified runs to observe dispersion consumes test time but is standard practice. Interpreting run-to-run variability needs statistical competence not always present. Additional replicate runs cost consumables and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that gas-flow obstruction prevents pressure equalization during transfer
rows.plain: Nothing should block the gas from flowing when pressure needs to become equal on both sides during a transfer. This helps the transfer happen safely and smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Gas-flow obstruction preventing pressure equalization causes pressure buildup and release; relevant to the mechanism and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Obstruction of the equalization path is uncommon but can occur with liquid wetting or filter clogging, defeating pressure balance. Gas-flow paths work passively without adding time when clear. The air path is device-managed so little operator skill applies. Filter/vent components are standard low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of measurement uncertainty in candidate vapor and aerosol release results
rows.plain: Tests of possible gas or mist releases should give results that are as certain and accurate as possible. This makes it easier to compare the available choices fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Measurement uncertainty in release results affects confidence in pass/fail; relevant to test validity and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Measurement uncertainty in vapor/aerosol quantification is often poorly characterized, undermining confidence in candidate results. Uncertainty quantification adds analysis time but fits within normal test cycles. Estimating measurement uncertainty requires metrology skill that many test labs underapply. Calibrated, low-uncertainty instrumentation is expensive and sometimes skipped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from controlled pressure limits when fluid movement resumes
rows.plain: Keep the pressure close to its safe target when fluid starts moving again. This helps prevent sudden pressure changes that could damage equipment or disrupt the process.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from controlled pressure limits when flow resumes can cause transient release; relevant to the mechanism and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled pressure limits are mostly maintained on resumption but transient deviations can occur, giving moderate reliability. Resumption of flow with balanced pressure adds no time. Device-managed pressure lowers reliance on operator monitoring at restart. Standard component cost applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual hazardous drug deposited on accessible connection surfaces after separation
rows.plain: Leave as little dangerous medicine residue as possible on connection surfaces that people can touch after the connection is separated. This helps protect workers from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Residual drug on accessible connection surfaces after separation is a well-documented dermal exposure path; relevant and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Dry-disconnect membranes leave little residue on accessible surfaces, though trace deposits are still detectable. Residual deposit on surfaces is created at separation and remains until cleaned. Dry-disconnect reduces reliance on technique but some residue management still needs care. Managed within existing consumable and cleaning cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that drug liquid enters the pressure-balancing path during transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the drug liquid gets into the passage used to balance pressure during transfer. Keeping the liquid out of that passage helps the transfer work as intended and avoids contamination or blockage.
rows.imp_rat: Drug liquid entering the pressure-balancing path can contaminate the air-handling route and defeat the equalization mechanism; relevant and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed devices keep drug out of the air path, but liquid intrusion into the balancing path can occur with inversion or overfill. Pressure-balancing operates automatically with no time cost. The internal air path is device-managed, so little operator skill applies here. The balancing chamber adds design cost within normal ranges.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a fluid-path component cracks or deforms during separation
rows.plain: Avoid cracking or bending a part that carries the fluid while it is being taken apart. This helps prevent leaks, damage, and contamination.
rows.imp_rat: A fluid-path component cracking or deforming during separation opens a release path; relevant and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Component cracking during separation is uncommon with quality devices but can happen with excessive force. Separation is quick and not time-constrained. Robust connectors reduce the force-control skill needed to avoid cracking. Durable materials add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of worker injury from a drug-contaminated component during preparation
rows.plain: Prevent workers from being hurt by a component that has drug on it while they prepare it. This protects people from harmful contact.
rows.imp_rat: Worker injury from a drug-contaminated component is a direct harm event; relevant to safety and severe due to injury plus drug exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Needle-free CSTDs greatly cut contaminated-sharps injury, though handling of any contaminated component still poses residual risk. Careful component handling adds negligible time. Blunt/needle-free CSTD designs reduce the skill needed to avoid sharps injury. Needle-free components add cost but reduce sharps-injury liability.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous drug contamination carried onto operator clothing during fill-line work
rows.plain: Keep dangerous medicine from getting onto the worker’s clothing during filling work. This helps protect the worker from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination carried onto operator clothing spreads exposure; relevant to worker safety and moderately severe as an ongoing dermal source.
rows.sat_rat: Contained transfers plus gowning keep clothing contamination low but surface residue transfer still happens intermittently. Clothing contamination accrues over the work session with no time-based control. Avoiding clothing contamination depends on gowning discipline and careful handling. Gowns and sleeves are routine inexpensive consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that confinement actions generate additional hazardous drug splashes or aerosol
rows.plain: Contain a spill or release without causing more splashes or airborne droplets of the hazardous medicine. This helps protect people and nearby surfaces.
rows.imp_rat: Confinement actions generating additional splashes or aerosol turns cleanup into new exposure; relevant to response safety and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Even with procedures, confinement actions still sometimes generate additional aerosol depending on handler skill. Careful confinement actions add some time but not excessively. Avoiding secondary splashes during spill response depends heavily on trained operator technique. Spill kits and absorbent materials are low-cost standard stock.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of connection, transfer, or disconnection steps performed outside the approved sequence
rows.plain: Make sure workers carry out connection, movement, and disconnection steps in the approved order. This reduces mistakes that could affect safety or cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Steps performed outside the approved sequence increase breach probability; relevant to procedural control and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Out-of-sequence steps still occur because most CSTD workflows do not physically enforce step order. Correct sequencing adds no time when followed as trained. Sequence adherence depends heavily on operator training and habit rather than forced ordering. No material cost is associated with following the correct sequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of accessible transfer surfaces contaminated with hazardous drug during fill-line work
rows.plain: Fewer reachable work surfaces should become contaminated with dangerous medicine during filling-line work. This helps protect workers from touching or spreading the medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Number of contaminated accessible transfer surfaces measures surface contamination burden; relevant and moderately severe as a chronic exposure source.
rows.sat_rat: CSTDs reduce but do not eliminate surface contamination, and studies still detect measurable residue on work surfaces. Surface contamination builds up across the shift and is addressed only at cleaning intervals. Reducing contaminated surfaces relies on disciplined wipe-down technique and awareness. Cleaning agents and wipes are low-cost but consumed continuously.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a fluid-path connection separates during handling
rows.plain: Prevent a connection carrying liquid from coming apart while it is being handled. This helps keep the product contained and prevents spills or contamination.
rows.imp_rat: A fluid-path connection separating during handling can release residual drug; relevant to reliability and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Locked fluid-path connections rarely separate during normal handling. Locking connections need no re-securing time during handling. Luer-lock and snap connectors reduce reliance on operator grip. Locking connectors are standard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that loss of sterility remains undetected under proposed use conditions
rows.plain: Make sure any loss of germ protection is noticed under the conditions in which the method will be used. This helps prevent unsafe or spoiled products from going undetected.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected loss of sterility is a product-safety detection concern tangential to the vapor/aerosol containment CFJ; moderate relevance, severe for patients.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility tests sample only limited conditions, so failures outside tested envelopes routinely go undetected. Loss of sterility often reveals itself only after release, offering no timely warning. Anticipating undetected sterility loss under proposed conditions demands scarce risk-analysis judgement. Broadening detection coverage adds nontrivial study cost that is often deferred.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of sterility test units showing microbial ingress after the full transfer sequence
rows.plain: Reduce the rate at which test samples show that germs entered them after the entire transfer process. This helps show whether the transfer method keeps samples protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial ingress after transfer is a sterility/product-safety concern that is peripheral to the vapor/aerosol containment CFJ; moderate relevance but severe for patient safety.
rows.sat_rat: Compendial sterility and media-fill methods reliably surface microbial ingress rates, making this one of the better-served needs. Media-fill and sterility test units follow long incubation cycles that delay confirmation. Sterility testing is a mature discipline with well-trained microbiology support widely available. Sterility test batches consume media and controlled-environment time but are routine budget items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of containment components damaged during line-side handling and connection
rows.plain: Reduce the number of parts that keep materials safely contained that are damaged while being moved and connected beside the production line. This helps prevent leaks, contamination, and work stoppages.
rows.imp_rat: Containment components damaged during line-side handling can leak later; relevant to reliability but framed as waste, with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Component damage during handling is an intermittent operator-dependent problem not addressed by device design. Handling damage occurs during setup and is not a time-limited event. Avoiding handling damage depends heavily on careful operator technique with delicate components. Damaged components must be discarded, wasting consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fluid-path component damage during transfer restoration
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of damaging the parts that carry the liquid when restarting a transfer that was interrupted. Damage could cause leaks or other problems.
rows.imp_rat: Fluid-path component damage during restoration can open a leak during a manual step; relevant and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Damage during transfer restoration is infrequent but possible with repeated reconnection cycles. Restoring transfer imposes no notable delay. Design robustness limits reliance on delicate handling at restoration. Standard component cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination on exposed surfaces enters the sterile fluid path during transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that germs or other contamination on exposed surfaces get into the path carrying the sterile liquid. This helps keep the liquid sterile during transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the sterile fluid path is a product-sterility concern tangential to the vapor/aerosol containment job; moderate relevance, severe for patients.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD dry-connection design plus media-fill challenge give reasonable but incomplete assurance against surface ingress. Detecting path contamination requires full sterility incubation, adding delay. Assessing surface-to-fluid-path transfer demands aseptic-process expertise that is available but specialized. Adds testing cost for swabbing and challenge studies, moderate in scale.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of vial vacuum during drug withdrawal
rows.plain: Keep the pressure inside the container from becoming too low while the medicine is being removed. This helps the medicine come out smoothly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Vial vacuum during withdrawal drives pressure imbalance and inward/outward flow issues; relevant to the mechanism and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Equalizing devices keep vial vacuum low during withdrawal, though some vacuum still develops depending on device type. Pressure-balanced withdrawal proceeds at normal speed. Automatic equalization reduces the technique needed to avoid deep vacuum. Equalization design is standard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial ingress occurs through transfer interfaces during connection or disconnection
rows.plain: Prevent germs from entering through connection points when equipment is joined or separated. This helps keep the product free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial ingress through transfer interfaces is a sterility concern peripheral to the vapor/aerosol containment CFJ; moderate relevance, severe for patients.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD dry-disconnect interfaces are specifically designed for this and have documented microbial-ingress challenge data, giving solid though not complete assurance. Connect/disconnect challenge testing fits standard validation timelines. Requires knowledgeable interpretation of interface microbial-challenge data, moderately specialized. Interface challenge studies add defined, manageable testing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous drug residue deposited on external interface surfaces while establishing the path
rows.plain: Keep dangerous medicine residue off the outside connection surfaces while setting up a sealed route for the liquid. This helps protect people and the surrounding area from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Residue deposited on external interfaces while establishing the path seeds surface contamination; relevant and moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Dry-connect designs limit external residue but trace deposition during path establishment still occurs. Residue deposited while establishing the path stays until cleaning cycles occur. Minimizing deposit relies on careful connection technique. Managed within routine cleaning consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of connection failures that interrupt preparation
rows.plain: Prevent connection problems from stopping the preparation work. This helps preparation continue without interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Connection failures that interrupt preparation are primarily throughput/reliability nuisances unless they release; moderate relevance and moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Interrupting connection failures are relatively uncommon with mature CSTDs but still occur enough to be a recurring nuisance. Connection failures that interrupt work cost rework time when they occur. Reliable connectors reduce troubleshooting skill demands. Failed connections waste components and drug.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of drug carryover contaminating another container or batch during transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that medicine left from one batch contaminates another container or batch during transfer. This helps keep each batch separate and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Drug carryover contaminating another container/batch is mainly a product cross-contamination issue, tangential to the vapor/aerosol worker-protection CFJ; moderate relevance, severe for patients.
rows.sat_rat: Single-use closed components make carryover between containers uncommon in standard practice. Single-use dedicated components avoid time lost to cross-contamination checks. Dedicated per-drug setups reduce reliance on operator tracking to prevent carryover. Single-use components raise consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile-path contamination from inward airflow during pressure equalization
rows.plain: Prevent unclean air from entering the clean product area while air pressures are being balanced. This helps keep the product free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-path contamination from inward airflow is a product-sterility issue somewhat tangential to worker vapor exposure; moderate relevance, severe for patients.
rows.sat_rat: Balloon or filtered air-management CSTDs largely prevent nonsterile inward airflow, a well-addressed design feature. Inward airflow during equalization occurs in real time with no delay buffer. Sterile-path protection during equalization is designed-in, requiring little operator skill. Air-filtering/balloon designs are integral to device cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of in-scope hazardous drugs whose volatility potency or aerosol-forming behavior is omitted from acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the safety requirements consider how easily each hazardous medicine can spread through the air, how strong it is, and whether it can form airborne clouds. This helps ensure that medicines needing more protection are not overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting a drug's volatility/aerosol behavior from acceptance criteria could let a high-risk drug pass improperly (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria typically ignore drug-specific properties, so criteria fit poorly across the in-scope drug range. Accounting for each drug's volatility/potency in criteria adds analysis time. Characterizing volatility, potency, and aerosol-forming behavior demands toxicology and physical-chemistry expertise. Drug-specific characterization studies are costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time for vial pressure to rebalance after fluid displacement
rows.plain: Help the pressure inside a small container return to normal quickly after liquid is moved. This helps prevent delays or problems with the container and its contents.
rows.imp_rat: Time for vial pressure to rebalance is a throughput/mechanism metric with limited direct exposure consequence; moderate relevance and lower severity.
rows.sat_rat: Rebalancing is generally prompt and consistent, though slower with viscous drugs or certain device geometries. Balanced-pressure devices rebalance pressure quickly so waiting is minimal. Automatic equalization removes the need for skilled pause-and-vent timing. Equalization mechanism cost is standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of obstruction recurrence after fluid movement is restored
rows.plain: Reduce how often a blockage happens again after the flow of liquid or gas has been restarted. This helps the transfer continue without another stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Obstruction recurrence after restoration is mainly an operational reliability issue with modest exposure impact; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Obstruction recurrence after restoration is an occasional persistent problem with certain drug/device combinations. Recurring obstruction repeatedly interrupts and costs time. Preventing recurrence depends on operator technique adjustments. Repeat clearing may consume extra components.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of drug-flow blockage during reconstitution or dose preparation
rows.plain: Prevent the medicine mixture from getting stuck or failing to flow during preparation or dose making. This helps the work continue smoothly and prevents delays.
rows.imp_rat: Drug-flow blockage during reconstitution is mostly an operational disruption with modest release risk; moderate relevance and lower severity.
rows.sat_rat: Drug-flow blockage during reconstitution happens intermittently, especially with viscous or poorly dissolving drugs. Flow blockages stall preparation and cost time to clear. Clearing blockage relies on operator troubleshooting skill. Blockages can waste a component or dose.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from containment-breach onset to recognition by the preparer
rows.plain: Make sure the person preparing the work notices a loss of containment as soon as it starts. Quick awareness allows the problem to be addressed before it worsens.
rows.imp_rat: Slow recognition of a containment breach prolongs exposure; moderately relevant to containment and moderate consequence, kept under 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Breach-to-recognition intervals are unreliable and often indefinite, leaving this need largely unmet. Time-to-recognition is inherently long without detection aids. Prompt recognition of a breach demands sensing capability the human operator does not possess. Real-time breach sensors are expensive and rarely present.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of credible leak and connection-failure exposure pathways omitted from evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the evaluation includes every believable way that a leak or failed connection could expose people or products. Missing a possible path can leave an important risk unaddressed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting credible leak/connection-failure pathways from evaluation leaves exposure routes unassessed (relevant) with moderate severity, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Credible leak and connection-failure pathways are frequently omitted, so real-world failure exposure is under-evaluated. Adding leak and connection-failure scenarios extends test duration. Designing failure-mode challenges requires reliability-engineering expertise. Failure-mode testing adds cost and is often deprioritized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the permissible hazardous-drug release threshold exceeds applicable worker-protection or product-contamination limits
rows.plain: Make sure the allowed release of a dangerous medicine is not higher than the level that protects workers and products. This helps prevent harmful exposure and contamination.
rows.imp_rat: A permissible release threshold set above worker/product limits directly defeats the CFJ's protective intent (relevant) with high potential severity, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Permissible thresholds sometimes exceed applicable protection limits, so passing does not guarantee safety. Aligning the release threshold to protection limits is a one-time definitional step. Deriving a threshold from worker-protection and product limits requires toxicology and quality expertise. Setting the threshold is low cost, though meeting it may not be.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of relevant hazardous-drug vapor and aerosol pathways excluded from acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the pass-or-fail requirements cover every important way dangerous medicine fumes or fine sprays could escape. This helps ensure the equipment is judged on all relevant safety risks.
rows.imp_rat: Excluding relevant pathways from acceptance criteria means containment failures pass unnoticed, central to the job, with meaningful exposure consequences, but capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria frequently exclude relevant vapor and aerosol pathways, so passing candidates remain unproven on omitted routes. Expanding acceptance criteria to cover all pathways is a definitional step with little time cost. Deciding which pathways belong in acceptance criteria requires expert judgement. Broader criteria may require more testing, raising cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of worker-relevant handling and failure conditions absent from evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the evaluation includes all handling situations and equipment failures that could affect workers. This helps avoid overlooking ways people could be harmed.
rows.imp_rat: Absent worker-relevant failure conditions from evaluation leaves exposure gaps (relevant) with moderate severity, under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many worker-relevant handling and failure conditions are absent from evaluation, biasing results optimistic. Adding more handling and failure conditions to the test matrix lengthens evaluation. Identifying the full set of worker-relevant conditions requires task-analysis skill. A larger condition matrix costs more to execute.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which hazardous-drug vapor and aerosol release risk is understated in the capital case
rows.plain: Make sure the cost decision does not downplay the risk that dangerous medicine fumes or fine sprays could escape. This helps people choose equipment with a realistic understanding of the safety risk.
rows.imp_rat: Understating release risk in the capital case is an administrative/business-case error far from the physical containment job; low relevance though consequential for investment safety.
rows.sat_rat: The status quo routinely understates release risk because exposure data is sparse and hard to quantify, so capital cases lean on optimistic assumptions that go unchallenged. Building a defensible risk narrative takes weeks of cross-functional input, but the delay is not the primary failure here. Accurately characterizing hazardous-drug vapor and aerosol release risk demands scarce toxicology and industrial-hygiene expertise that most capital sponsors lack. Understated risk directly leads to under-scoped, under-funded requests, but the money lost is a downstream symptom.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that a hazardous drug release remains unrecognized while preparation continues
rows.plain: Shorten the time that a release of dangerous medicine goes unnoticed while preparation continues. This helps workers stop the work and avoid breathing or touching the released material.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecognized ongoing release during continued preparation extends worker exposure duration, relevant to the CFJ with real severity, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Releases can persist unrecognized for the whole session, so this need is chronically underserved. The unrecognized-release duration is long precisely because detection lags. Shortening recognition time depends on perceptual ability the operator lacks without tools. Monitoring equipment to shorten recognition is costly and uncommon.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in pass-fail rules for results near the hazardous-drug release threshold
rows.plain: Make the pass-or-fail rules clear when test results are close to the limit for releasing dangerous medicine. This helps people reach the same safety decision instead of guessing.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in pass-fail rules near the release threshold could pass a failing system (relevant) with moderate severity, held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pass-fail decisions near the threshold are frequently ambiguous, leading to inconsistent dispositions. Clarifying near-threshold pass-fail rules is a definitional step with little time cost. Setting defensible near-threshold decision rules requires statistical and metrology judgement. Clearer rules have negligible material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible hazardous-drug vapor and aerosol release pathways omitted from the protocol
rows.plain: Make sure the test plan includes every believable way that dangerous medicine fumes or fine sprays could escape. This helps the testing reflect real safety risks.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting credible release pathways from the protocol directly undermines the CFJ of preventing vapor/aerosol release (high relevance), and unassessed pathways can lead to real worker exposure (notable severity), but the ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Protocols routinely omit credible release pathways, leaving blind spots in evaluation. Enumerating all credible release pathways is an upfront analysis that adds modest time. Comprehensive pathway identification demands experienced hazard-analysis judgement. Thorough pathway analysis costs analyst time but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exposure routes from escaping hazardous-drug vapors or aerosols left unidentified
rows.plain: Identify every way workers could come into contact with escaping fumes or fine sprays from dangerous medicine. This helps reveal how people might be exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified exposure routes from escaping vapors/aerosols leave workers unprotected (relevant) with moderate severity, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure routes are frequently left unidentified because pathway analysis is incomplete. Mapping exposure routes is an upfront analytic task with limited time impact. Identifying all exposure routes from escaping vapors/aerosols requires hygiene expertise. Route mapping is low material cost but consumes expert time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that escaped hazardous drug is mistaken for benign moisture or residue during preparation
rows.plain: Prevent workers from mistaking escaped dangerous medicine for harmless water or leftover material during preparation. This helps them respond to a leak instead of continuing to work around it.
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking escaped drug for benign moisture delays recognition of a release (moderately relevant) and could prolong exposure (moderate severity), but capped.
rows.sat_rat: Escaped drug is frequently mistaken for benign moisture because most drugs are colorless and undetectable, a poorly served need. Misjudging residue as benign happens instantly, not a time issue. Distinguishing hazardous drug from harmless moisture requires knowledge and cues the operator often lacks. Indicator technologies exist but are seldom purchased.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which assessment conditions exclude credible worst-case transfer stresses
rows.plain: Make sure the testing includes believable worst-case stresses that could occur when items are transferred. This helps reveal contamination risks that might be missed under easier conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Excluding worst-case transfer stresses from assessment could hide failure modes (relevant) with moderate exposure consequence, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Assessments routinely test nominal rather than worst-case conditions, leaving a chronic gap. Worst-case scenario design lengthens study planning. Identifying credible worst-case stresses is expert-dependent and frequently underdone. Worst-case testing raises study cost, encouraging its omission.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which documented detection capability is insufficient to determine criterion exceedance
rows.plain: Reduce the gap between how well the written detection method can find a problem and what is needed to tell whether a limit has been exceeded. This provides stronger proof that the test can identify unacceptable conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient detection capability to determine criterion exceedance limits ability to verify containment (relevant), and undetected exceedance risks exposure (moderate severity), held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented detection capability is frequently insufficient to confirm whether a criterion was exceeded, weakening conclusions. Documenting detection capability is a one-time characterization effort. Establishing method detection capability requires analytical expertise often lacking in reviews. Method validation studies to prove detection capability are costly and sometimes omitted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the transfer path remains partially connected after attempted separation
rows.plain: The transfer line should be fully disconnected when separation is attempted. A partly connected line could create a safety or process problem.
rows.imp_rat: A transfer path partially connected after separation is a potential release/leak point (relevant) with moderate severity, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Positive-latch disconnect designs mostly ensure clean separation, though partial-separation edge cases remain. A partial-separation state persists until noticed, delaying clean disconnection. Detecting incomplete separation relies on operator observation. No added cost involved.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the pressure-equalization path becoming obstructed during fluid transfer
rows.plain: Keep the route that lets pressure balance open while liquid is being moved. This prevents pressure from building up or disrupting the transfer.
rows.imp_rat: An obstructed pressure-equalization path defeats a core containment mechanism, driving release (relevant) with moderate severity, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Air-path filters can occasionally clog or wet-out, an uncommon but real failure that is not actively monitored. Obstruction of the equalization path can develop during transfer without warning. Little operator skill addresses internal path obstruction, which is not directly observable. No dedicated cost to prevent equalization-path clogging.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that staff without current qualification are assigned to hazardous-drug transfers
rows.plain: Make sure only staff with up-to-date training and approval handle transfers of dangerous medicines. This matters for protecting workers, the product, and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Unqualified staff assigned to hazardous-drug transfers raises exposure risk (moderately relevant) with real severity, held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Training matrices and LMS systems reasonably prevent unqualified assignments, though lapses occur under staffing pressure. Checking qualification before assignment adds minimal time when records are accessible. Assigning qualified staff depends on supervisor knowledge of who is current. Maintaining qualification tracking costs modest administrative effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous-drug transfers conducted by personnel without current procedural authorization
rows.plain: Make sure only people with current permission and training carry out transfers of hazardous medicines. This helps prevent unsafe handling.
rows.imp_rat: Transfers by unauthorized personnel raise exposure and error risk (moderately relevant) with real severity, held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Authorization checks are fairly reliable where competency systems exist, though unauthorized transfers slip through during coverage gaps. Verifying authorization before transfer is quick with a maintained roster. Reliance is on supervisors correctly reading current authorization status. Authorization tracking is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous-drug transfers involving deviations from approved handling requirements
rows.plain: Make sure transfers of hazardous medicines follow the approved safety instructions. This protects workers and reduces the chance of exposure or spills.
rows.imp_rat: Transfers deviating from approved handling requirements directly risk exposure and contamination (relevant), with meaningful consequence, capped by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Deviations from approved handling remain a recurring finding since enforcement is largely procedural and self-policed. Following handling requirements does not slow the transfer when procedures are clear. Adherence relies on staff knowing and applying detailed handling requirements consistently. Compliance carries little direct cost beyond training.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of under-allocation of exposure-control resources at the highest-risk hazardous-drug release points
rows.plain: Enough protective resources should be assigned to the points where dangerous medicines are most likely to escape. This helps focus protection where workers and the surroundings face the greatest risk.
rows.imp_rat: Under-allocation of controls at highest-risk points could leave key release points exposed (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Under-allocation at top-risk points recurs because risk ranking and budgeting are loosely coupled. Reallocating resources to hot spots is a planning cadence issue, not time-critical. Knowing where the highest-risk points are requires risk-ranking expertise that is inconsistently applied. The core failure is misallocation of finite exposure-control resources, leaving high-risk points under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that allocated exposure-control resources are unavailable during scheduled high-risk hazardous-drug activities
rows.plain: Make sure the protective equipment and other safeguards assigned for scheduled work with dangerous medicines are available when needed. This helps protect people during higher-risk activities.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure-control resources unavailable during high-risk activities directly leaves workers exposed (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Allocated controls being unavailable at go-time (broken isolator, missing PPE, absent qualified staff) is a recurring operational failure. Resource unavailability surfaces at the moment of the scheduled activity, sometimes forcing delays. Ensuring qualified staff and equipment are present depends on scheduling competence that varies. Maintaining spare capacity and backup equipment costs money that is often trimmed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that favorable secondary results mask failure of the hazardous-release criterion
rows.plain: Do not let good results in other areas hide a failure in the requirement for safely releasing dangerous material. That safety failure must remain decisive in the evaluation.
rows.imp_rat: Favorable secondary results masking hazardous-release failure could hide a genuine containment breach (relevant to the job), with moderate exposure consequences, but ranked below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregated or favorable secondary data can hide primary-criterion failure, and few protocols explicitly prevent this. Checking that primary criteria aren't masked adds little time to review. Recognizing when secondary metrics obscure a failure demands experienced critical review. No material cost attaches to guarding against masking beyond reviewer effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that high-consequence containment and sterility failures receive insufficient evaluation priority
rows.plain: Give the highest attention to failures that could seriously affect containment or keeping the product free from germs. This helps ensure the most dangerous contamination risks are investigated first.
rows.imp_rat: High-consequence failures receiving insufficient evaluation priority could leave the worst risks unaddressed (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Without disciplined risk prioritization, high-consequence failures sometimes receive too little evaluation. Prioritization analysis adds planning time. Ranking high-consequence failures requires structured risk-management expertise. Misprioritization wastes evaluation effort on low-value tests.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of relevant worst-case transfer conditions omitted from documented validation testing
rows.plain: Fewer important worst-case transfer situations should be left out of the written testing. This helps show that the method works even under the most difficult relevant conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting worst-case transfer conditions from validation testing leaves failure modes unproven (relevant) with moderate severity, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented validation frequently omits relevant worst-case conditions, weakening the evidence base. Documenting worst-case coverage adds validation reporting time. Deciding which worst cases to document requires seasoned validation judgement. Omitted worst-case runs save cost now but risk expensive rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of relevant vapor and aerosol release pathways omitted from candidate evidence
rows.imp_rat: Omitting release pathways from candidate evidence weakens comparative selection (relevant) with moderate downstream exposure risk, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor evidence typically covers only a subset of pathways, leaving unquantified routes. Gathering evidence across all pathways lengthens the evidence-collection phase. Recognizing missing pathways in candidate evidence needs review expertise. Requiring more complete evidence increases testing and documentation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of critical transfer and connection events excluded from candidate evidence
rows.imp_rat: Excluding critical transfer/connection events from candidate evidence weakens containment assessment, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Critical transfer and connection events are frequently excluded from candidate evidence, leaving gaps. Requiring evidence for all critical transfer/connection events extends evidence gathering. Judging which events are critical requires process expertise. More event coverage increases testing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of worker task phases with potential exposure omitted from pathway evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting task phases with potential exposure from evaluation leaves gaps (relevant) with moderate severity, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Some task phases with real exposure potential are routinely omitted from pathway evaluation. Covering all worker task phases enlarges the evaluation scope and time. Task-phase decomposition for exposure requires human-factors and hygiene skill. Evaluating more task phases increases labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of internal pressure oscillation during fluid transfer
rows.imp_rat: Internal pressure oscillation during transfer can drive vapor/aerosol release (relevant to CFJ mechanism) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD pressure-equalization moderates oscillation adequately for typical transfers, though large or rapid transfers can still oscillate. Pressure oscillation occurs during transfer with no operator time control. Damping is engineered into the device, requiring minimal skill. Part of the device's integral design cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incompatible transfer-component pairings accepted before fluid movement
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible component pairings accepted before fluid movement can cause leaks (relevant) with moderate severity, held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Proprietary keyed CSTD connectors within one system physically prevent most incompatible pairings, making this fairly reliable. Incompatible pairings are caught at connection, sometimes only after wasted attempts. Recognizing incompatibility relies on operator familiarity with the component set. Mismatched components may be wasted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in determining whether each transfer connection is secure
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in determining whether a connection is secure relates to leak prevention (relevant) with moderate severity, held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Click/lock feedback reduces ambiguity but does not fully eliminate uncertainty about secure connection. Ambiguous connection status forces extra checking time. Resolving ambiguity relies on operator tactile/visual judgement. No added cost beyond time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that expired or suspended qualifications remain active for hazardous-drug transfer assignments
rows.imp_rat: Expired/suspended qualifications remaining active for assignments allows unqualified handling (moderately relevant) with real severity, held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: LMS auto-flags reduce lingering expired qualifications, though manual systems let them persist inconsistently. Expired qualifications can remain active for a while before systems flag or purge them. Catching lapses depends on administrators monitoring expiry dates. Automated expiry tracking is inexpensive where LMS exists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of suspected operator exposure events not reported to accountable oversight
rows.imp_rat: Suspected exposure events unreported to oversight is a reporting/governance gap, modestly relevant though missed events carry real worker-health severity.
rows.sat_rat: Suspected exposures are underreported because events are often invisible and reporting is voluntary and stigmatized. Delayed exposure reporting extends time to medical follow-up. Staff must recognize a suspected exposure to report it, which requires awareness of subtle events. Reporting has negligible direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of worker exposure pathways incorrectly characterized across sterile fill-finish tasks
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly characterized worker exposure pathways in fill-finish tasks misdirects protection, moderately relevant with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Misclassified pathways are common because assumptions substitute for measurement, undermining assessment validity. Characterizing pathways is an analytical activity done outside time pressure. Correctly mapping exposure pathways requires specialized IH judgement that is a limiting factor. The effort is labor cost rather than material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containment activities not evaluated against applicable airborne exposure limits
rows.imp_rat: Containment activities not evaluated against airborne exposure limits leaves protection unverified (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Many containment activities are never formally evaluated against airborne limits, leaving unverified coverage. Evaluating activities against limits is periodic, not urgent. Comparing containment performance to airborne limits needs IH interpretation skill in short supply. The evaluation is analytical labor rather than capital spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required containment conditions lacking valid verification evidence
rows.imp_rat: Required containment conditions lacking valid verification evidence leaves assurance unproven (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: A substantial share of required containment conditions lack current valid verification evidence, a common audit finding. Gathering verification evidence is scheduled work, not time-pressured. Producing valid verification evidence requires validation expertise that is unevenly available. Verification testing (wipe sampling, smoke studies) carries recurring cost that limits coverage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible hazardous vapor and aerosol release modes omitted from the common comparison framework
rows.imp_rat: Omitting credible release modes from the comparison framework biases candidate selection (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Common comparison frameworks often omit release modes, so cross-candidate comparison is incomplete. Building a comparison framework is a one-time construction effort. Defining all release modes for comparison requires expert framing. Framework development is mostly labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required vapor and aerosol containment evidence missing from candidate evaluations
rows.imp_rat: Missing containment evidence from candidate evaluations weakens selection assurance (relevant) with moderate severity, under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor data packages frequently omit required containment evidence, leaving candidate evaluations partial. Chasing missing evidence lengthens the evaluation. Identifying which evidence is missing needs a knowledgeable reviewer. Filling evidence gaps means paying for more studies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of candidate containment claims lacking traceable supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Containment claims lacking traceable evidence weaken assurance for the recommended system, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Candidate containment claims often lack traceable supporting evidence, undermining trust. Tracing claims to evidence adds review time. Verifying traceability requires diligent, trained reviewers. Traceability review is labor cost, not major capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in criteria distinguishing actual containment failure from expected test dispersion
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity distinguishing real failure from expected dispersion affects pass/fail integrity, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria distinguishing true containment failure from expected variability are typically vague, harming decisions. Defining failure-versus-dispersion criteria is a planning step, not a delay. Drawing the line between real failure and test noise requires statistical and domain judgement. Ambiguity forces costly reruns while clarification is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification evidence pertains to a system configuration other than the evaluated configuration
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence pertaining to a different configuration than evaluated invalidates assurance, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence frequently pertains to a configuration other than the one evaluated, so applicability is uncertain. Confirming configuration provenance takes minimal time. Tracking which configuration was tested requires careful specialist attention. Retesting the evaluated configuration adds expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required qualification conditions lacking documented vapor and aerosol containment evidence
rows.imp_rat: Required conditions lacking documented containment evidence leave containment claims unproven (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Some required conditions routinely lack documented containment evidence, so coverage is incomplete. Collecting condition-specific evidence extends evaluation duration. Knowing which conditions need documented evidence takes specialist insight. Testing every required condition is resource-intensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification evidence covers a configuration different from the recommended configuration
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence covering a different configuration than recommended invalidates assurance (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification evidence often reflects a different configuration than the one recommended, a common hidden mismatch. Checking configuration match is a fast review step. Detecting configuration mismatches needs detailed device knowledge. Reconfiguring and retesting to match is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility-compromising ingress pathways overlooked during evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Overlooked sterility-ingress pathways relate to the sterility side rather than the core vapor/aerosol containment job; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Without a systematic ingress taxonomy, evaluations routinely miss credible sterility-compromising paths. Overlooked ingress paths surface late, not as a scheduling issue. Comprehensive ingress-pathway identification depends on aseptic-design expertise that is unevenly distributed. Overlooked pathways impose downstream contamination cost rather than upfront spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that drug-contact material and transfer-interface incompatibilities remain unidentified
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified material/interface incompatibilities can cause leaks or failures, moderately relevant with moderate severity, held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard extractables/leachables and compatibility protocols catch many but not all incompatibilities. Material compatibility studies run over extended periods, adding time. Identifying drug-contact and interface incompatibilities needs materials-science expertise that is specialized but obtainable. Extractables/leachables and compatibility testing are costly, limiting breadth.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containment criteria that cannot be evaluated under representative transfer conditions
rows.imp_rat: Criteria that cannot be evaluated under representative conditions produce unusable assurance, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful share of containment criteria cannot be evaluated representatively, leaving them unverified. Determining evaluability under representative conditions is an upfront planning cost. Judging which criteria are testable representatively needs method-development skill. Non-evaluable criteria waste effort defining unusable requirements.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which evaluation conditions fail to represent proposed approved use
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation conditions failing to represent approved use undermines validity of containment assurance (relevant) with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Evaluation conditions often diverge from real approved use, a recurring representativeness failure. Aligning evaluation to approved use extends scoping effort. Mapping test conditions to intended approved use requires regulatory-process expertise. Poor representativeness risks costly repeat studies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate approaches evaluated with noncomparable containment endpoints
rows.imp_rat: Noncomparable containment endpoints across candidates biases comparison, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Candidates are often evaluated with noncomparable endpoints, making head-to-head comparison unreliable. Harmonizing endpoints across candidates adds coordination time. Defining comparable endpoints requires measurement-science judgement. Standardizing endpoints may require re-testing, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch in challenge conditions across candidate transfer approaches
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched challenge conditions across candidates undermines fair comparison, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Challenge conditions frequently differ across candidates, confounding comparisons. Matching challenge conditions across candidates adds setup coordination time. Ensuring identical conditions across approaches needs disciplined experimental control. Consistent conditions may require dedicated fixtures, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in candidate release results across repeated evaluations
rows.imp_rat: Variation in candidate release results across repeats is a measurement-reliability issue, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Repeatability of CSTD release results is poor, with high run-to-run variability widely reported. Reducing result variation requires replicates that lengthen evaluation. Achieving repeatable results needs skilled, consistent technique. More replicates raise cost per candidate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a containment breach is attributed to the wrong transfer-path location
rows.imp_rat: Attributing a breach to the wrong path location misdirects remediation, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Misattributing breach location is common because there is no engineered localization, a poorly served need. Locating a breach takes investigative time that delays response. Correctly attributing a breach to the right location demands diagnostic experience with no aiding instrumentation. No cost tooling supports localization.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of environmental contamination entering the sterile path during transfer-path separation
rows.imp_rat: Environmental contamination entering during path separation is a sterility concern peripheral to hazardous-release containment, moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Self-sealing membranes reliably block environmental ingress during separation, making this well served. Separation-time ingress is momentary and not time-controllable by the operator. Dry-disconnect closes the path automatically, reducing reliance on technique. Built into the standard consumable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate ingress into the sterile path while joining interfaces
rows.imp_rat: Microbial/particulate ingress while joining interfaces is a sterility issue peripheral to the hazardous-release job, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed membrane interfaces plus swabbing keep microbial/particulate ingress low during joining, a mature aseptic practice. Ingress at joining happens in the connection instant. Aseptic joining still depends on disinfection technique despite closed interfaces. Disinfectant wipes are low-cost routine consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microorganisms entering the sterile drug path during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Microorganisms entering the sterile drug path is a sterility failure with high patient severity but low relevance to the vapor/aerosol containment CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sterile paths substantially prevent microbial entry, backed by microbial-ingress testing standards. Microbial entry during transfer is not a time-bounded event for the operator. Closed-path design reduces the aseptic skill burden compared with open transfer. No extra cost beyond the closed system already in use.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing nonsterile material into the fluid path during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Introducing nonsterile material into the fluid path is a sterility risk tangential to hazardous-release containment, high severity if realized.
rows.sat_rat: Closed transfer plus swabbing reliably keeps nonsterile material out of the fluid path in normal practice. Introduction of nonsterile material occurs at connection instants, not over time. Preventing it still relies partly on disinfection discipline. Only disinfectant consumable cost is involved.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of reverse flow entering the sterile path from a nonsterile segment
rows.imp_rat: Reverse flow from a nonsterile segment is a sterility risk tangential to the vapor/aerosol CFJ, though serious if it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Many CSTDs incorporate features that limit reverse flow, though not all designs fully prevent it, leaving some variability. Reverse flow can happen instantly if pressure reverses, with no reaction time. Check-valve behavior is engineered, needing little operator skill. Valving is part of device cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial contamination entering the sterile path during flow restoration
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination during flow restoration is a sterility concern peripheral to vapor/aerosol containment, high severity but low relevance here.
rows.sat_rat: Because the path stays closed, microbial ingress during flow restoration is well controlled by design. Contamination during flow restoration is momentary at the restart. Closed path limits the skill needed to keep restoration sterile. No added cost for restoration handling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the obstruction source is misidentified before flow-restoration intervention
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the obstruction source before intervention is an operational-diagnostic issue, modestly relevant with low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Obstruction sources are frequently misidentified due to lack of visibility, giving inconsistent outcomes. Misidentifying the obstruction source wastes intervention time. Correct source identification depends on operator inference without internal visibility. No diagnostic cost is incurred.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual obstruction after a flow-restoration attempt
rows.imp_rat: Residual obstruction after flow-restoration is an operational-recovery issue only loosely tied to release prevention, low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: There is no engineered confirmation that an obstruction is fully cleared, so residual blockage is a variable outcome. Residual obstruction after a clearing attempt prolongs the interruption. Judging whether obstruction is cleared requires operator inference without direct visibility. Failed clearing may waste the consumable if replaced.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of obstruction recurrence after fluid movement resumes
rows.imp_rat: Obstruction recurrence after flow resumes is an operational reliability concern, modestly relevant with low-moderate severity for the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Obstruction recurrence is not systematically prevented, making it an inconsistent, drug-dependent problem. Recurring obstruction repeatedly interrupts flow, extending total transfer time. Preventing recurrence relies on operator adjustment rather than design. Recurrence may force component replacement, wasting cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that a disconnected fluid-path end remains open after separation
rows.imp_rat: A disconnected fluid-path end left open is both a sterility and potential release exposure point, moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Disconnected ends reseal reliably in closed systems, sharply limiting open-end duration. Self-sealing ends close immediately, minimizing open time. Auto-sealing membranes reduce operator dependence on prompt capping. Self-sealing connectors are standard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial ingress into the sterile fluid path during connection or disconnection
rows.imp_rat: Microbial ingress during connection/disconnection is a sterility failure, only tangentially relevant to the hazardous-release CFJ though patient-severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Closed connectors reduce microbial ingress at connection well, but disinfection compliance still governs residual risk. Swab-and-connect adds a brief aseptic step. Needle-free closed connectors reduce dependence on aseptic touch technique. Disinfecting caps/wipes are low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of using a transfer component with a breached sterile barrier during preparation
rows.imp_rat: Using a breached sterile barrier is a sterility failure peripheral to vapor/aerosol containment, high severity but low relevance to this scope.
rows.sat_rat: Packaging-integrity checks catch most breached barriers, but subtle breaches occasionally pass unnoticed. Checking barrier integrity adds a short inspection step. Recognizing a breached sterile barrier relies on operator inspection. Discarding a compromised unit wastes some cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of ambient air entering the sterile fluid path during pressure changes
rows.imp_rat: Ambient air entering the sterile fluid path is a sterility concern, tangential to the vapor/aerosol containment CFJ, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed pressure-balanced systems limit air ingress fairly reliably, though pressure swings can still draw in air. Sealed transfers proceed normally without added time. Closed connectors reduce reliance on aseptic finesse to keep air out. Closed components are standard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial ingress into the sterile fluid path during transfer restoration
rows.imp_rat: Microbial ingress during transfer restoration is a sterility concern only tangential to the hazardous-release CFJ, with high consequence if it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Ingress during restoration is fairly well controlled, with residual risk at each reconnection event. Restoration adds negligible time. Closed reconnection reduces aseptic technique demands. Standard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial ingress into the sterile fluid path during dose withdrawal or transfer
rows.imp_rat: Microbial ingress during withdrawal/transfer is a sterility issue peripheral to vapor/aerosol containment, with high patient severity but low relevance here.
rows.sat_rat: Microbial ingress during transfer is well controlled by the closed sealed path in typical practice. Withdrawal/transfer under closed conditions adds no time. Closed path lessens the aseptic skill needed during active transfer. Standard consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation across shifts in applying approved hazardous-drug transfer procedures
rows.imp_rat: Cross-shift variation in applying approved procedures is a consistency/training issue, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: SOPs promote consistency, but real cross-shift variation persists because adherence depends on who is working. Shift variation does not itself change transfer duration. Consistency across shifts depends on uniform training and supervision, which varies with staff experience. Standardization carries little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous-drug transfer deviations closed without verified corrective action
rows.imp_rat: Deviations closed without verified corrective action allow recurrence of exposure risk, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Deviations are sometimes closed without confirmed effectiveness because verification steps are inconsistently enforced. Verifying corrective action before closure adds time to the deviation cycle. Judging whether corrective action truly works requires quality expertise. CAPA verification consumes quality staff resource.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering incompatible transfer interfaces at the point of use
rows.imp_rat: Discovering incompatible interfaces at point of use is an operational/logistics failure, moderately relevant with modest severity for containment.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized single-vendor CSTD kits reduce mismatches, but incompatibilities still surface at the point of use across product lines. Discovering an incompatible interface at point of use causes an immediate but short delay. Avoiding incompatibility depends on staff verifying component matching, a knowledge-dependent step. Mismatched or wasted components add some material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous-drug transfers affected by conflicting hazard classifications or handling requirements
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting hazard classifications for transfers create handling ambiguity, moderately relevant to correct protection with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Hazard classifications from NIOSH lists give reasonable consistency, though conflicting or ambiguous classifications still affect some transfers. Resolving conflicting hazard classifications delays the affected transfers. Interpreting conflicting handling requirements requires specialist knowledge of hazard classification. Reclassification work consumes some administrative resource.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of hazardous-drug handling scenarios omitted from the facility risk assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting handling scenarios from the facility risk assessment is more administrative and indirect to the device's containment job, with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Non-routine and edge-case handling scenarios are frequently omitted from formal risk assessments, leaving coverage gaps. Scenario cataloguing is periodic and not driven by urgency. Enumerating all handling scenarios depends on deep process familiarity that experienced assessors provide inconsistently. Comprehensive scenario mapping consumes assessor hours but little capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a change to drugs, tasks, rooms, staffing, or production volume and reassessment of affected exposure scenarios
rows.imp_rat: Delay reassessing exposure scenarios after changes leaves stale controls, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Change-driven reassessments are often missed or delayed, so controls lag actual conditions. Reassessment after changes typically lags weeks or months behind the change itself, a chronic delay. Triggering timely reassessment relies on change-control discipline and assessor availability that is limited. Rapid reassessment consumes assessor time but not major capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in risk ratings assigned to equivalent hazardous-drug exposure scenarios
rows.imp_rat: Variation in risk ratings for equivalent scenarios is a consistency issue in risk assessment, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Equivalent scenarios frequently receive different risk ratings because scoring is subjective and poorly standardized. Risk rating is a periodic exercise, not time-critical. Consistent ratings require calibrated assessor judgement that differs between individuals. Rating consistency is a training cost, not a material one.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the occupational exposure limit applicable to each hazardous drug and handling task
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in applicable occupational exposure limits affects criteria correctness, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguity is high because many hazardous drugs have no established occupational limit, forcing uncertain surrogate values. Establishing the applicable OEL per drug is a reference task, not time-driven. Assigning correct exposure limits requires toxicological expertise, and many HDs lack published OELs. Purchasing or deriving OELs costs modestly but is manageable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a change in exposure conditions and adjustment of exposure-control resource priorities
rows.imp_rat: Delay adjusting control resource priorities after exposure changes leaves controls misaligned, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Because reassessment triggers are weak, priority adjustments happen late and inconsistently. Re-prioritizing resources after a condition change typically lags, leaving stale priorities in place. Rapid re-prioritization needs experienced risk judgement that is not always on call. Reprioritization is administrative cost rather than capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in exposure-control coverage for equivalent hazardous-drug tasks across shifts and batches
rows.imp_rat: Variation in exposure-control coverage across shifts/batches is an operational-consistency issue moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure-control coverage for identical tasks varies meaningfully across shifts and batches, a well-known and unaddressed inconsistency. Shift-to-shift variation is not a timing issue but a consistency one. Uniform coverage depends on consistent operator training and supervision that varies notably by shift and batch. Standardizing coverage across all shifts requires staffing investment often not funded equally.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between verification conditions and actual sterile fill-finish production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between verification and actual fill-finish conditions weakens validity, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Verification often occurs under idealized rather than actual fill-finish conditions, so evidence overstates real containment. Aligning verification to real conditions is a design choice, not a timing problem. Designing representative verification demands validation and process expertise that is scarce. Testing under true production conditions costs more, so simplified surrogate tests are common.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that containment measures remain in use after a material change without current verification evidence
rows.imp_rat: Containment measures used after a material change without current verification leaves unproven controls in place, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Continued reliance on unverified controls after changes is a recurring, poorly-controlled exposure risk. Measures often stay in use for extended periods after a material change without refreshed verification. Recognizing when re-verification is due depends on change-control discipline that varies. Re-verification carries testing cost that incentivizes delay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of background contamination included in candidate hazardous-drug release measurements
rows.imp_rat: Background contamination in candidate measurements biases evaluation accuracy, moderately relevant with low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Background contamination routinely inflates candidate release measurements, and correction is inconsistently applied. Controlling and subtracting background adds baseline sampling time. Proper background correction requires analytical expertise. Additional background samples raise analytical cost.
