rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of consequential sterile fill-finish conditions omitted from acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting consequential fill-finish conditions from acceptance criteria pertains to a narrower use context, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Important sterile fill-finish conditions are often omitted from acceptance criteria, weakening relevance. Incorporating consequential fill-finish conditions into criteria adds definitional time. Identifying consequential conditions requires process expertise. Broader condition coverage increases test cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of containment-result variation attributable to uncontrolled challenge conditions
rows.imp_rat: Containment-result variation from uncontrolled challenge conditions undermines measurement validity, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Uncontrolled challenge conditions introduce substantial unexplained variation into containment results. Controlling challenge conditions requires disciplined setup that adds time. Holding conditions constant demands experimental-control skill. Tighter control needs better equipment and monitoring, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of measured vapor or aerosol signal attributable to background sources
rows.imp_rat: Measured signal from background sources is a measurement-cleanliness issue, moderately relevant to accurate release quantification, low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful fraction of measured signal often derives from background, and it is not reliably partitioned out. Quantifying background signal contribution adds analytical steps. Attributing signal to background sources needs analytical-chemistry skill. Extra blanks and controls increase cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that planned observations cannot distinguish meaningful containment differences among candidates
rows.imp_rat: Observations that cannot distinguish meaningful containment differences among candidates weakens selection validity, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Studies are frequently underpowered, so real containment differences among candidates go undetected. Powering a study to distinguish candidates requires more runs and time. Determining sufficient observations to detect meaningful differences requires statistical expertise. Adequately powered comparisons cost more.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the replicate plan fails to characterize containment-result variability
rows.imp_rat: A replicate plan failing to characterize variability weakens statistical confidence, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Replicate plans commonly under-characterize variability, so reported uncertainty is understated. Designing an adequate replicate plan is a planning step with modest time cost. Building a replicate plan that captures variability requires statistical design expertise that is scarce. Adequate replication increases testing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attributing a hazardous-drug release to the wrong transfer step or interface
rows.imp_rat: Attributing a release to the wrong step/interface misdirects remediation, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Releases are commonly misattributed across transfer steps because sampling lacks step resolution. Attributing release to the correct step requires event-resolved sampling that adds time. Correct attribution demands experienced analytical judgement. Step-resolved measurement increases sampling cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which distinct vapor and aerosol exposure routes are treated as equivalent
rows.imp_rat: Treating distinct vapor and aerosol routes as equivalent could obscure a real exposure route, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Vapor and aerosol routes are frequently lumped together, obscuring route-specific containment failures. Treating routes distinctly requires route-specific sampling that adds time. Distinguishing vapor from aerosol routes requires specialized measurement skill. Route-specific methods increase cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of mistaking background hazardous-drug contamination for transfer-generated release
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking background contamination for transfer-generated release is a measurement-attribution error, moderately relevant to accurate evaluation with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-existing surface contamination is commonly mistaken for transfer-generated release, a recognized confounder. Distinguishing background from transfer release requires pre-test baseline runs that add time. Separating background contamination from generated release needs analytical judgement. Baseline characterization adds sampling cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of indirect exposure routes from deposited material or secondary aerosolization left unidentified
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified indirect exposure routes (deposited material, secondary aerosolization) are a secondary but real gap, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Indirect exposure routes are commonly overlooked entirely, a persistent blind spot. Assessing indirect routes adds analysis time but not test time. Recognizing secondary aerosolization and deposited-material routes demands specialized and uncommon expertise. Analyzing indirect routes is mostly an expert-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous-drug residue from one evaluation run distorts a subsequent release measurement
rows.imp_rat: Residue carryover distorting a subsequent release measurement is a test-integrity issue, moderately relevant with modest severity for the containment job.
rows.sat_rat: Residue carryover between runs distorts subsequent measurements when decontamination is imperfect, a frequent issue. Decontamination and verification between runs adds turnaround time. Effective carryover control requires disciplined technique and validation skill. Cleaning and verification between runs consumes consumables and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of release measurements lacking traceability to the candidate approach handling condition and evaluation run
rows.imp_rat: Release measurements lacking traceability weakens data integrity, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Measurements often lack full traceability to condition and run, undermining defensible conclusions. Maintaining traceability adds documentation overhead per run. Rigorous chain-of-custody and traceability require data-management discipline. Traceability systems add administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in qualified test conditions across candidate systems
rows.imp_rat: Variation in qualified test conditions across candidate systems undermines comparability, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Test conditions vary widely across candidate systems, making comparisons unreliable. Normalizing conditions across candidates is planning effort. Harmonizing test conditions requires methodological expertise. Standardizing conditions may require repeat testing at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between qualified test conditions and intended sterile fill-finish use
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between qualified test conditions and intended fill-finish use weakens applicability, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified test conditions frequently mismatch intended sterile fill-finish use, reducing applicability. Aligning test to sterile fill-finish use is a design decision, not a delay. Matching conditions to fill-finish reality needs aseptic-process expertise. Representative fill-finish test setups are expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acceptance criteria tied to conditions unqualified for intended sterile fill-finish transfers
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria tied to unqualified conditions weakens applicability to intended transfers, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria are often tied to conditions not qualified for the intended fill-finish transfers. Checking criteria-to-use linkage is a quick review. Judging fill-finish relevance requires specialized knowledge. Requalifying to relevant conditions costs money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acceptance criteria not objectively assessable from candidate evidence
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria not objectively assessable from evidence weakens evaluation rigor, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria are frequently not objectively assessable from available evidence, weakening conclusions. Assessing objectivity of criteria is quick during review. Framing objectively assessable criteria requires evaluation expertise. Little direct cost attaches beyond reviewer time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of intended system configurations and use scenarios excluded from evaluation scope
rows.imp_rat: System configurations/scenarios excluded from evaluation scope leaves gaps in coverage, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Intended configurations and use scenarios are commonly excluded from scope, leaving unqualified uses. Defining evaluation scope is upfront planning, not delay. Enumerating all configurations and use scenarios needs broad process knowledge. Wider scope means more testing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which uncontrolled test variables obscure the source of detected release
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled test variables obscuring the release source undermines measurement validity, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Uncontrolled variables frequently obscure release sources, making findings ambiguous. Controlling variables adds setup time. Isolating release sources under controlled variables requires expert experimental design. Controlled environments and fixtures are costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an inconclusive containment finding from insufficient valid replicates
rows.imp_rat: An inconclusive finding from insufficient replicates leaves containment status unresolved, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inconclusive findings from too few valid replicates are a recurring problem without standard replicate requirements. Ensuring enough valid replicates lengthens the campaign. Designing adequate replication requires statistical competence. More replicates cost consumables and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in protocol execution across operators or test sites
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent protocol execution across operators/sites is a consistency issue, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Execution inconsistency across operators and sites is common and degrades comparability. Harmonizing execution across sites is upfront effort. Consistent protocol execution depends heavily on trained, disciplined operators across sites. Cross-site standardization and training cost money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the protocol fails to distinguish vapor release from aerosol release
rows.imp_rat: Failing to distinguish vapor from aerosol release is an evaluative ambiguity with modest direct bearing on preventing release and low immediate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Many protocols conflate vapor and aerosol release, so the distinction is unreliably captured. Separating vapor from aerosol measurement can extend test setup time modestly. Distinguishing vapor from aerosol requires specialized sampling and analytical skill. Dual measurement approaches add instrumentation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of observed containment failures not reproduced under equivalent qualified conditions
rows.imp_rat: Failures not reproduced under equivalent conditions is a reproducibility concern, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Observed failures are frequently not reproduced under equivalent conditions, signaling weak control. Reproducing failures under equivalent conditions extends timelines. Reproducing failures reliably demands skilled experimental control. Repeat runs to reproduce failures add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that setup or handling release is misclassified as transfer-system containment failure
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying setup/handling release as transfer-system failure is a diagnostic attribution error, moderately relevant and modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Without clear source-attribution rules, handling artifacts are frequently misread as device failures. Classifying release source rarely delays the test timeline itself. Correctly attributing release to setup/handling versus the device hinges on operator and analyst expertise that is scarce. Misclassification wastes retest resources but the classification act is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between documented qualification conditions and intended sterile fill-finish transfers
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between documented qualification conditions and intended fill-finish transfers weakens applicability, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Documented qualification conditions often diverge from intended fill-finish transfers, weakening relevance. Comparing documented versus intended conditions is a quick check. Interpreting documented conditions demands careful specialist review. Correcting documentation mismatch is low direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between qualified conditions and intended sterile fill-finish transfers
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between qualified conditions and intended fill-finish transfers undermines relevance of assurance, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: A persistent mismatch between qualified and intended sterile transfer conditions remains common. Assessing the mismatch degree is fast. Quantifying condition mismatch needs process expertise. Closing the mismatch requires added testing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification requirements lacking demonstrable evidence for the recommended system
rows.imp_rat: Qualification requirements lacking demonstrable evidence weaken assurance for the recommended system, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recommended systems often lack demonstrable evidence for every qualification requirement, leaving gaps. Assembling evidence for the recommended system takes time but is a standard deliverable. Judging evidence sufficiency requires experienced evaluators. Generating missing evidence via new testing is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible carryover and cross-contamination pathways between batches omitted from the assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitted carryover/cross-contamination pathways between batches is more a cross-contamination-assessment issue, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Single-use CSTD components reduce carryover, but pathway enumeration remains incomplete. Carryover pathway analysis adds assessment effort. Enumerating cross-contamination pathways depends on process-specific expertise, prone to omission. Missed carryover paths carry deferred contamination cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant sterile fill-finish transfer conditions excluded from containment criteria
rows.imp_rat: Excluding sterile fill-finish transfer conditions from containment criteria addresses a narrower use context; moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Containment criteria frequently exclude real fill-finish conditions, undermining representativeness. Enumerating fill-finish conditions extends criteria-setting but is not the main loss. Knowing which transfer conditions matter requires process-specific fill-finish expertise. Excluded conditions cost little to omit but much to discover missing later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts between containment criteria and sterility or GMP requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts between containment and sterility/GMP requirements create decision ambiguity, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: There is no systematic reconciliation framework, so conflicts routinely remain unresolved at recommendation. Reconciling conflicting requirements lengthens review cycles. Resolving containment-versus-sterility/GMP conflicts requires senior cross-functional judgement. Unresolved conflicts drive costly late redesign.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variability in containment classifications under equivalent transfer conditions
rows.imp_rat: Variability in containment classifications under equivalent conditions undermines evaluation reliability, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Containment classifications vary under equivalent conditions because standardized surrogate methods are still immature. Classification variability shows up across studies over time. Consistent containment classification depends on expert interpretation that varies by evaluator. Reclassification and disputes waste resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between validated materials, container-closure configurations, and transfer conditions and intended sterile fill-finish use
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between validated materials/configs and intended fill-finish use undermines applicability, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Gaps between validated configurations and intended fill-finish use recur frequently. Confirming validated-to-intended-use alignment adds review time. Mapping validated configurations to intended use requires broad process expertise. Mismatch risks costly revalidation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical validation endpoints with ambiguous or conflicting acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Validation endpoints with ambiguous acceptance criteria weaken pass/fail integrity, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Endpoint acceptance criteria are frequently ambiguous or conflicting in current protocols. Clarifying acceptance criteria adds protocol-development time. Defining unambiguous endpoints demands experienced validation authorship. Ambiguous endpoints cause costly rework and disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in validation results across operators, material lots, and intended-use conditions
rows.imp_rat: Variation in validation results across operators/lots/conditions undermines confidence, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validation results vary meaningfully across operators, lots and conditions with limited control. Cross-operator/lot variation emerges only across repeated runs. Controlling operator-dependent variation depends on trained-personnel finesse. Variation drives repeat testing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which sterility verification conditions omit worst-case transfer variables
rows.imp_rat: Sterility verification omitting worst-case variables pertains to sterility side; moderate relevance to the vapor/aerosol CFJ, modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility verification commonly omits worst-case transfer variables, weakening assurance. Adding worst-case variables to sterility verification extends validation duration. Selecting worst-case sterility variables demands specialized aseptic risk expertise. Expanded sterility matrices increase media and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between transfer conditions used for sterility and hazardous-drug containment evaluations
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between sterility and hazardous-drug evaluation conditions is a coordination gap, moderately relevant to valid evaluation with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility and containment evaluations are typically run under different conditions, creating a persistent, poorly controlled mismatch. Reconciling two evaluation condition sets adds coordination time. Aligning sterility and containment test conditions requires cross-disciplinary judgement rarely held by one reviewer. Redundant or mismatched studies waste testing resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved evidence gaps concerning containment, sterility assurance, material compatibility, and regulatory compliance
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved evidence gaps across containment/sterility/compatibility/compliance is a broad assurance concern, moderately relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD evidence remains notoriously fragmented across vendor claims and inconsistent test protocols, so gaps frequently persist undetected into contracting. Assembling containment, sterility, compatibility, and regulatory evidence takes many procurement cycles today, and gaps often surface only after long back-and-forth with suppliers. Judging evidence sufficiency demands cross-functional expertise the purchaser rarely holds alone, forcing heavy reliance on scarce quality and regulatory specialists. Unresolved evidence gaps drive costly late testing, requalification, and rework that inflate total acquisition cost.

rows.source_job: Contain Spills at the Point of Failure
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of contamination on adjacent sterile surfaces during spill containment
rows.imp_rat: Contamination on adjacent sterile surfaces during spill containment is a sterility/cleanup concern peripheral to the CFJ, modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: During an actual spill, adjacent-surface contamination is largely uncontrolled and depends on operator response quality. Contamination of adjacent surfaces during spill control accrues during the response event. Limiting spread requires trained containment technique. Absorbent and cleaning materials are low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required for internal pressure to stabilize after fluid transfer begins
rows.imp_rat: Time for internal pressure to stabilize is a performance-efficiency measure only loosely tied to release prevention, low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Equalization consistently and quickly stabilizes internal pressure in normal use. Pressure stabilization is near-instant with equalization designs, adding negligible time. Stabilization is automatic, requiring no operator action. No added cost beyond the device.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of flow restriction within the established source-to-destination path
rows.imp_rat: Flow restriction within the source-to-destination path is mostly a functional-performance issue only loosely tied to release prevention, low severity.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD flow paths are consistently narrower, so some flow restriction is an accepted, predictable characteristic. Flow restriction slows transfer, and narrow CSTD paths are a known throughput limiter. Little operator skill affects internal flow restriction. No direct cost effect from flow restriction.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of foreign particles introduced into the drug during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Foreign particles introduced into the drug is a product-quality/sterility issue tangential to vapor/aerosol containment, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Needle-free membrane connections greatly reduce particle and coring introduction versus spikes, making this well served. Particle introduction happens at connection moments, not over a duration. Needle-free closed interfaces reduce coring and particle-generating technique risk. No incremental cost for particle control.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of fluid-contact surface exposure during connection transitions
rows.imp_rat: Fluid-contact surface exposure during connection transitions is a sterility timing concern peripheral to vapor/aerosol containment, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed dry-connect transitions keep fluid-contact surface exposure minimal, a well-served design intent. Fluid-contact surface exposure during transitions is brief but not eliminated. Dry-disconnect minimizes exposed wetted surfaces without operator finesse. Inherent to consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile mating surfaces contacting nonsterile hands, materials, or work surfaces during preparation
rows.imp_rat: Sterile mating surfaces contacting nonsterile items is a sterility/technique issue, tangential to the vapor/aerosol CFJ, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed connectors reduce exposed sterile surfaces, but technique-dependent contact still happens intermittently. Aseptic sequencing adds modest time. Keeping sterile surfaces from nonsterile contact depends heavily on trained aseptic technique. No significant added material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of viable contamination remaining on a vial stopper or access port at entry
rows.imp_rat: Viable contamination on a stopper/port at entry is a sterility concern peripheral to vapor/aerosol containment, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Stopper disinfection is standard but variable, leaving occasional viable contamination at entry. Swabbing the stopper adds a brief step. Effective disinfection before entry depends on operator swab technique and dwell. Alcohol swabs are trivial cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unintended sterile-fluid-path exposure between preparation steps
rows.imp_rat: Unintended sterile-path exposure between steps is a sterility timing concern tangential to hazardous-release containment, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed systems keep the path sealed between steps well, though open moments still occur with syringe swaps. Minimizing exposure time between steps is a technique habit costing little. Limiting open exposure relies on disciplined workflow. No added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that container-closure damage introduces particles into the drug during access
rows.imp_rat: Container-closure damage introducing particles is a product-quality/sterility issue tangential to the CFJ, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closure damage introducing particles is uncommon with proper adapters but coring still occurs occasionally. Clean vial access adds no meaningful time. Guided adapters reduce the skill needed to avoid coring particles. Adapters add standard consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identified transfer-procedure deviations not escalated to accountable oversight
rows.imp_rat: Deviations not escalated to oversight is an administrative governance gap, modestly relevant with modest severity for containment.
rows.sat_rat: Deviations still fail to reach oversight when reporting relies on individual initiative rather than automatic routing. Escalation delays exist when reporting paths are manual. Recognizing what must be escalated depends on operator judgment. Escalation itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a shift handoff omits an unresolved hazardous-drug transfer deviation
rows.imp_rat: A shift handoff omitting an unresolved deviation is a communication/governance gap, modestly relevant with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Shift handoff tools like logs and huddles help, but unresolved deviations still get dropped when handoffs are informal. Handoff omissions delay resolution of open deviations into the next shift. Complete handoffs depend on the outgoing operator's diligence in communicating. Handoff logs are cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that drug-contact materials cause unacceptable product-quality changes under proposed use conditions
rows.imp_rat: Drug-contact materials causing product-quality changes is a compatibility/product concern tangential to vapor/aerosol containment, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard stability and compatibility testing reasonably surfaces product-quality effects, though rare-condition effects persist. Product-quality impact studies extend over stability timelines. Assessing quality changes uses established analytical-chemistry expertise. Stability and quality testing are significant recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable GMP requirements omitted from the suitability assessment
rows.imp_rat: GMP requirements omitted from suitability assessment is a compliance-completeness issue tangential to vapor/aerosol containment, modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established GMP checklists make requirement coverage fairly complete, though niche requirements are still occasionally missed. GMP requirement mapping adds documented review time. GMP requirement identification is well-supported by regulatory frameworks and QA specialists. Compliance review is a routine, budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing sterile boundary integrity during the longest intended hold interval
rows.imp_rat: Losing sterile boundary integrity during hold is a sterility concern tangential to the hazardous-release CFJ, moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Container-closure integrity and hold-time methods are mature, giving reasonable assurance over intended holds. Hold-time studies require extended real-time incubation, inherently slow. Designing longest-hold integrity studies uses established stability/hold-time expertise. Hold-time studies consume material over long periods, a defined cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transfer-related contamination remains undetected during sterility verification
rows.imp_rat: Transfer contamination undetected during sterility verification is a sterility-detection issue tangential to the vapor/aerosol CFJ, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility verification detects some transfer contamination but coverage of transfer-specific modes is incomplete. Sterility verification incubation delays detection of transfer contamination. Distinguishing transfer-related contamination requires microbiological interpretive skill. Additional targeted sampling adds moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the wrong transfer-path junction is disconnected after fluid movement stops
rows.imp_rat: Disconnecting the wrong junction after flow stops risks opening a hazardous path, but it is a discrete manipulation error capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: With multiple similar junctions and no labeling standard, wrong-junction disconnection is an operator-error-prone situation. Disconnecting the wrong junction wastes time reestablishing the correct state. Correct junction identification relies on operator tracking of the setup. Wrong disconnection may waste components or drug.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting the hazardous-drug source to the wrong destination
rows.imp_rat: Connecting the source to the wrong destination could cause misrouting/exposure, but as a dosing-routing defect it caps at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: There is no physical interlock preventing wrong-destination connection, so it relies on procedural checks, leaving a real error gap. Misrouting to the wrong destination is caught only by verification steps, costing rework time. Correct routing depends on operator attention and labeling checks. A wrong connection wastes drug and components.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transferring hazardous drug into the wrong destination container
rows.imp_rat: Transferring drug into the wrong container is a dosing-safety error with exposure potential, yet ranked below batch 2 at 2.0 max.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/label verification helps in some settings but the connection itself has no compatibility interlock, so errors persist. Wrong-container transfer is discovered late, requiring costly correction time. Correct container selection depends on operator verification. Wrong transfers waste drug and destination containers.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of extra manual manipulations caused by incompatible source and destination interfaces
rows.imp_rat: Extra manual manipulations from incompatible interfaces could increase exposure opportunities, but it is efficiency waste ranked below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Within a single vendor system this is largely designed out, but cross-system incompatibility still forces extra manipulations. Incompatible interfaces force extra manual manipulations that add handling time. Adapters and workarounds require operator know-how to manage. Adapters add consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reconnection manipulations caused by incomplete connection engagement
rows.imp_rat: Reconnection from incomplete engagement adds manipulation and potential leak points, yet it is rework subordinate to batch 2 at 2.0 max.
rows.sat_rat: Click-lock feedback reduces but does not eliminate reconnection attempts, an occasional recurring nuisance. Reconnection after incomplete engagement adds repeat handling time. Achieving full engagement first time depends on operator technique. Repeated handling risks wasting components.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterility breach remains undetected before transferred product proceeds downstream
rows.imp_rat: An undetected sterility breach proceeding downstream affects product quality more than vapor containment; relevant but indirect and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is no real-time sterility-breach detection, so breaches can proceed undetected until later testing, a poorly served need. Undetected sterility breach may not surface until downstream testing, a long delay. Detection depends on inspection and aseptic judgement with no inline sensor. Undetected breaches risk costly product loss downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer connections repeated after failed first-attempt engagement
rows.imp_rat: Repeated connections after failed engagement add manipulation and potential leak exposure, but it is rework ranked below batch 2 at 2.0 max.
rows.sat_rat: Most connections seat on the first attempt with lock feedback, so repeats are relatively uncommon. Repeating a failed connection adds time to the transfer. First-attempt success depends on operator technique. Repeated attempts risk wasting components.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer connections omitted from integrity verification before fluid movement
rows.imp_rat: Connections omitted from integrity verification could leave undetected leak points, but it is a verification-completeness defect capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Verification completeness relies entirely on procedure and attention, so omissions occur without a systematic guard. Skipping integrity verification saves time but is the error being reduced. Complete verification depends on operator diligence with no forcing function. No material cost to verification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the wrong fluid-path junction is disconnected after dose transfer
rows.imp_rat: Disconnecting the wrong junction could open a hazardous path, yet it is a discrete manipulation error ranked below batch 2 at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Wrong-junction disconnection is infrequent but relies on vigilance rather than a forcing function. Confirming the right junction adds a moment. Correct junction selection depends on operator attention in multi-line setups. Wrong disconnection may waste a dose.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous drug preparations performed with damaged transfer components
rows.imp_rat: Preparations with damaged components could compromise containment, but as a component-condition defect it is ranked below batch 2 at 2.0 max.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-use inspection catches most damaged components, but subtle defects occasionally get used. Rejecting damaged components adds a brief check. Spotting damaged components before use depends on operator inspection. Discarding damaged components wastes cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of preparations discarded after sterile-boundary breaches
rows.imp_rat: Preparations discarded after sterile-boundary breaches is a waste/cost outcome peripheral to the vapor/aerosol containment CFJ, low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-boundary breaches leading to discard occur at a moderate, manageable rate under standard practice. Discarding and remaking a preparation costs redo time. Avoiding boundary breaches that force discard relies on aseptic skill. Discarded preparations waste drug and components, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting the wrong source or destination container during dose transfer
rows.imp_rat: Connecting the wrong container is a manipulation error that could cause spills/exposure, but as a dosing-safety defect it caps at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Independent verification and barcode checks reduce wrong-container errors, though manual checks still fail occasionally. Verifying containers adds a check step. Preventing wrong-container connection relies on operator verification and label reading. Wrong connections waste materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification decisions approving staff despite containment-critical execution errors
rows.imp_rat: Approving staff despite containment-critical errors could enable exposure, but as a qualification-decision defect it is upstream of actual release and capped at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Evaluators sometimes pass staff despite critical errors because scoring criteria are subjective and inconsistently applied. Qualification decisions do not add floor time directly. Judging containment-critical execution errors during assessment requires expert evaluators. Rigorous assessment consumes evaluator time and resource.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualified staff develop containment-critical execution gaps before reassessment
rows.imp_rat: Competence decay before reassessment can erode containment execution, yet it is a gradual reassessment-timing issue subordinate to batch 2 items.
rows.sat_rat: Long periods between reassessments let containment-critical gaps develop undetected. Skill decay between assessments accrues over the reassessment interval. Preventing execution-gap drift depends on ongoing practice and observation, which is scarce. Frequent reassessment costs supervisory time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous-drug transfers governed by obsolete or conflicting procedure revisions
rows.imp_rat: Transfers governed by obsolete procedures could allow unsafe technique, but it is a document-control error ranked below batch 2 at 2.0 max.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems largely ensure current procedures, though obsolete copies occasionally remain in use. Using an obsolete revision is not slower per transfer but causes rework when discovered. Ensuring current revisions requires document-control discipline rather than operator skill. Document control systems are modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of competency evaluations that pass personnel who incorrectly execute critical safe-handling steps
rows.imp_rat: Passing personnel who misexecute safe-handling steps risks exposure, but as an evaluation defect it is upstream and ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Evaluations frequently pass workers who err on critical steps because scoring is lenient and subjective. Competency evaluation timing is not the issue. Reliable evaluation depends on evaluator expertise to catch incorrectly executed critical steps, which varies widely. Evaluation is labor cost, modest per event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel begin hazardous-drug handling before their qualification is verified
rows.imp_rat: Unverified qualification before handling risks exposure but is a policy-adherence error; ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Training-record systems and supervisor sign-off make pre-handling qualification verification a fairly mature, reliable practice, though gaps persist. Qualification checks are quick when embedded in scheduling systems. Verifying qualification before handling is administrative, needing little specialized skill. Learning-management and access-control systems that gate handling are established and affordable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that personnel remain authorized for hazardous-drug handling after their qualifications expire
rows.imp_rat: Expired authorization continuing is a compliance lapse that could enable poor technique, but it is administrative and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated expiry alerts in LMS platforms make this reasonably well controlled, though lapses still occur. Expired qualifications can linger active for a time because renewal tracking is imperfect. Tracking expirations is administrative and low-skill. Training-management systems that flag expirations are inexpensive and common.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between recognition of a hazardous-drug release and notification of the investigation lead
rows.imp_rat: Delay notifying the investigation lead after a release slows response; the release already occurred, so relevance to prevention is indirect.
rows.sat_rat: Standard incident-reporting SOPs make notification reasonably consistent once the release is actually recognized. Notifying the investigation lead after a recognized release is usually prompt via existing escalation chains. Escalation requires little specialized skill once a release is recognized. Notification mechanisms cost nothing significant.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of additional worker exposure during incident-scene assessment
rows.imp_rat: Additional worker exposure during scene assessment is real harm, but it is a post-incident procedural error capped at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Because residual airborne HD is invisible, assessors can be exposed during scene assessment, a poorly controlled risk. Scene assessment can begin before hazards are cleared if procedures are rushed. Safe assessment depends on trained responders knowing residual airborne risk, expertise that is uneven. PPE and detection for safe assessment cost little relative to benefit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the investigation stops at the immediate failure before identifying the underlying cause
rows.imp_rat: Stopping at immediate failure without root cause risks recurrence, but it is an investigation-depth defect capped at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Investigations frequently stop at the proximate failure, so recurring underlying causes persist. Root-cause depth is not primarily a time issue. Reaching underlying causes rather than immediate failures requires trained root-cause analysis expertise often lacking. Deeper investigation costs analyst time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identified exposure causes not addressed by corrective actions
rows.imp_rat: Exposure causes not addressed by corrective actions can leave hazards unmitigated, but it is a post-incident rework gap ranked below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful share of identified causes go unaddressed because CAPA follow-through is inconsistent. CAPA closure timing is a secondary concern here. Linking causes to effective corrective actions requires analytical rigor that varies. CAPA implementation carries cost that can incentivize incomplete closure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that localized or transient containment failures are masked by aggregate exposure-control KPIs
rows.imp_rat: Aggregate KPIs masking localized failures can hide real releases, yet it is a monitoring-design concern subordinate to batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregate KPIs routinely hide localized or transient containment failures, a recognized and unresolved blind spot. KPI reporting cadence is not the core issue. Detecting masked local failures requires analytical skill to disaggregate KPIs that many lack. Finer-grained monitoring costs more data collection effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protocol execution compromises sterile conditions in the fill-finish environment
rows.imp_rat: Protocol execution compromising sterility risks product quality more than hazardous-vapor containment; relevance to the CFJ is indirect and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established aseptic practices in fill-finish environments make sterility compromise during evaluation relatively controllable. Protecting sterility during protocol execution may add controlled setup time. Executing containment tests without compromising sterility requires aseptic-technique expertise. Maintaining sterile conditions during testing adds facility and consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the worker-safety recommendation when vapor and aerosol evidence conflicts
rows.imp_rat: Uncertain recommendation when vapor/aerosol evidence conflicts could misguide selection, but it is an evidence-interpretation issue ranked below batch 2.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of containment failures concealed by aggregated candidate results
rows.imp_rat: Aggregation concealing failures could hide real containment gaps, but it is a reporting defect ranked below batch 2 at 2.0 max.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregated candidate results can conceal individual containment failures, a known reporting weakness. Disaggregating results is a quick analysis step. Recognizing concealment in aggregated data needs statistical acuity. No material cost beyond analyst time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable worker-protection containment and aseptic requirements omitted from acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting protection requirements from acceptance criteria could leave containment untested, but as a criteria-completeness defect it caps at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Applicable worker-protection and aseptic requirements are often partially omitted from acceptance criteria. Mapping worker-protection requirements to criteria is upfront work. Knowing all applicable worker-protection and aseptic requirements needs regulatory expertise. Compliance coverage adds review labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an observed containment failure is assigned to the wrong root cause
rows.imp_rat: Misassigning a containment-failure root cause can misdirect fixes, but the failure was already detected; relevance is indirect and ranking caps at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Failures are often assigned to the wrong root cause because attribution methods are informal. Root-cause assignment can extend investigation time. Correct root-cause attribution is highly expertise-dependent and error-prone. Misattribution wastes remediation resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of observed containment failures with an unresolved release origin within the transfer system
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved release origin leaves a real failure incompletely understood, but detection already occurred; ranking limits it to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Many observed failures leave the release origin unresolved, weakening conclusions. Resolving release origin extends investigation duration. Pinpointing release origin within the device needs deep expertise. Investigative teardown and retesting add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of post-validation changes lacking documented impact assessment against qualified transfer conditions
rows.imp_rat: Post-validation changes lacking impact assessment could silently degrade containment, but it is a change-control gap ranked below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems exist but post-validation changes still sometimes lack documented impact assessment. Change-impact assessment adds documentation time. Assessing post-validation change impact uses established change-control expertise. Unassessed changes risk costly revalidation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical evidence gaps unresolved when the transfer method is recommended
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved evidence gaps at recommendation time only indirectly affect containment of vapors/aerosols; consequences are procedural, and per ranking this sits below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Critical evidence gaps are frequently unresolved when recommendations are made. Chasing evidence gaps before recommending delays the decision. Recognizing critical gaps demands seasoned reviewer judgement. Recommending with gaps risks costly downstream failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incoming CSTD lots rejected for containment, package-integrity, or specification defects
rows.imp_rat: Rejected lots for containment/integrity defects show incoming-quality issues, but detection at receipt limits release risk and ranking caps at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established incoming inspection and CoA review catch most defects, though containment and package-integrity failures still slip through occasionally. Incoming inspection adds handling time but is a routine established step. Standard incoming QC procedures reduce reliance on specialized judgement for lot acceptance. Rejected lots waste procurement spend and expedite costs when replacements are needed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that failure of the sole qualified source causes complete loss of CSTD supply
rows.imp_rat: Sole-source failure causing supply loss threatens continuity, but it is a supply-chain risk indirect to the containment CFJ and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD markets are concentrated and proprietary interfaces lock buyers into single sources, leaving supply catastrophically exposed. Qualifying a second source takes long lead times, so single-source risk lingers unaddressed for extended periods. Dual-sourcing CSTDs demands specialized qualification effort that stretches limited technical resources. Maintaining a backup qualified source carries duplicate qualification and inventory costs that many buyers avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of breach indications while containment remains intact during transfer
rows.imp_rat: False breach indications while containment holds cause unnecessary alarm, a reliability nuisance indirect to actual release.
rows.sat_rat: With minimal active indication today, false-positive breach signals are rare simply because indication is sparse, but interpretation is inconsistent. False breach indications waste investigation time. Distinguishing false from real indications requires operator judgement. False alarms cost time, not materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of manual checks caused by ambiguous transfer-connection status
rows.imp_rat: Manual checks from ambiguous connection status add effort, a usability concern indirect to actual containment and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguity-driven rechecks are a recurring workflow drag with no automated status confirmation. Ambiguous status triggers extra manual checks that consume time. Interpreting ambiguous status needs operator experience. No material cost, only time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of drug-flow interruptions during closed transfer
rows.imp_rat: Flow interruptions during closed transfer are a reliability nuisance; the system stays closed, so containment relevance is indirect.
rows.sat_rat: Interruptions from narrow paths and pressure management are a known, recurring characteristic of CSTD transfers. Flow interruptions during closed transfer extend overall duration. Minimizing interruptions relies on operator monitoring. No direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of integrity-verification results assigned to the wrong transfer connection
rows.imp_rat: Misassigned integrity-verification results distort diagnostics, an analysis defect with indirect containment relevance and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual verification logging is error-prone with no automated linkage, leaving misassignment risk. Misassigned verification results cause rework time later. Correct assignment depends on careful operator record-keeping. No material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of syringe-plunger displacement caused by pressure imbalance before dose measurement is finalized
rows.imp_rat: Plunger displacement from pressure imbalance is a dose-measurement stability issue, indirect to containment and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Balanced-pressure CSTDs largely prevent plunger creep before measurement, improving reading stability. Plunger stabilization adds negligible time. Pressure-equalized devices reduce the skill needed to hold the plunger steady. Equalization design is standard cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of containment-critical actions omitted from hazardous-drug transfer qualification criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting containment actions from qualification criteria is a documentation-scope gap; relevance to actual vapor control is indirect and severity is administrative.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification criteria often omit some containment-critical actions because checklists are built from generic templates. Criteria completeness does not affect transfer timing. Building complete criteria requires expert task analysis of containment-critical actions. Developing thorough criteria is a one-time modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which qualification assessment conditions differ from hazardous-drug transfer conditions on the floor
rows.imp_rat: Assessment conditions differing from the floor reduces realism but is a validation-design gap, subordinate to batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Assessments often occur in idealized conditions, so qualification poorly predicts real floor performance. Off-floor assessment conditions do not affect transfer duration. Matching assessment conditions to real floor conditions requires deliberate scenario design skill. On-floor or simulated assessment costs more than classroom testing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among evaluators judging equivalent hazardous-drug transfer execution
rows.imp_rat: Evaluator disagreement on equivalent execution is a consistency issue, peripheral to actual containment and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inter-evaluator agreement is inconsistent because scoring rubrics leave room for subjective interpretation. Evaluator disagreement does not affect transfer timing. Consistent judgment across evaluators requires calibrated, expert assessors that are scarce. Evaluator calibration consumes training resource.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between a competence-affecting change and reassessment of affected staff
rows.imp_rat: Delay between a change and reassessment is a timing gap; its containment impact is indirect and severity administrative.
rows.sat_rat: Reassessment is usually calendar-driven rather than event-driven, so change-triggered reassessment is inconsistent. The lag from a competence-affecting change to reassessment is typically long under periodic scheduling. Triggering timely reassessment requires someone to recognize the change's relevance. Ad hoc reassessment adds resource demand.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether a hazardous-drug transfer deviation surfaced soon enough for intervention
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether a deviation surfaced in time is a detection-confidence issue, indirect to release and low severity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous-drug release incidents or worker-exposure indicators are omitted from accountability records
rows.imp_rat: Omitting incidents from accountability records is a recordkeeping defect; the release already happened, so relevance to prevention is indirect.
rows.sat_rat: Under-reporting means release incidents and exposure indicators are sometimes omitted from accountability records. Recording incidents is not a speed problem. Capturing all incidents in accountability records is administrative, low-skill. Recordkeeping systems are inexpensive and common.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical hazardous-drug handling requirements omitted from competency criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting requirements from competency criteria is a documentation gap with indirect containment relevance and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Competency criteria often omit critical handling requirements because they are drafted from generic templates rather than task analysis. Building competency criteria is an upfront exercise, not time-driven. Defining complete criteria requires subject-matter expertise to capture all critical steps. Criteria development is one-time labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which competency evaluations differ from actual sterile fill-finish handling conditions
rows.imp_rat: Competency evaluations differing from real conditions reduces realism; it is a validation-design gap with indirect containment effect.
rows.sat_rat: Competency evaluations often differ from real fill-finish conditions, so demonstrated competence overstates field performance. Evaluation realism is a design issue, not timing. Designing evaluations that mirror real handling needs process expertise that is scarce. Realistic evaluation setups cost more than checklist demos.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation among evaluators' competency judgments for the same worker execution
rows.imp_rat: Evaluator variation in judging the same execution is a consistency concern, peripheral to actual release and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Different evaluators reach different judgments on the same execution because criteria are subjective and calibration is weak. Timing is not the concern in evaluator consistency. Inter-rater consistency depends on evaluator calibration and expertise that varies significantly. Calibration training is a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant workers, batches, or operating intervals omitted from investigation scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting workers or intervals from investigation scope narrows learning, an analysis gap with indirect containment relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Relevant workers, batches, or intervals are often omitted from scope, leaving exposure extent understated. Scope definition is not time-critical. Defining complete investigation scope requires experienced judgement that varies. Broader scope costs more investigator effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in specified protective levels and pass-or-fail criteria
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous protective levels and pass/fail criteria affect judgment consistency, not the physical release; ranking caps at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Protective levels and pass/fail criteria are often vaguely specified, leaving room for inconsistent interpretation. Specifying criteria is an upfront task, not time-driven. Writing unambiguous pass/fail criteria requires expertise to translate protective goals into observable behaviors. This is documentation labor, low material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of verification results attributed to the wrong containment configuration or production condition
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing results to the wrong configuration distorts evidence, an analysis defect with indirect containment relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Verification results are sometimes misattributed to the wrong configuration or condition, corrupting the evidence base. Attribution errors are not about speed. Correctly attributing results to configurations requires careful documentation discipline that varies. Better tracking is low-cost labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of candidate containment claims lacking traceable supporting observations
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable containment claims weaken evidence quality but do not directly cause release; both proxies are modest.
rows.sat_rat: Many containment claims lack traceable supporting observations, so buyers cannot verify them. Requiring traceable support for claims adds documentation review time. Auditing claim traceability requires evidence-evaluation skill. Traceability documentation adds administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that candidate containment rankings change under plausible sterile fill-finish transfer conditions
rows.imp_rat: Ranking instability under plausible conditions is a selection-robustness issue, peripheral to actual release prevention.
rows.sat_rat: Candidate rankings can flip under plausible real conditions, and this instability is rarely tested. Testing ranking stability under varied conditions requires additional runs and time. Assessing ranking robustness requires sensitivity-analysis expertise. Robustness testing across conditions adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among worker-safety product-quality and regulatory requirements defining permissible release
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among safety/quality/regulatory definitions of permissible release is a requirements-reconciliation gap, indirect and low-severity here.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts among worker-safety, product-quality, and regulatory definitions of permissible release are common and unresolved. Reconciling competing requirements takes deliberation time but not test time. Resolving conflicts among safety, quality, and regulatory limits demands cross-disciplinary expert judgement. Reconciliation is mostly an expert-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the disposition of inconclusive containment results
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in dispositioning inconclusive results is a decision-process gap, peripheral to physical containment and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inconclusive-result disposition is frequently ambiguous, producing inconsistent decisions. Defining disposition rules for inconclusive results is a one-time policy step. Setting defensible disposition rules for inconclusive results requires statistical and regulatory judgement. Rule definition has negligible material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in exposure-pathway definitions across candidate approaches
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent exposure-pathway definitions across candidates is a comparison-methodology issue peripheral to actual release.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure-pathway definitions vary across candidates, undermining comparability. Standardizing pathway definitions is a definitional coordination step. Consistent exposure-pathway definitions require shared expert frameworks that are scarce. Definition consistency is low material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in translating quantified hazardous-drug releases into residual worker health risk
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty translating releases into residual health risk is an interpretation gap downstream of containment, capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Large uncertainty persists in converting measured releases into worker health risk. Translating releases into residual risk adds modeling time. Quantitative risk translation requires toxicology and exposure-modeling expertise that is scarce. Risk modeling is mostly expert-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which residual worker-risk estimates omit exposure severity or task recurrence
rows.imp_rat: Omitting severity or recurrence from risk estimates weakens analysis but does not itself cause release; both proxies modest.
rows.sat_rat: Residual-risk estimates commonly omit severity or recurrence, biasing risk conclusions. Incorporating severity and recurrence adds analysis time. Accounting for exposure severity and task recurrence requires risk-assessment expertise. Fuller risk models cost more expert time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of doubt that the recommended transfer approach keeps worker exposure within defined limits
rows.imp_rat: Duration of doubt about exposure limits is a confidence-timing issue; it does not itself cause release and caps at 2.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility loss when worker-protection evidence behind a recommendation fails scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when evidence fails scrutiny is a reputational/assurance concern, indirect to preventing release and low severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in acceptance thresholds across candidate systems
rows.imp_rat: Variation in acceptance thresholds across systems is a comparability issue, peripheral to actual release and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance thresholds vary across candidate systems, making comparison unreliable. Comparing thresholds across candidates is quick. Judging threshold comparability requires expert framing. Harmonizing thresholds costs little directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of candidate ranking instability under reasonable changes to criterion weights
rows.imp_rat: Ranking instability under weight changes is a decision-robustness issue, largely unrelated to actual release; both proxies modest.
rows.sat_rat: Candidate rankings are often unstable under reasonable weight changes and this is seldom checked. Sensitivity analysis adds modest analysis time. Testing ranking stability under weight changes requires decision-analysis skill rarely applied. Sensitivity analysis is low cost if done.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts between containment criteria and aseptic integrity requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved containment-versus-aseptic conflicts are a requirements-reconciliation gap with indirect, low-severity impact on release.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved conflicts between containment and aseptic requirements commonly persist. Resolving containment-versus-aseptic conflicts takes deliberation time. Reconciling containment and aseptic integrity demands rare dual expertise. Conflict resolution is mostly expert labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts between protocol requirements and applicable regulatory quality or user obligations
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved protocol-versus-regulatory conflicts are compliance-reconciliation issues, indirect to release and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts between protocol requirements and regulatory or user obligations frequently go unresolved. Reconciling regulatory obligations adds review time. Aligning protocol with regulatory and user obligations needs specialist knowledge. Regulatory reconciliation is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between observing a containment failure and reaching an evidence-supported root-cause conclusion
rows.imp_rat: Time to reach a root-cause conclusion after a failure is an investigation-timing matter; the failure already occurred, capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Delays in root-cause conclusions leave decisions pending and evidence stale. The gap between observing a failure and concluding root cause is often long due to slow investigation cycles. Fast conclusions depend on scarce investigative expertise. Expedited investigation may require added resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that documented vapor or aerosol findings are confounded by unrelated contamination
rows.imp_rat: Confounded findings distort measurement rather than allow real releases; severity is low and it sits below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Findings can be confounded by unrelated contamination, and controls to prevent this are inconsistently applied. Ruling out confounding contamination adds investigation time. Distinguishing genuine findings from contamination needs analytical skill. Cleanliness controls and blanks add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material containment or implementation risks omitted from the recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting risks from the recommendation is a completeness gap with indirect containment relevance and administrative severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recommendations frequently omit material containment or implementation risks, exposing decisions. Documenting risks adds report-writing time. Identifying material implementation risks requires broad experience. Risk documentation is low direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the assessment underrepresents operator-dependent assembly and transfer variability
rows.imp_rat: Underrepresenting operator variability weakens assessment realism but is a study-design gap with indirect containment consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Assessments systematically underrepresent operator-dependent assembly variability. Capturing operator variability requires extended multi-operator studies. CSTD assembly is highly operator-dependent and assessments rarely capture that finesse variation. Multi-operator studies add cost that is often skipped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in interpreting containment acceptance thresholds
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in interpreting containment thresholds affects consistency of judgment, not the physical release itself; severity is low and it ranks below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Containment acceptance thresholds remain ambiguous because industry standards are still evolving. Resolving threshold ambiguity adds interpretation time. Interpreting containment thresholds requires containment expertise given weak standardization. Ambiguity drives disputes and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the transfer interfaces and manipulations covered by containment criteria
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity over which interfaces are covered by criteria is a scope-definition issue with indirect, administrative consequences for containment.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria often leave the covered interfaces and manipulations ambiguous. Defining covered interfaces adds scoping time. Specifying which manipulations criteria cover requires detailed process expertise. Ambiguous scope causes costly coverage gaps.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that background hazardous-drug contamination confounds containment findings
rows.imp_rat: Background contamination confounding containment findings distorts measurement rather than causing release; severity low and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Background hazardous-drug contamination frequently confounds surrogate containment findings and is hard to fully eliminate. Background contamination checks add setup and analysis time. Controlling background hazardous-drug contamination requires specialized decontamination and analytical skill. Baseline wipe sampling and cleaning add measurable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical validation conditions supported by insufficient valid replicate runs
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient replicate runs weakens validation confidence but is a statistical-evidence gap peripheral to preventing an actual release.
rows.sat_rat: Insufficient valid replicates for critical conditions remain common, weakening confidence. Additional replicate runs extend study duration. Determining adequate replication uses established statistical-validation skill. More replicates cost more material and labor, often trimmed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of representative transfer executions failing acceptance criteria because of execution variability
rows.imp_rat: Executions failing criteria due to variability signals inconsistent technique, a validation-outcome concern with indirect containment effect.
rows.sat_rat: Execution-variability failures recur because assembly is technique-sensitive. Execution failures trigger repeat runs that extend timelines. Reducing execution variability depends on operator training and technique. Failed executions waste material and rerun cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among containment and sterility findings across studies, material lots, or evaluators
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among findings across studies is an evidence-consistency concern, only loosely tied to actual vapor release and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Findings frequently disagree across studies, lots and evaluators without a resolution mechanism. Cross-study disagreements surface over the evaluation program. Reconciling divergent findings needs expert adjudication. Disagreements trigger additional confirmatory testing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty that hazardous-drug protections are sufficient while setting acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty about protection sufficiency is a confidence-timing issue; it does not cause release and caps at 2.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among containment, sterility, production, and procurement requirements
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among containment/sterility/production/procurement requirements is a reconciliation gap, peripheral to physical release and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: No standard framework aligns these competing requirements, so conflict is a persistent and poorly managed friction. Reconciling competing containment, sterility, production, and procurement requirements drags out through repeated cross-functional meetings. Resolving these conflicts demands seasoned facilitation and domain judgement across several disciplines. Unresolved requirement conflict leads to rework and compromise choices with hidden cost consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of CSTD deliveries rendered unusable by unapproved changes to materials, design, or manufacturing site
rows.imp_rat: Deliveries unusable from unapproved changes is a supply/change-control issue, indirect to actual release and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Change-notification clauses are common but supplier compliance is inconsistent, so unapproved changes still cause rejections. Unapproved changes surface without warning, forcing sudden requalification delays. Detecting undisclosed material or site changes requires vigilant change-control expertise that is not always present. Unusable deliveries from unnotified changes cause scrap and rebuy costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of hazardous-drug transfer conditions omitted from the proposed investment scope before capital authorization
rows.imp_rat: Omitting transfer conditions from investment scope is a capital-planning gap, indirect to actual containment and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Scope omissions are common today because there is no standard checklist that guarantees all transfer conditions are captured before authorization. Enumerating every transfer condition is slow but the recurring failure is completeness, not speed. Identifying all hazardous-drug transfer conditions requires deep operational knowledge that the purchase executor must borrow from pharmacy and safety staff. Omitted conditions surface later as unplanned capital, a real but secondary loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed hazardous-drug transfers missing an execution record
rows.plain: Fewer completed transfers of dangerous medicines should be missing a record of what was done. A record makes it possible to confirm that each transfer took place correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing execution records is a documentation defect loosely tied to the containment job (relevance low) with consequences confined to traceability (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without automated logging, omitted execution records are a known manual-documentation gap. Missing records are found only during reconciliation, delaying batch review. Completeness depends on operator remembering to document. No material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aborted or partial hazardous-drug transfers recorded as complete
rows.plain: The records should not say that a transfer of dangerous medicine was finished when it was stopped early or only partly completed. Accurate records help people know what actually happened and avoid unsafe follow-up work.
rows.imp_rat: Recording aborted transfers as complete is a record-integrity defect only loosely tied to preventing vapor/aerosol release (relevance low); consequences are documentation gaps rather than physical exposure (severity low). Ranking caps this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual record-keeping without automated capture makes miscategorized transfers a recurring documentation error. Misrecording an aborted transfer as complete surfaces only in later review. Accurate status recording depends on operator diligence. No direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer records linked to the wrong batch or destination
rows.plain: The person carrying out the transfer wants fewer records connected to the wrong production batch or receiving location. This helps ensure the transfer is traceable to the correct place and product.
rows.imp_rat: Records linked to wrong batch/destination is a data-integrity defect tangential to vapor/aerosol control (relevance low) with traceability consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual batch/destination linkage is transcription-error-prone without barcode automation, a persistent risk. Wrong-batch links are caught late, causing reconciliation delay. Correct linkage depends on operator transcription accuracy. Mislinked records can jeopardize batch value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from hazardous-drug transfer completion to execution-record entry
rows.plain: Record the work as soon as possible after moving a hazardous medicine is finished. A prompt record creates a clearer and more reliable account of what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Time to execution-record entry is a documentation-timeliness delay peripheral to vapor/aerosol control (relevance low) with traceability consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deferred manual documentation is routine, so timely record entry is inconsistently achieved. Delay between transfer completion and record entry is the core error, and manual entry is often deferred. Timely entry relies on operator habit rather than any prompt. No material cost to entry timing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous-drug transfer records missing operator attribution
rows.plain: Reduce the number of records about moving dangerous medicines that do not identify the person who performed the work. This makes it clear who carried out each transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Missing operator attribution in records is a documentation defect peripheral to vapor/aerosol control (relevance low) with traceability consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual sign-off usually captures attribution but omissions still occur without automated identity linkage. Missing operator attribution surfaces only in audit, delaying traceability. Attribution depends on operators signing records manually. No material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous-drug transfer records missing a breach or intervention detail
rows.plain: Reduce the number of records about moving dangerous medicines that fail to say what went wrong or what was done about it. This helps people understand and respond to problems safely.
rows.imp_rat: Missing breach/intervention detail in records is a documentation defect loosely tied to the job (relevance low) with traceability consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Intervention details are inconsistently documented because capture is fully manual, leaving frequent gaps. Missing breach/intervention detail is discovered late during investigation. Capturing these details relies on operator recall and discipline. No material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between transferred drug quantity and target quantity
rows.plain: Make sure the amount of drug moved is as close as possible to the amount that was supposed to be moved. This helps ensure each transfer is accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between transferred and target quantity is a dose-accuracy issue, essentially unrelated to hazardous-vapor containment.
rows.sat_rat: Volumetric accuracy relies on manual measurement rather than the CSTD itself, so deviation is a recurring variability. Quantity deviation is detected at measurement, and correction takes time. Accurate quantity depends on operator reading and technique. Deviation wastes drug or requires rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of intended drug quantity retained in the source or transfer path after transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of medicine left behind in the original container or the path it travels through after a transfer. This helps deliver more of the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Drug retained in source or path after transfer is a waste/dose-loss concern with minimal direct containment relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Residual hold-up volume is consistent per device but not minimized, so drug loss is predictable and accepted. Retained drug is a waste issue rather than a time issue. Reducing hold-up relies partly on priming/flushing technique. CSTD dead-volume traps expensive hazardous drug, a recognized material-cost loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of preceding-drug residue entering the destination during transfer
