rows.plain: Keep leftover material from the previous drug out of the place receiving the next drug during a transfer. This helps prevent one product from contaminating another.
rows.imp_rat: Preceding-drug residue entering the destination is a cross-contamination/quality issue, not vapor/aerosol release; both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Single-use CSTD components largely prevent preceding-drug residue, making carryover well controlled. Cross-contamination residue is addressed by flushing, adding modest time. Preventing carryover relies on line-clearance discipline. Dedicated single-use paths add consumable cost but prevent carryover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of repeated actuations required to open or close the transfer path
rows.plain: Reduce how many times a person must operate the controls to open or close the route used to move materials. Fewer repeated actions make the work quicker and easier.
rows.imp_rat: Repeated actuations to open/close the path are a usability nuisance with minimal direct containment consequence; both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard CSTD actuation usually works first try, so repeated actuation is infrequent. Repeated actuations to open/close the path add cumulative time. Actuation is straightforward, needing little skill. No material cost per actuation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of setup manipulations required to orient transfer components at the point of use
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times workers must handle and turn parts so they face the right way when used. Fewer handling steps make the job simpler and reduce opportunities for mistakes or damage.
rows.imp_rat: Setup manipulations to orient components is an efficiency issue, essentially unrelated to preventing release; both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Setup manipulation counts are stable and predictable, though not minimized by design. Orienting components at point of use adds setup manipulations each transfer. Correct orientation relies on learned handling routine. No material cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of repositioning manipulations caused by obstructed transfer-interface access
rows.plain: Reduce the need to move or adjust things again when the access point for moving materials is blocked. This makes the work smoother and reduces extra handling.
rows.imp_rat: Repositioning manipulations from obstructed access is a usability/waste concern with minimal direct containment relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Access obstruction is layout-dependent and inconsistently managed, a modest recurring inefficiency. Repositioning for obstructed access adds setup time. Working around cramped access requires operator dexterity. No direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transfer interruption before controlled fluid movement resumes

rows.imp_rat: Hesitation awaiting a go-or-stop decision is an emotional/timing delay peripheral to the mechanism (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.plain: They want a quick decision about whether to continue moving a dangerous medicine. This helps workers know how to proceed safely without compromising the work.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while awaiting a transfer decision is an emotional-state delay peripheral to the mechanism (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of air included as drug in the prepared dose-volume reading

rows.sat_rat: Dead-volume retention after measurement is a known, only partly mitigated loss in CSTD fluid paths. Recovering fluid-path holdup adds some time. Minimizing measured-dose holdup relies on technique. Drug retained in the path is wasted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in locating the liquid boundary against syringe graduations

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cumulative volume deviation across partial withdrawals for one dose

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty about the transferred volume after an interrupted withdrawal

rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about volume after interrupted withdrawal is a dose-accuracy concern with negligible direct containment relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Post-interruption volume uncertainty is a recurring manual-tracking weakness. Reassessing after interruption adds time. Determining volume after an interrupted withdrawal relies on operator reconstruction. Errors here can waste a dose.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of benign transfer artifacts classified as containment breaches

rows.sat_rat: Without reliable detection, benign artifacts are inconsistently classified, so this need is weakly served. Investigating false-positive breaches wastes time. rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the intended dose stranded in the fluid path by premature disconnection
rows.plain: Prevent any of the planned product amount from being left behind in the connected tubing when it is disconnected too soon. This helps deliver the intended amount and reduces waste.
rows.imp_rat: Dose stranded by premature disconnection is a dosing/waste concern with negligible direct containment relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Premature-disconnection stranding is uncommon with attentive practice but occurs occasionally. Premature disconnection forces redo time. Completing transfer before disconnect depends on operator attention. Stranded dose portions waste drug.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a preparation record is linked to the wrong prepared dose

rows.sat_rat: Barcode and label systems make record-dose mislinks uncommon in modern practice. Linking record to dose adds a labeling step. Correct record-dose linkage depends on operator diligence. Mislinks may waste a dose or force rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prepared doses lacking a preparation record

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of preparation records with inaccurate drug identity, strength, concentration, or volume

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of preparation records with missing or inaccurate lot or expiration details

rows.imp_rat: Missing lot/expiration details is a record-accuracy defect tangential to the containment CFJ (relevance low) with documentation consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Lot and expiration capture is fairly reliable where scanning is used but manual entry still errs occasionally. Capturing lot/expiry adds entry time. Lot/expiration accuracy relies on manual transcription unless scanned. Little cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of containment releases or transfer deviations omitted from preparation records
rows.plain: Avoid leaving out instances where material escaped or moved between areas from the preparation records. Complete records help others understand exactly what happened and what was done.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from prepared-dose completion to preparation-record completion
rows.plain: Complete the preparation record as soon as possible after preparing a dose. This keeps the written record closely connected to the work that was actually done.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed preparation records altered without attributable change details

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of undissolved drug remaining after reconstitution

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous-drug transfers repeated because the initial transfer was incomplete

rows.sat_rat: CSTDs reduce failed transfers, so discard is occasional rather than routine. Discarding failed-transfer product is quick but the transfer must be repeated. Reducing discard depends on operator technique during the transfer. Discarded hazardous drug product is a direct and sometimes substantial material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous-drug transfer records containing missing or incorrect status data
rows.plain: Make sure records of transfers of dangerous medicines have complete and correct status information. This helps people know what has happened to each transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or incorrect status data in transfer records is a recordkeeping defect with negligible direct containment relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Manual transfer records still contain missing or incorrect status fields, though electronic batch records reduce this. Fixing missing status data adds rework time downstream. Accurate status entry depends on operator diligence in documentation. Record systems cost little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between completion of a hazardous-drug transfer and confirmation for subsequent floor work
rows.plain: Shorten the time between finishing a transfer of dangerous medicine and confirming that the worker can continue with other work on the production floor. This helps keep work moving safely.
rows.imp_rat: Delay confirming completion for subsequent work is a throughput concern with little bearing on release prevention.
rows.sat_rat: Completion confirmation is fairly routine but delayed inconsistently when reviewers are unavailable. Confirmation for subsequent floor work can lag when it depends on manual sign-off. Confirming completion requires a qualified reviewer's judgment. Confirmation steps cost little.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty while locating complete and traceable hazardous-drug transfer evidence requested by an auditor
rows.plain: Quickly locate complete records that show where and how hazardous medicine was transferred. This helps answer an auditor’s request with evidence that can be followed from start to finish.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty while locating audit evidence is a retrieval delay remote from preventing release (relevance low) with audit-timing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of composure lost when transfer records and personnel qualification evidence conflict during an audit
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to stay calm when movement records disagree with proof that the workers were trained during a review. This matters because the disagreement can raise questions about whether the work was done properly.
rows.imp_rat: Composure lost during audit evidence conflict is an emotional-state issue peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of visible unease when an auditor's transfer-control question cannot be answered promptly
rows.plain: The overseer wants to answer an auditor's question about how material is moved under control without visible worry or delay. Being ready with an answer helps the review go smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Visible unease during audit questioning is a demeanor concern, peripheral to vapor/aerosol containment; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of alarm when an auditor unexpectedly expands the hazardous-drug transfer evidence sample
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to reduce the chance of alarm or confusion if an auditor suddenly asks to review more records showing how dangerous medicines were moved. This helps the review proceed calmly and smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Alarm at expanded audit sample is an emotional response irrelevant to physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of visible composure loss when containment indications conflict during a live transfer
rows.plain: Avoid visibly losing confidence when different signs about containment disagree during a live transfer. This helps the overseer remain calm and guide the situation safely.
rows.imp_rat: Visible composure loss during a live transfer is an emotional-state issue not a containment mechanism (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while awaiting clarification of an unexpected containment concern
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries while waiting to understand an unexpected concern about keeping material safely contained. This helps them know that people, products, and the surrounding area remain protected.
rows.imp_rat: Emotional anxiety spikes while awaiting clarification do not directly control vapor/aerosol containment (relevance low) and cause only personal discomfort (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of calm lost while attention shifts among simultaneous live hazardous-drug transfers
rows.plain: Reduce the loss of calm when attention must move between several ongoing transfers of dangerous medicine. Clear control and monitoring help prevent mistakes or unwanted exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of calm across simultaneous transfers is an emotional-state issue not a containment control (relevance low) with only personal impact (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of composure lost when floor personnel provide conflicting accounts of a transfer deviation
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to stay calm when workers give different stories about a problem while material was being moved. This matters because a clear account is needed to understand what happened and what to do next.
rows.imp_rat: Composure lost over conflicting accounts is an emotional-state issue peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting confirmation of a suspected hazardous-drug transfer deviation
rows.plain: They want quick confirmation of whether something went wrong while a dangerous medicine was being moved. This helps the supervisor address any problem before it causes harm.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting deviation confirmation is a psychological-timing concern, essentially unrelated to physical release prevention; both proxies minimal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt after learning that a hazardous-drug transfer deviation surfaced later than expected
rows.plain: The person overseeing the work wants to stop doubting what happened after learning that a problem while moving a dangerous medicine was found later than expected. They need to understand the delay and know whether it was handled properly.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt after learning a deviation surfaced late is an emotional/assurance concern with negligible direct containment relevance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that auditors attribute a CSTD transfer-control finding to the job overseer's governance before site quality leadership
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that inspectors say the work overseer caused a problem with controls for moving dangerous medicines before the site’s quality leaders review the facts. This helps ensure responsibility is assigned fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution order of a finding to the overseer is a reputational/governance concern remote from containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with auditors when transfer records do not substantiate the job overseer's control
rows.plain: Avoid losing auditors’ trust when handover records do not prove that the person overseeing the work was in control. This matters because the records should clearly show who managed each step.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when records don't substantiate control is a reputational concern remote from containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of auditors second-guessing the job overseer's operator-qualification judgments
rows.plain: They want auditors to question their decisions about whether workers are properly trained and able to do the job less often. This matters because their judgments should be supported by clear, reliable evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Auditors second-guessing operator-qualification judgments is a reputational/authority concern remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality leadership bypasses the job overseer in transfer-governance decisions after an audit escalation
rows.plain: Avoid having quality leaders stop involving the supervisor in future decisions about moving work between people, equipment, or locations after an audit issue is raised. This helps preserve trust in the supervisor’s ability to guide those changes.
rows.imp_rat: Quality leadership bypassing the overseer after escalation is a governance/authority concern remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with role consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an operator exposure is attributed to the containment overseer's governance before plant leadership
rows.plain: The person overseeing containment wants to avoid being blamed by plant leaders if a worker is exposed to a hazard. Strong oversight should help keep workers safely separated from the hazard.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution order of an exposure to the overseer is a reputational/governance concern remote from containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with QA and EHS leaders after a hazardous vapor or aerosol release
rows.plain: They want quality and workplace-safety leaders to keep trusting them after a dangerous cloud or mist escapes. This matters because the response must show that people and the work area are being protected.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with QA/EHS after a release concerns reputation rather than the containment mechanism (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that auditors assign blame to the containment overseer for inadequate hazardous-drug transfer governance
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that inspectors blame the person overseeing the work because transfers of dangerous medicines were not properly controlled. This helps protect people and shows that the transfer process was responsibly managed.
rows.imp_rat: Auditors blaming the overseer for governance is a reputational concern remote from containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which production leadership bypasses the containment overseer in hazardous-drug transfer authorization decisions
rows.plain: Production leaders should go around the containment supervisor less often when deciding whether dangerous medicines may be transferred. This helps ensure those transfers are approved with proper safety oversight.
rows.imp_rat: Production leadership bypassing the overseer is a governance/authority concern remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with role consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which floor personnel publicly second-guess the containment overseer's judgments
rows.plain: Workers on the floor should publicly challenge the containment leader’s decisions less often. This helps people follow one clear plan for keeping dangerous material safely contained.
rows.imp_rat: Floor personnel second-guessing the overseer is a reputational/authority concern remote from containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable exposure-control requirements lacking documented governance evidence
rows.plain: Make sure there is written proof that the required rules for controlling people’s exposure to hazards are being managed. This shows that someone is overseeing these safety responsibilities.
rows.imp_rat: Missing governance evidence for exposure-control requirements is a documentation-completeness defect peripheral to the physical job (relevance low), with compliance-evidence consequences (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Many applicable requirements lack documented governance evidence, a frequent audit finding. Governance evidence gathering is periodic, not urgent. Documenting governance evidence requires quality-system literacy that varies. Documentation is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of exposure-control KPI results that cannot be substantiated by source records
rows.plain: Make sure reported results about controlling harmful exposure can be backed up by the original records. This makes it possible to confirm whether the controls are working.
rows.imp_rat: Unsubstantiated KPI results is a reporting-integrity defect remote from physical containment (relevance low) with credibility consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: KPI results often cannot be traced to source records, undermining trust in reported performance. Substantiation is not primarily a timing issue. Linking KPIs to source records requires data-governance discipline that is uneven. Substantiation tooling adds modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evidence cannot be traced from an exposure-control requirement to its control, owner, and result
rows.plain: Make it possible to follow each safety requirement from the danger it addresses to the safeguard, the person responsible, and the result. This shows that the safety program is being managed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable evidence from requirement to result is a traceability defect with low direct relevance to preventing release.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence often cannot be traced end-to-end from requirement to control, owner and result, weakening accountability. Traceability is not chiefly a speed issue at capture time. Maintaining requirement-to-control-to-result traceability requires quality-system discipline that varies. Traceability tooling adds modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exposure-control exceptions lacking documented ownership, disposition, or closure
rows.plain: Reduce problems involving exposure controls that have no recorded owner, decision, or proof that they were closed.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure-control exceptions lacking ownership or closure is an administrative-tracking defect, indirect to release and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure-control exceptions frequently lack documented ownership or closure, a common governance gap. Timing is secondary for exception tracking. Managing exception ownership and closure is administrative discipline, not deep skill. Tracking tools are low-cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to retrieve complete exposure-control governance evidence during an accountability review
rows.plain: Spend less time finding complete records showing how exposure risks are controlled and overseen during a review of responsibility.
rows.imp_rat: Retrieval time for governance evidence is an administrative-efficiency delay remote from preventing release (relevance low) with review-timing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because evidence is scattered, retrieval is slow and sometimes incomplete during accountability reviews. Retrieving complete governance evidence during a review is often slow, requiring manual assembly across systems. Efficient retrieval depends on records-management skill and system familiarity that varies. Document-management systems that speed retrieval are affordable but unevenly implemented.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of incident-scene evidence lost before the investigation begins
rows.plain: Avoid losing important information from the scene of an incident before someone can investigate it. Preserving what happened helps people understand the cause and respond correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Lost incident-scene evidence hampers investigation quality, not release prevention; both proxies are low for the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Transient airborne evidence is essentially lost before investigation, so this need is poorly served. Evidence degrades quickly (vapors dissipate, surfaces cleaned) before investigation starts. Preserving scene evidence requires forensic discipline rarely trained for HD incidents. Preservation is low-cost but requires foresight.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of incident records missing release location, event sequence, equipment state, or worker-activity details
rows.plain: Make sure reports about exposure incidents record where the release happened, what happened and when, the condition of the equipment, and what workers were doing. These details help people understand the cause and prevent it from happening again.
rows.imp_rat: Missing incident-record details is an administrative completeness error remote from live containment (relevance low), with consequences limited to weaker post-hoc analysis (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Incident records commonly miss location, sequence, equipment state or activity detail, degrading investigation value. Record completeness is about content, not speed. Capturing full incident detail needs trained investigators who know what to record. Better forms and capture are low-cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of exposure-control capacity consumed by redundant coverage at lower-risk points
rows.plain: Reduce the use of protective measures that are repeated at lower-risk points when they are not needed. This frees those resources for places where the risk is greater.
rows.imp_rat: Redundant coverage at low-risk points is efficiency waste, essentially unrelated to preventing hazardous release; both proxies minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant coverage rarely harms safety, so it is tolerated and seldom optimized. Redundant coverage is not a timing problem. Identifying redundancy needs risk-analysis skill to know what can safely be trimmed. The core loss is wasted exposure-control capacity spent on low-risk points.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while conflicting worker-exposure information remains unresolved
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while conflicting information about workers’ exposure remains unresolved. This matters because they need confidence that nobody is being exposed to danger.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while conflicting information is unresolved is an emotional-state issue peripheral to the job (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of lingering doubt after a worker-exposure concern is declared resolved
rows.plain: Quickly be certain that a concern about a worker being exposed to harm has truly been resolved. This protects people from assuming the danger is gone when it is not.
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt after a concern is declared resolved is a confidence ambiguity peripheral to the job (relevance low) with limited consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty about current exposure-control status while stakeholders await direction
rows.plain: People need to quickly know whether current steps to control the exposure are working while they wait for instructions. This helps them respond confidently instead of making decisions in the dark.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty about status while awaiting direction is a communication/emotional ambiguity peripheral to the job (relevance low) with limited consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when conflicting evidence challenges the working causal explanation
rows.plain: Stay calm when different pieces of evidence do not support the current explanation of what caused a problem. This helps you investigate fairly and change the explanation if needed.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over conflicting causal evidence is an emotional-state issue peripheral to the CFJ (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt about whether corrective actions justify investigation closure
rows.plain: The person overseeing the work wants to stop doubting whether the fixes are strong enough to close the investigation. They need to know the problem was properly addressed before declaring the review finished.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about investigation closure is a post-hoc confidence ambiguity peripheral to preventing release (relevance low) with limited consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to defend an exposure-control KPI during an inspection
rows.plain: The person wants to defend a key measure showing how well exposure to a hazard is being controlled during an inspection. This matters because inspectors need evidence that the controls work in practice.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to defend a KPI is an emotional-state issue peripheral to the CFJ (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when an inspector challenges exposure-control evidence
rows.plain: Stay calm when an inspector questions the proof that people and products were protected from harmful exposure. This helps you show that the safety measures are supported by facts.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when an inspector challenges evidence is an emotional-state issue remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty while reconciling conflicting exposure-control evidence for an inspector
rows.plain: The team needs to quickly resolve conflicting evidence about how exposure to contamination is controlled before explaining the situation to an inspector.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty while reconciling evidence for an inspector is an emotional/audit ambiguity remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that workers or senior leaders publicly attribute a hazardous-drug exposure exceedance to the overseer's stewardship
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that workers or senior leaders publicly blame the person overseeing the job if exposure to a dangerous medicine goes above the safe limit. This helps protect workers and confidence in the oversight.
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of an exposure exceedance to stewardship is reputational; though tied to exposure, ranking caps it at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which EHS leaders or regulators doubt the overseer's credibility when worker-protection evidence is challenged
rows.plain: Make safety leaders and government inspectors less likely to question the supervisor’s trustworthiness when evidence about protecting workers is challenged. This helps preserve confidence that worker safety is being handled responsibly.
rows.imp_rat: Overseer credibility doubts are reputational and administrative, not core to physical release prevention; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which facility leaders bypass the overseer in decisions affecting protection from hazardous-drug vapors and aerosols
rows.plain: Facility leaders should not go around the person responsible for decisions that protect workers from fumes and tiny airborne droplets released by dangerous medicines. Including this person helps keep workers safe.
rows.imp_rat: Bypassing the overseer in protection decisions is an organizational dynamics issue peripheral to physical containment; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulators or auditors publicly attribute inspection findings to the overseer's governance failure
rows.plain: The overseer wants to avoid having inspectors publicly say that poor management caused their findings. Strong oversight and clear records help show that problems were identified and handled properly.
rows.imp_rat: Regulators attributing findings to governance failure is a reputational/administrative concern, peripheral to physically preventing vapor/aerosol release; ranking caps it low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility the overseer loses with regulators or auditors when exposure-control evidence and accountable-party accounts conflict
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with government reviewers or auditors when proof of controlling exposure conflicts with what responsible people say happened. The evidence and each person's account should agree about who did what and how exposure was controlled.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with regulators over conflicting accounts is a reputational concern peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulators or auditors perceive the overseer as concealing or downplaying adverse exposure-control evidence
rows.plain: The overseer wants to avoid appearing to hide or minimize evidence that workers or products may have been exposed to contamination. Openly handling troubling evidence helps maintain trust.
rows.imp_rat: Perception of concealing adverse evidence is a reputational/integrity concern peripheral to containment (relevance low) with credibility consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration by which protocol execution exceeds the available purchase-evaluation window
rows.plain: Keep the testing process from running past the time available to evaluate the purchase. This helps the purchase decision stay on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding the purchase-evaluation window is a procurement-timing issue, unrelated to physical containment; both proxies minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Schedule overruns are common but at least predictable, and procurement often proceeds on vendor data to stay on time. Protocol execution frequently overruns the purchase-evaluation window because sensitive multi-run testing is slow. Compressing timelines without losing rigor requires experienced study management. Expediting evaluation costs more in overtime and parallel testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty when auditors question the documented containment criteria
rows.plain: People need to respond quickly when auditors question the written rules for containing a problem, with clear evidence that those rules are sound.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty when auditors question criteria is an emotional/communication ambiguity peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when reviewers challenge the interpretation of vapor and aerosol evidence
rows.plain: The recommender needs to stay calm when reviewers question what the evidence about airborne mist and vapor means. This helps them give a clear explanation of whether these airborne substances could create a problem.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over vapor/aerosol evidence interpretation is an emotional-state issue peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety episodes when auditors request support for CSTD evaluation conclusions
rows.plain: A recommender wants strong support for conclusions about equipment that helps transfer hazardous material safely when auditors ask for proof. Having the supporting information ready reduces worry during an audit.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety episodes when auditors request support is an emotional-state issue peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt about whether protocol assumptions are defensible under scrutiny
rows.plain: People should be confident that the assumptions used in the planned testing can be explained and supported if questioned. This makes the testing approach more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about defensibility of protocol assumptions is an assurance concern, indirect to release and low severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty when reviewers challenge the rationale for acceptance criteria
rows.plain: People need to quickly explain why the chosen pass-or-fail rules are appropriate when reviewers question them.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty when acceptance criteria are challenged is an emotional/communication ambiguity peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when an anomalous result cannot be reconciled with the evaluation conclusion
rows.plain: The buyer wants to avoid being caught off guard when an unusual result does not fit the final conclusion. This matters because the conclusion should explain the unusual result rather than leave a contradiction.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over an unreconciled result is an emotional-state issue peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to answer a safety-critical question about the recommended approach
rows.plain: The person wants to explain the recommended approach when asked an important safety question. This matters because decision-makers need to know that the approach will not put people or products at unnecessary risk.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to answer a question is an emotional-state issue peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulatory rejection of a containment conclusion damages the evaluator's credibility
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that regulators reject the conclusion that the containment system works. This helps protect the evaluator's professional reputation.
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory rejection damaging evaluator credibility is reputational, not a containment control (relevance low), with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior quality leadership blames the evaluator for a recommendation supported by insufficient containment evidence
rows.plain: The evaluator wants to avoid being blamed if a recommendation is made without enough proof that harmful material would be kept safely contained. Strong evidence is needed to make the recommendation trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Blame directed at the evaluator concerns reputational dynamics, not physical containment (relevance low), and its consequence is personal standing (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which reviewers attribute irreproducible containment findings to the evaluator's criteria
rows.plain: Reviewers should be less likely to blame the person's standards when tests for keeping contamination contained cannot be repeated with the same results. This helps separate problems with the testing from problems with the standards themselves.
rows.imp_rat: Irreproducible findings attributed to evaluator criteria is a reputational/analysis concern peripheral to the CFJ (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that leadership bypasses the evaluator because the containment criteria appear methodologically biased
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that leaders skip the evaluator because the rules used to judge containment appear unfair or one-sided. Clear and balanced rules help the evaluator's advice be taken seriously.
rows.imp_rat: Leadership bypassing the evaluator over perceived bias is a reputational/process concern peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an exposure-linked transfer endorsement is publicly attributed to the evaluator
rows.plain: The evaluator wants to avoid being publicly blamed if a decision to move equipment or materials exposes workers to danger. The evaluation should show that worker safety was considered.
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of an exposure-linked endorsement is a reputational concern remote from containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of exclusion from transfer decisions after worker-protection judgments are viewed as inconsistent
rows.plain: Avoid being left out of decisions about moving materials because others think judgments about worker safety are inconsistent. Reliable safety reasoning helps protect workers and keep the person involved.
rows.imp_rat: Exclusion from decisions after inconsistent judgments is a role/reputation concern peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of public blame for allowing commercial pressure to weaken the worker-exposure standard
rows.plain: Avoid being publicly blamed for letting business pressure weaken rules that protect workers from harmful exposure. Worker safety should not be sacrificed to meet commercial goals.
rows.imp_rat: Public blame for weakening the exposure standard is reputational, not a control mechanism (relevance low), with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among candidate containment evidence sources
rows.plain: The person influencing the purchase wants fewer unresolved disagreements between the different sources of proof used to judge ways of containing a problem. This makes it easier to compare the options fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among evidence sources is an analysis ambiguity downstream of containment (relevance low) affecting decision quality (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved conflicts among containment evidence sources are common. Reconciling evidence sources takes review time. Resolving conflicts among evidence sources requires expert judgement. Reconciliation is mainly labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of accepted candidate evidence lacking traceability to conditions samples and results
rows.plain: Make sure every piece of accepted proof can be connected to the conditions under which it was collected and to its results. This makes the proof trustworthy and easier to check.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence lacking traceability is a documentation-integrity defect remote from the physical job (relevance low) with audit-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Accepted evidence frequently lacks traceability to conditions, samples, and results. Traceability review adds time. Verifying traceability to conditions and samples requires diligent review. Traceability is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of qualification runs with false failures caused by background contamination or carryover
rows.plain: Avoid having approval tests fail because unwanted material from the surroundings or an earlier test gets into them. This saves time and prevents people from chasing problems that are not truly caused by the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: False failures from background contamination cause wasted rework, not exposure; both proxies are low for the containment CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: False failures from background contamination or carryover recur without standardized controls. Managing carryover and background requires extra cleaning time. Preventing background contamination requires careful lab technique. Blanks and cleaning add consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of personnel exposure incidents during vapor and aerosol containment challenges
rows.plain: They want to reduce how often workers are exposed to harmful vapors or fine sprays when containment is difficult. This helps keep people safe while the equipment is being tested.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure during containment challenges is directly tied to the CFJ, but ranking places it below the prior batch, capping at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established safe-handling practice and surrogate agents make exposure incidents during testing relatively rare. Safe challenge setup adds handling time. Preventing exposure during challenges relies on trained safe-handling practice. PPE, containment, and surrogate agents add cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of observed containment failures with incomplete records of conditions event sequence or anomalies
rows.plain: When the equipment fails to keep materials contained, record the conditions, the order of events, and anything unusual completely. This helps people understand what went wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete records of failure conditions is a documentation defect downstream of containment (relevance low) with analysis-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Failure records frequently lack complete condition, sequence, or anomaly detail. Recording conditions and event sequences adds documentation time. Complete records require disciplined, trained documentation. Record-keeping is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of observed containment failures with concurrent deviations preventing causal isolation
rows.plain: Reduce failures where several other problems happen at the same time and make it impossible to identify the true cause of a containment failure. Clearer failure information helps people fix the right problem.
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent deviations preventing causal isolation is an investigation-analysis ambiguity downstream of containment (relevance low) with root-cause-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Concurrent deviations frequently prevent causal isolation of failures. Isolating concurrent deviations adds investigation time. Separating concurrent deviations requires strong investigative skill. Additional isolating runs add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that failed components or connections are altered before causal examination
rows.plain: Keep failed parts and connections unchanged until they can be examined to find the cause of the failure. Altering them first could destroy important clues.
rows.imp_rat: Altering failed components before examination is an investigation-integrity defect downstream of the release (relevance low) with root-cause consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Failed components are often altered before causal examination, destroying evidence. Preserving failed components before examination needs prompt action. Proper preservation of failed items depends on disciplined, trained handling. Preservation adds minimal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting root-cause conclusions from the same containment-failure evidence
rows.plain: Avoid reaching different explanations for the same failure involving material escaping or moving where it should not. A shared understanding of the evidence helps people choose the right fix.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting root-cause conclusions is an analysis-consistency ambiguity downstream of containment (relevance low), affecting investigation quality not exposure (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting root-cause conclusions from identical evidence recur without standardized methods. Reconciling conflicting conclusions adds deliberation time. Consistent conclusions from the same evidence need disciplined analytical method. Conflict resolution is expert labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification conclusions lacking traceability to supporting results and acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure each conclusion about whether something is ready can be connected to the results and rules used to judge it. This makes the decision easier to verify and trust.
rows.imp_rat: Conclusions lacking traceability to criteria is a qualification-documentation defect downstream of containment (relevance low) with audit-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Conclusions often lack traceability to results and acceptance criteria. Linking conclusions to results adds review time. Tracing conclusions to supporting data needs careful review. Traceability is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved discrepancies among observations calculations results and qualification conclusions
rows.plain: Make sure the observations, calculations, results, and final conclusions all agree. This makes the proof of successful testing easier to trust.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies among observations and conclusions is an analysis-consistency defect downstream of containment (relevance low) affecting report quality (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Discrepancies among observations, calculations, and conclusions frequently go unresolved. Reconciling discrepancies extends review time. Detecting discrepancies among data and conclusions needs careful review. Reconciliation is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of test deviations or failed runs without a documented impact assessment
rows.plain: The person influencing the purchase wants every unusual test result or failed test run to include a written review of what it affected. This shows whether the problem could have changed the overall conclusion.
rows.imp_rat: Failed runs without impact assessment is a qualification-documentation defect downstream of containment (relevance low) with analysis-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deviations and failed runs often lack documented impact assessments, a known quality gap. Impact assessments add documentation time. Assessing deviation impact needs quality expertise. Assessment is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification conclusions rely on criteria defined or changed after testing
rows.plain: Avoid reaching conclusions from testing by using rules that were created or changed only after the testing was finished. This helps ensure the results are judged fairly and were not tailored afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification relying on post-test criteria is an evidence-integrity concern peripheral to physical containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Post hoc criteria changes still occur without strict pre-registration discipline, undermining conclusions. Locking criteria before testing is an upfront act, not a delay. Pre-specifying criteria requires disciplined protocol governance. Criteria pre-definition is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of evidence records with unverified origin authorship date or revision status
rows.plain: Make sure each piece of evidence has a confirmed source, author, date, and current version. This helps people know the information is genuine and up to date.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence records with unverified origin is a documentation-integrity ambiguity downstream of containment (relevance low) with audit-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity practices reasonably address origin, authorship, and revision verification in regulated settings. Verifying record provenance adds review time. Confirming authorship and revision status requires records discipline. Data-integrity controls carry cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification records that are untraceable altered or missing
rows.plain: Keep checking records from being changed without a clear history, lost, or unavailable. Reliable records are needed to prove what was checked and what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable or altered qualification records is a documentation-integrity defect downstream of containment (relevance low) with audit-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Under GMP document-control practice, untraceable or altered records are comparatively well managed though not eliminated. Records-management checks add time. Maintaining traceable, unaltered records requires quality-system discipline. Document control systems carry cost but are common.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for complete qualification evidence before the recommendation deadline
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for proof that the systems meet the requirements before it is time to recommend one. This gives the decision-maker the information needed to make a timely recommendation.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for qualification evidence before deadline is a process delay peripheral to physical containment (relevance low) with schedule consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Late-arriving evidence forces decisions on incomplete data, but the core loss felt is schedule delay. Waiting for complete qualification evidence before a deadline is a chronic delay driven by long test cycles. Accelerating evidence delivery depends on scarce testing capacity. Expedited testing raises cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that qualification evidence represents intended sterile fill-finish use
rows.plain: Quickly confirm that the available proof shows the equipment or process is suitable for making sterile medicine and putting it into its final container. This helps people feel confident before they rely on it.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that evidence represents intended use is a confidence ambiguity tangential to vapor/aerosol control (relevance low) with limited consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while containment-evidence gaps or conflicts remain unresolved
rows.plain: The buyer wants missing or conflicting proof about keeping the contents contained to be resolved. This matters so the decision is based on dependable information.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while evidence gaps persist is a stress concern peripheral to physical release prevention; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when a containment claim cannot be linked to supporting evidence
rows.plain: The buyer wants to avoid being caught off guard if a claim that a problem was contained cannot be connected to proof. This matters because the claim should be supported by records or other reliable information.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over unlinked claims is an emotional concern peripheral to release prevention; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty when reconciling containment claims with qualified conditions
rows.plain: Make it quick and easy to show that claims about keeping harmful material contained match the conditions that were properly tested. This reassures reviewers that the claims are realistic and supported.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty reconciling claims is a demeanor/decision concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure under questioning about qualification criteria and evidence
rows.plain: Stay calm when someone asks why the product or service meets the required standards and what proof supports that claim. This helps you give a clear, convincing answer.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure under questioning is a poise concern irrelevant to physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt triggered by conflicting judgments about whether evidence demonstrates containment
rows.plain: Avoid uncertainty when people disagree about whether the evidence proves that harmful material was kept contained. Clear proof helps the decision withstand close questioning.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting containment judgments is a decision-confidence concern, not physical release prevention; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether containment evidence justifies the recommendation
rows.plain: Know quickly whether the proof that problems are contained is strong enough to support the recommendation. This helps the person making the recommendation explain and defend it with confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty about evidence justification is an emotional/decision concern peripheral to the core containment job; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting information needed to support the recommendation
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while waiting for the information needed to support their recommendation. This matters because they need to make a decision without unanswered doubts.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting recommendation support is a stress concern, not central to containment; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt caused by conflicting interpretations of qualified results before recommendation
rows.plain: The buyer needs a clear understanding of which test results meet the requirements before making a recommendation. Conflicting explanations of the results could lead to the wrong choice.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting result interpretations is an analysis-confidence ambiguity peripheral to the CFJ (relevance low) with decision-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulatory objections are attributed to weak containment qualification protocol design
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that regulators object because the plan for proving the containment system works was poorly designed. This protects the person's reputation for making sound recommendations.
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory objections attributed to protocol design is a reputational/process concern, not physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which cross-functional reviewers second-guess the containment acceptance criteria
rows.plain: People from different teams should not repeatedly doubt the standards used to decide whether a containment system works. Clear, trusted standards help everyone agree on whether it is safe and effective.
rows.imp_rat: Reviewers second-guessing acceptance criteria is a review-friction concern, not physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with regulatory reviewers when cited containment evidence is challenged
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with regulatory reviewers when evidence said to prove that harmful material is contained is questioned. The evidence should clearly show that the containment works as claimed.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss with regulators on evidence is reputational, not physical containment; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exclusion from future transfer-system qualification decisions after a challenged recommendation
rows.plain: Remain involved in future decisions about testing the material-transfer system, even if someone challenges a recommendation. A well-supported recommendation helps preserve that authority.
rows.imp_rat: Exclusion from future decisions is an org-dynamics/reputational concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unrecognized containment-evidence gap is attributed to weak evaluation rigor
rows.plain: The evaluator wants to avoid being blamed for missing a gap in the proof that a containment system is safe and effective. A careful review should reveal missing information before the purchase decision.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of an evidence gap to weak rigor is a reputational/process concern, not physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which EHS or Quality challenges the safety judgment because qualification evidence appears contradictory
rows.plain: The advisor wants safety and quality teams to stop challenging their safety decisions because the supporting proof seems to disagree with itself. Consistent proof would make the decision more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: EHS/Quality challenging safety judgment is a review-friction concern peripheral to physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which Engineering or Validation second-guesses transfer-system implementation assumptions during capital review
rows.plain: Provide enough reliable information about how the transfer system will be put in place that engineering and testing reviewers do not need to repeatedly question the plan during the funding review. This helps the investment decision move forward with confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Engineering second-guessing implementation assumptions is a review-friction concern peripheral to containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that capital decision-makers bypass the recommendation because the evaluation appears biased
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that decision-makers ignore the recommendation because they think the comparison was unfair or favored one option. This matters because a useful solution may be rejected for the wrong reason.
rows.imp_rat: Decision-makers bypassing a biased-appearing evaluation is a governance-credibility concern, not physical containment; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a missed evidence-readiness commitment damages credibility with capital decision-makers
rows.plain: The needed proof should be ready as promised so the person influencing the purchase does not lose the trust of people deciding how to invest money. Missing it could make them question the safety of the decision.
rows.imp_rat: Missed evidence-readiness commitment damaging credibility is a reputational concern, not physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supporting evidence with incomplete traceability to transfer conditions, materials, and deviations
rows.plain: Make sure the evidence can be connected to the conditions of transfer, the materials involved, and any problems that occurred. This helps people judge the risk of unwanted material entering the product.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence with incomplete traceability is an assurance gap with low direct relevance to preventing vapor/aerosol release.
rows.sat_rat: Supporting-evidence traceability is generally good but incomplete linkage to conditions and materials persists. Building full traceability adds documentation effort. Evidence traceability relies on standard document-control skill. Traceability systems are a routine cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validation deviations lacking documented impact assessment and disposition
rows.plain: Make sure every departure from the testing plan has a written assessment of its effect and a recorded decision about what to do. This helps show whether the test results can still be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Validation deviations lacking impact assessment is a documentation-control gap, minimally relevant to preventing release.
rows.sat_rat: GMP deviation systems reliably document and disposition most deviations, making this fairly well served. Deviation disposition adds QA processing time. Deviation handling is a mature QA competency broadly available. Deviation management is a routine cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required GMP validation records that are incomplete, unapproved, or untraceable to source data
rows.plain: Make sure all required records proving that a testing method works are complete, approved, and linked back to the original information. This makes the results trustworthy and reviewable.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete GMP validation records is a documentation-quality defect, minimally relevant to physical release prevention.
rows.sat_rat: Mature GMP documentation practices keep most validation records complete and traceable, one of the better-served needs. Record completion and approval add documentation time. GMP record-keeping is a well-established competency with QA support. Documentation overhead is a normal budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve evidence gaps and issue the suitability recommendation
rows.plain: Spend less time filling evidence gaps and deciding whether a method is suitable so a clear recommendation can be made.
rows.imp_rat: Time to resolve evidence gaps and issue a recommendation is a project-timing matter, indirect to release and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: The recommendation timeline is unpredictable because gap resolution depends on ad hoc back-and-forth with vendors. Resolving evidence gaps and issuing the recommendation is a slow, iterative process with no streamlined path. Closing gaps efficiently depends on scarce experienced reviewers who can prioritize what matters. Prolonged resolution delays procurement decisions and ties up resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility findings invalidated by unrelated environmental or handling contamination
rows.plain: Prevent test results showing that a product stayed germ-free from being wrongly rejected because of contamination from the surroundings or from handling. This helps separate real product problems from unrelated contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility findings invalidated by unrelated contamination is a measurement-confounding issue tied to sterility, not hazardous-vapor containment.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental-monitoring and investigation practices reasonably control false-positive sterility findings. Investigating and invalidating false positives adds delay. Distinguishing unrelated contamination uses established microbiology investigation skill. Reruns of invalidated tests add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in sterility-maintenance acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make the rules for deciding whether sterility has been preserved clear. This helps people judge consistently whether the process kept the product free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility-criteria ambiguity relates to product quality more than hazardous-vapor containment, so relevance and severity to the CFJ are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Compendial sterility criteria are relatively unambiguous, making this comparatively well served. Clarifying sterility criteria adds modest protocol time. Sterility acceptance criteria draw on mature compendial standards requiring less novel judgement. Well-defined criteria minimize rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of verification samples or findings lacking traceability to transfer conditions
rows.plain: Make sure fewer test samples or findings lose their connection to the conditions under which they were moved. This helps confirm that sterility was kept during transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Findings lacking traceability to conditions is an evidence-quality gap with low direct relevance to release prevention.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability of samples to conditions is generally maintained but gaps still occur. Establishing traceability adds documentation effort. Sample traceability uses standard GMP documentation practice. Traceability systems are a normal recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty when tracing the recommendation to containment, sterility, and GMP evidence under auditor questioning
rows.plain: People need to quickly show how the recommendation is supported by evidence about containing problems, keeping products sterile, and following required medicine-making rules when an auditor asks.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty tracing recommendation to evidence is an audit-communication delay peripheral to containment (relevance low) with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt triggered by conflicting containment, sterility, and GMP evidence during scrutiny of the recommendation
rows.plain: Reduce uncertainty caused by conflicting evidence about keeping harmful material contained, preventing germs, and following required manufacturing practices. This helps the recommendation remain credible during inspection or review.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting evidence during scrutiny is an evaluator-experience issue remote from preventing release (relevance low) with limited practical consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when an auditor challenges an assumption behind the recommendation
rows.plain: Stay calm when an auditor questions an assumption behind your recommendation. This helps you explain what the recommendation is based on and whether it should change.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure under audit is a personal-poise concern irrelevant to physical containment performance; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting clarification of containment, sterility, or GMP evidence
rows.plain: Reduce the worry that continues while waiting for clear answers about evidence of containment, sterility, or required manufacturing practices. Clear answers help the organization prepare for inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting evidence clarification is an emotional concern, not central to the containment CFJ; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that doubt about an unexamined contamination pathway persists through sign-off
rows.plain: Make sure concerns about a possible way contamination could enter are resolved before the work is approved. This helps people feel confident that the process is safe.
rows.imp_rat: Persisting doubt about an unexamined pathway is an evaluator-confidence ambiguity tangential to the physical job (relevance low) with limited consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of losing calm when containment and sterility evidence conflict
rows.plain: Make it easier to stay calm when signs that the area is protected do not agree with signs that it is free of contamination. This helps people respond carefully instead of reacting in panic.
rows.imp_rat: Losing calm when evidence conflicts is a composure concern irrelevant to physical containment; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence needed to resolve a sterility concern
rows.plain: The person wants the records needed to settle a concern that the product or process may no longer be free from harmful germs. This helps determine whether it stayed safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting sterility evidence is an emotional concern peripheral to the containment CFJ; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reassurance collapses when contamination-risk assumptions change near sign-off
rows.plain: The adviser wants safety assurances to remain trustworthy even if beliefs about the risk of contamination change shortly before approval. The reasoning should be strong enough to handle new information.
rows.imp_rat: Reassurance collapsing near sign-off is an emotional-state ambiguity peripheral to the physical job (relevance low) with limited consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt arising from conflicting operator-exposure and GMP evidence during evaluation
rows.plain: Conflicting evidence about worker exposure and required manufacturing safety and quality rules should not leave the evaluation uncertain. This helps protect workers and supports a sound purchase decision.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting exposure/GMP evidence during evaluation is an evaluator-experience concern peripheral to the CFJ (relevance low) with limited consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of losing composure when stakeholders challenge the operator-safety rationale behind acceptance criteria
rows.plain: They want to stay calm when others question why the buying requirements are meant to protect workers. This helps keep important safety protections in place.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when rationale is challenged is a poise concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence needed to resolve operator-safety concerns before finalizing acceptance criteria
rows.plain: The person wants evidence needed to address worker-safety concerns before agreeing on how success will be judged. This helps ensure the final requirements protect the people doing the work.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting safety evidence is an emotional concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterility or hazardous-drug containment lapse is publicly attributed to the influencer's evaluation criteria
rows.plain: The person choosing the equipment wants to avoid being blamed publicly if cleanliness or dangerous-drug containment fails because of the standards they used to judge it. This matters because their evaluation could affect their reputation and accountability.
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of a lapse to the influencer's criteria is reputational, not a containment control (relevance low), with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with validation and microbiology reviewers when the recommendation fails sterility or containment scrutiny
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with testing and germ-control reviewers when a recommendation cannot withstand questions about preventing germs or containing harmful material. The recommendation should show that it protects both the product and the people handling it.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with reviewers under scrutiny is reputational, not a control mechanism (relevance low), with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which QA or validation leaders exclude the influencer from establishing or approving transfer-method criteria
rows.plain: The buyer wants quality leaders who check whether processes work properly to include them when setting or approving the rules for moving material. Being included helps ensure those rules reflect the need to keep the material safely contained.
rows.imp_rat: Exclusion from setting transfer-method criteria is a role/authority concern remote from the mechanism (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that QA and the selection team perceive the recommendation as commercially biased rather than grounded in sterility and containment protection
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that quality reviewers and the selection team think the recommendation is driven by price or business interests instead of protecting the product from germs and leaks. The reason for the choice should clearly focus on keeping the product safely contained and clean.
rows.imp_rat: Perceived commercial bias in the recommendation is a reputational concern remote from containment (relevance low) with credibility consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that auditors publicly attribute a GMP objection to the influencer's transfer-method judgment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that inspectors publicly blame the person advising on the purchase for objecting to a transfer method. This helps show that the recommendation was based on sound judgment rather than personal error.
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of a GMP objection to the influencer is reputational, not a control (relevance low), with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with quality, regulatory, and customer auditors when the recommendation is challenged
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with quality managers, government reviewers, and customers when a recommendation is questioned. The recommendation should be supported well enough to explain and defend clearly.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with auditors when challenged is reputational, not a control mechanism (relevance low), with personal consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that auditors perceive GMP evaluation criteria as inconsistently applied across transfer-method recommendations
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that inspectors see the rules for judging manufacturing quality as being used differently for different transfer methods. This helps show that each option was judged by the same standards.
rows.imp_rat: Perceived inconsistent GMP criteria across recommendations is a reputational/methodology concern remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with credibility consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which quality leadership publicly overrules the recommendation during auditor scrutiny
rows.plain: Quality managers should be less likely to publicly overrule the person's recommendation during an inspection. This helps the person appear trustworthy and makes decisions seem consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Quality leadership publicly overruling the recommendation is a reputational/process concern remote from the mechanism (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being excluded from later transfer-method decisions after an audit challenge
rows.plain: The person wants to stay involved in later decisions about how materials are moved after an inspection challenge. They want their judgment to remain trusted even when someone questions the approach.
rows.imp_rat: Exclusion from later decisions after a challenge is a role/reputation concern remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with personal-standing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the total-cost justification for the CSTD acquisition
rows.plain: Make the explanation of the protective device’s full cost over its lifetime as reliable as possible. This helps the buying team decide quickly and confidently.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in total-cost justification is a financial-analysis ambiguity peripheral to the CFJ (relevance low) with only decision-quality consequences (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: TCO inputs for CSTDs are speculative and inconsistently sourced, leaving justifications highly uncertain. Building a defensible total-cost justification takes considerable analytical time gathering inputs. Total-cost modeling for CSTDs requires financial and operational expertise that procurement often lacks. Uncertain cost justification risks over- or under-investment and weakens negotiating leverage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time purchase authorization remains pending after financial-case submission
rows.plain: Make it faster to approve a purchase after the financial explanation has been submitted. This helps the agreement with the supplier start sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Purchase authorization pending time is a procurement-cycle concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Approval timing is inconsistent depending on leadership availability. Authorization frequently lingers after case submission, directly extending the cycle. Expediting approvals depends on relationship and process navigation skill. Pending authorization delays savings and can miss pricing windows.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in scope, assumptions, and commercial terms across CSTD supplier proposals
rows.plain: Make supplier offers use the same description of what is included, the same basic assumptions, and comparable business terms. This helps the buyer compare offers quickly and agree on a contract sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Variation across supplier proposals is a procurement-comparison ambiguity remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with evaluation-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers respond with inconsistent assumptions and terms, so like-for-like comparison is chronically difficult. Normalizing divergent proposals into comparable form is a slow manual effort. Reconciling varied scopes and commercial terms requires skilled sourcing analysis. Proposal variation obscures true price differences and can lead to costlier selections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of CSTD acquisition approvals without a designated decision owner or sequence
rows.plain: Each approval to buy special equipment for safely handling dangerous medicines should have one clearly named decision-maker and a clear order of steps. This prevents approvals from stalling or being handled without accountability.
rows.imp_rat: Approvals lacking a decision owner is a governance-process gap peripheral to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Approval owners and sequences are often undefined, making sign-off unpredictable. Undefined approval ownership causes stalled routing and delay. Establishing decision governance requires organizational process skill. Approval ambiguity delays value capture with minor direct cost.
