rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of CSTD requirement changes that trigger proposal, financial-case, or contract revisions
rows.plain: Reduce how often requirements for a device that safely moves materials between containers change after proposals, financial plans, or contracts have been prepared. This helps avoid repeated revisions and speeds up the agreement.
rows.imp_rat: Requirement changes triggering revisions is a rework/process concern peripheral to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements churn frequently mid-process, destabilizing the whole acquisition. Requirement changes force rework of proposals and contracts, adding rounds. Managing requirement stability needs disciplined change control. Each change triggers costly revision cycles.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unresolved negotiation over CSTD pricing, liability, supply obligations, and acceptance terms
rows.plain: Reach agreement quickly on the price, responsibilities, supply promises, and conditions for accepting the equipment. This allows the contract to be signed and the purchase to move ahead sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved negotiation over terms is a procurement delay remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with acquisition-timing consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Negotiation duration is unpredictable given complex CSTD liability and containment terms. Negotiation over pricing, liability, and acceptance terms frequently stalls for extended periods, delaying contracts. Efficient negotiation demands experienced commercial and legal skill. Prolonged negotiation delays savings realization and consumes staff resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that proposed CSTD acquisition cost exceeds the authorized funding ceiling
rows.plain: Avoid choosing the proposed drug-handling equipment if its purchase price would go over the approved budget. This helps keep the purchase within the money that has been authorized.
rows.imp_rat: Acquisition cost exceeding the funding ceiling is a financial-constraint error not tied to preventing release (relevance low) with budgetary consequence only (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Budget ceilings are known upfront but cost estimates remain uncertain, so overruns are a recurring possibility. Aligning proposals to the funding ceiling can require several costing iterations. Estimating whether costs fit the ceiling requires budgeting competence that is generally available. Exceeding the authorized ceiling forces re-scoping or reapproval, the core financial risk here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that CSTD supplier qualification or quality-agreement gaps are discovered late in contracting
rows.plain: Avoid discovering late that the supplier of closed-transfer devices has not passed the required checks or that the quality responsibilities are unclear. This helps the contract move forward without last-minute problems.
rows.imp_rat: Late discovery of supplier-qualification gaps is a contracting-timing risk, unrelated to physical containment; both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard supplier qualification workflows exist but are inconsistently sequenced against contracting, so late surprises remain common. Supplier qualification and quality-agreement reviews are often deferred until late in contracting, compressing timelines when issues appear. Detecting qualification gaps early requires experienced quality auditors whose bandwidth is limited during procurement. Late-discovered gaps trigger expensive contract renegotiation and delayed launches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of financially material CSTD lifecycle costs omitted from the business case
rows.plain: Include as much as possible of the major costs of owning and using the equipment over its full life in the business plan. This helps show the true amount the company will need to spend.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting lifecycle costs from the business case is a financial-analysis defect not tied to release prevention (relevance low) with decision-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Business cases routinely miss consumable, disposal, and support costs because no standard CSTD cost taxonomy is applied. Enumerating all lifecycle costs takes time but is bounded once a checklist exists. Identifying material lifecycle cost categories needs experienced cost analysts. Omitted lifecycle costs understate true spend and distort the business case financially.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of claimed CSTD financial benefits that lack defensible evidence
rows.plain: Reduce the share of claimed financial savings from the protective equipment that cannot be supported by reliable proof. This makes the financial case more believable.
rows.imp_rat: Claimed financial benefits lacking evidence is a business-case ambiguity remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with decision-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD benefit claims frequently rest on vendor assertions without defensible evidence. Substantiating claimed benefits with evidence takes prolonged data-gathering. Validating financial benefit claims requires analytical rigor often lacking in procurement. Unsupported benefits overstate ROI and risk poor investment decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between projected and actual CSTD component consumption in sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: Make sure the predicted use of closed drug-transfer parts is close to actual use in sterile medicine production. This makes the expected financial benefits and costs more reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Consumption deviation in fill-finish is a planning concern peripheral to the containment CFJ; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Actual fill-finish consumption often diverges from projections. Tracking fill-finish consumption against projections requires ongoing monitoring time. Production consumption forecasting needs manufacturing-planning expertise. Deviation drives inventory waste or shortfall costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among CSTD baseline cost inputs from finance, procurement, quality, safety, and manufacturing stakeholders
rows.plain: Make sure the finance, purchasing, quality, safety, and manufacturing teams use matching starting cost figures for the protective transfer equipment. This helps everyone build the financial case from the same information.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent baseline cost inputs are a financial-alignment issue peripheral to preventing release; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Finance, quality, safety, and manufacturing supply divergent baseline inputs with no reconciliation standard. Reconciling baseline inputs across five stakeholder groups is slow. Aligning cross-functional cost inputs requires coordination and financial skill. Inconsistent baselines produce unreliable cost figures and flawed decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in cost scope and evaluation horizons across CSTD acquisition alternatives
rows.plain: Compare each closed-system transfer option using the same cost coverage and time period. This gives a fair picture of what each option will cost.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in cost scope across alternatives is a procurement-analysis ambiguity not tied to containment (relevance low) with decision-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alternatives are frequently compared on mismatched horizons and scopes, producing unreliable rankings. Normalizing cost scope and horizons across alternatives adds analytical effort each comparison. Ensuring comparable evaluation horizons requires disciplined financial-analysis skill. Inconsistent scope leads to poor alternative selection and wasted spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of projected CSTD savings counted more than once across labor, waste, exposure, and contamination categories
rows.plain: Avoid counting the same expected savings from closed transfer devices more than once—for example, as labor, waste, safety exposure, and contamination savings. This keeps the financial estimate honest.
rows.imp_rat: Double-counted projected savings is a financial-analysis defect remote from the mechanism (relevance low) with business-case-accuracy consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Overlapping labor, waste, and exposure savings are commonly double-counted with no standard guard against it. Untangling double-counted savings requires careful reconciliation time. Detecting double-counting across categories needs financial-modeling discipline. Double-counted savings inflate the business case and misrepresent true cost benefit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of lifecycle-cost distortion caused by incorrect cash-flow timing for acquisition, implementation, operating costs, and avoided events
rows.plain: Use the correct timing for purchase, setup, running costs, and costs avoided by preventing problems. This prevents the investment’s long-term cost from being shown inaccurately.
rows.imp_rat: Cash-flow timing distortion is a financial-modeling error unrelated to containment; ranking caps it at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Cash-flow timing for acquisition and avoided events is commonly mishandled, distorting results. Correctly phasing cash flows adds modeling time. Proper cash-flow timing requires specialized financial-analysis skill. Mis-timed cash flows distort lifecycle-cost and NPV conclusions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material CSTD financial assumptions lacking documented support
rows.plain: Reduce the number of money estimates for equipment that safely transfers medicines from one container to another without direct contact that lack written evidence. This makes the financial case for the equipment more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Undocumented financial assumptions are an administrative rigor issue, not central to preventing hazardous drug release; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Many CSTD financial assumptions go undocumented, undermining case reliability. Documenting support for assumptions adds analytical effort. Sourcing defensible assumption support requires analytical expertise. Unsupported assumptions weaken cost credibility and risk misinvestment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that plausible changes in production volume, CSTD pricing, or implementation schedule reverse the acquisition justification
rows.plain: The buyer wants the purchase to remain financially worthwhile even if production levels, the price of protective transfer equipment, or the schedule changes. This matters because a small change in assumptions should not turn a justified purchase into a poor investment.
rows.imp_rat: Sensitivity of justification to volume/price changes is a business-case robustness issue peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Justifications are rarely stress-tested, so plausible changes can silently reverse them. Running scenario sensitivity adds analytical time upfront. Sensitivity analysis requires modeling expertise not always applied. Fragile justifications that reverse under volume or price shifts risk stranded investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to obtain validated CSTD cost and usage inputs before the budget approval deadline
rows.plain: Make it faster to obtain checked, reliable information about the cost and use of equipment that keeps dangerous medicines contained during transfer before the budget deadline. This supports a sound funding decision.
rows.imp_rat: Time to obtain validated cost inputs is a budgeting-cycle concern unrelated to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Input validation timelines are unpredictable and often miss deadlines. Gathering validated cost and usage inputs before budget deadlines is a recurring time crunch. Producing validated inputs quickly requires cross-functional data skill. Late inputs risk missed budget cycles and deferred investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required CSTD units unavailable because supplier output falls below contracted demand
rows.plain: Make sure enough protective transfer units are available when needed, even if the supplier is struggling to make the agreed quantity. This helps prevent work from stopping because supplies run out.
rows.imp_rat: CSTD units unavailable due to supplier shortfall is a supply-availability concern remote from the mechanism (relevance low) with operational consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier output can fall below contracted demand and enforcement is weak, so availability is not assured. Shortfalls against contracted demand emerge with little warning, forcing reactive scrambling. Anticipating output shortfalls requires supplier-capacity assessment expertise. Unavailable units cause production loss and expedite costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that CSTD replenishment arrives after available inventory is exhausted
rows.plain: Avoid replacement closed-transfer devices arriving only after the available supply has run out. This helps prevent work from stopping because essential protective devices are missing.
rows.imp_rat: Replenishment arriving after inventory exhausted is a supply-timing delay remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with operational-availability consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Lead-time variability makes replenishment arrival timing unreliable, causing periodic exhaustion. Replenishment timing versus inventory runout is a core scheduling risk poorly buffered for specialized CSTDs. Timing reorders against variable lead times needs planning expertise not always applied to CSTDs. Stockouts trigger expedite and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which forecast CSTD demand understates actual sterile fill-finish consumption
rows.plain: Make sure the expected need for supplies used in sterile production is not lower than the amount actually needed. Underestimating demand could cause shortages.
rows.imp_rat: Demand understatement is a forecasting concern unrelated to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Forecasts tend to understate real fill-finish consumption fairly consistently. Correcting understated demand forces urgent reorders costing time. Avoiding forecast understatement requires disciplined demand estimation. Understated demand causes stockouts and emergency premium purchases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of CSTD shortages caused by imbalanced inventories across required configurations
rows.plain: Avoid running short of the different equipment setups that are needed. Keeping the right balance of supplies helps prevent delays.
rows.imp_rat: CSTD shortages from imbalanced inventory is a supply concern peripheral to the containment CFJ; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration-level imbalance is common and causes avoidable shortages. Rebalancing configuration inventories after a shortage takes reactive time. Balancing multi-configuration inventory requires planning expertise. Imbalanced stock causes shortages of some configs while others expire, wasting cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in supplier obligations for allocating CSTD supply during shortages
rows.plain: Make it clear what the supplier must do to distribute the closed drug-transfer equipment fairly when supplies are limited. This helps the organization plan for shortages and avoid unclear promises.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in supplier allocation obligations is a contractual concern remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with supply-risk consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Allocation terms are often vague or absent, leaving supply certainty during shortages low. Clarifying allocation obligations during shortages drags through negotiation cycles. Drafting robust allocation clauses requires contracting expertise. Ambiguous allocation exposes the buyer to costly last-minute sourcing during shortages.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of CSTD inventory lost to expiry, damage, or compromised package integrity before use
rows.plain: Reduce supplies that become unusable because they expire, are damaged, or their packaging is no longer safely sealed. This prevents wasted stock and helps ensure usable supplies are available when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Inventory lost to expiry/damage is a supply-waste concern peripheral to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: FEFO and integrity checks are established but expiry and damage losses still occur. Managing shelf-life and rotation to avoid expiry is a routine ongoing task. Standard inventory rotation practices require modest planning skill. Expired, damaged, or compromised inventory is a direct write-off cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in supplier lead times for contracted CSTD replenishment
rows.plain: Keep suppliers' delivery times for replacement supplies consistent. Reliable delivery helps prevent shortages and interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in supplier lead times is a supply-chain variability concern remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with availability consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contracted lead times still fluctuate, making replenishment inconsistent. Lead-time variation directly lengthens and destabilizes planning cycles. Managing lead-time variance requires planning skill that is generally available. Variable lead times force safety-stock carrying costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in lifecycle cost scope across CSTD acquisition options
rows.plain: Include the same kinds of costs over the full life of every closed-system transfer option. This makes the total purchase costs genuinely comparable.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in lifecycle cost scope across options is a procurement-analysis ambiguity remote from the CFJ (relevance low) with comparison-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a standard lifecycle template, option cost scopes vary and comparisons stay unreliable. Harmonizing lifecycle cost scope across options consumes review time. Consistent lifecycle scoping demands cost-engineering discipline not always present. Scope inconsistency skews which option looks cheapest, risking misallocated funds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between forecast and actual CSTD component consumption over the expected lifecycle
rows.plain: Make sure the predicted use of the closed drug-transfer parts is close to how many will actually be used over the equipment's working life. This helps keep the total purchase and operating cost from being higher than expected.
rows.imp_rat: Forecast-vs-actual consumption deviation is a demand-planning concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle consumption forecasts routinely deviate from actuals for consumables. Reconciling forecast versus actual consumption is a periodic review effort. Accurate consumption forecasting requires demand-planning skill. Consumption deviation causes over- or under-buying with cost impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of drug product lost during CSTD transfers over the expected lifecycle
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of medicine that is wasted while it is moved through the special transfer equipment during its expected use. This saves product and money.
rows.imp_rat: Drug product lost during transfers is a yield/waste concern peripheral to vapor/aerosol containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer losses vary by device and are inconsistently measured. Quantifying product loss during transfers requires measurement effort over time. Estimating transfer losses needs process characterization skill. Drug product lost in transfers is a direct and often high-value waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of ancillary component, testing, and requalification cost caused by CSTD incompatibility with fill-finish materials and transfer interfaces
rows.plain: Avoid extra costs for parts, tests, and repeat checks when the protective containment equipment does not work with the materials and connection points used to fill and move the product. This keeps the purchase from requiring expensive adjustments.
rows.imp_rat: Ancillary cost from incompatibility is a financial/logistics burden, essentially unrelated to hazardous-vapor containment; both proxies minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility data is patchy and interface standards vary, so incompatibility surprises recur despite pre-purchase review. Compatibility testing with fill-finish materials adds significant lead time when incompatibilities emerge. Assessing material compatibility across transfer interfaces requires specialized materials-science and validation expertise. Incompatibility drives substantial ancillary component, testing, and requalification spend that is hard to predict upfront.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish disruption during CSTD implementation and changeovers
rows.plain: The buyer wants changes to a closed transfer system and the related equipment switches to interrupt sterile filling for less time. This helps the change happen without a long production pause.
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish disruption during implementation is a schedule/production concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Implementation disruption duration is variable and hard to predict for sterile lines. Fill-finish disruption during implementation and changeover directly extends downtime. Minimizing changeover disruption requires validation and production expertise. Production disruption during changeover carries high opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of CSTD component price escalation beyond contracted assumptions over the expected lifecycle
rows.plain: The prices of parts used to transfer materials in a closed and protected way should not rise much more than the contract expected over the equipment’s useful life. Any extra increase should be kept very small.
rows.imp_rat: Component price escalation beyond assumptions is a cost concern unrelated to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation clauses are inconsistently enforced, so overruns beyond assumptions recur. Negotiating escalation caps adds contracting time. Structuring escalation protection requires commercial expertise. Uncapped price escalation erodes projected savings over the lifecycle, the central concern.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of freight, service, support, or minimum-purchase charges excluded from the CSTD contract baseline
rows.plain: Include delivery, service, support, and required minimum-purchase charges in the basic cost of the special transfer equipment contract. This shows the contract's real cost.
rows.imp_rat: Excluded freight/service charges are a contract-baseline error unrelated to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Ancillary charges are routinely omitted from baselines and surface later unpredictably. Capturing all ancillary charges into the baseline takes diligent review time. Identifying hidden freight, service, and minimum-purchase charges needs contracting experience. Excluded charges surprise the budget after commitment, the key waste here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of incremental purchasing and production disruption cost caused by CSTD supply interruptions
rows.plain: Reduce extra purchasing costs and production interruptions caused when supplies of the special transfer equipment stop unexpectedly. This helps keep production running and controls costs.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption cost from supply interruptions is a supply-chain/financial concern not tied to containment (relevance low) with operational-cost consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: CSTD supply disruptions are not fully preventable and their cost impact is inconsistently controlled. Supply interruptions force rushed expediting that consumes urgent time. Mitigating disruption cost requires supply-planning judgement that varies in maturity. Interruptions drive incremental purchasing and production disruption costs that are the crux of this need.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of CSTD inventory cost lost through expiration, specification changes, or demand reductions
rows.plain: As little as possible should be lost on closed-transfer parts that expire, become unsuitable after requirements change, or are no longer needed. This helps avoid paying for supplies that cannot be used.
rows.imp_rat: Inventory cost lost to expiration/spec changes is a supply-waste concern unrelated to the CFJ; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Obsolescence from spec or demand shifts recurs despite planning. Adjusting stock to spec changes or demand drops takes managed effort. Minimizing obsolescence requires inventory-planning judgement. Expiration, spec changes, and demand reductions strand inventory cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of lifecycle disposal cost attributable to used CSTD components and packaging
rows.plain: Keep the cost of throwing away used parts and packaging as low as possible over the equipment’s entire life. This helps prevent disposal expenses from being overlooked when buying it.
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle disposal cost is a waste/cost concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Disposal requirements are known but costs vary with waste classification. Arranging compliant hazardous-waste disposal adds handling time. Managing disposal of contaminated CSTD components needs EHS expertise. Disposal of used components and packaging is a recurring lifecycle cost often underestimated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which projected hazardous-drug containment benefits lack decision-grade evidence before capital authorization
rows.plain: Make sure there is strong enough evidence, before money is approved, that the new system will protect workers from dangerous medicines. This helps decision-makers approve the investment with confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Containment benefits lacking decision-grade evidence is a business-case ambiguity not tied to the physical job (relevance low) with authorization-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Projected containment benefits are typically asserted rather than evidenced, so authorization rests on unverifiable claims. Assembling evidence is slow but insufficiency of evidence is the true gap. Producing decision-grade containment benefit evidence needs industrial-hygiene measurement expertise rarely on hand. Weak evidence risks funding the wrong scope, wasting capital indirectly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of quality, validation, and regulatory requirements unresolved in the capital request before authorization
rows.plain: Make sure all quality checks, required tests, and legal or safety requirements are resolved before asking for approval to spend the money. This prevents funding from being approved while important issues are still open.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved requirements in the capital request is a procurement-completeness ambiguity peripheral to the CFJ (relevance low) with authorization-delay consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Capital requests frequently reach authorization with open quality and regulatory items because no gate forces their resolution first. Resolving quality and regulatory questions before authorization stretches timelines but timing is secondary. Interpreting USP 800, validation, and regulatory requirements demands specialized quality expertise the purchaser depends on others for. Unresolved requirements later trigger rework costs, an indirect loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of requested capital exceeding available funding at authorization
rows.plain: Make sure the requested investment does not cost more than the money available to approve it. Otherwise, the purchase cannot be fully funded.
rows.imp_rat: Requested capital exceeding funding is a budget-constraint defect not tied to physical containment (relevance low) with authorization consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established finance controls make funding-ceiling breaches fairly detectable, though late scope additions still cause surprises. Comparing request to available funding is quick within normal budgeting cadence. Basic budget-fit checks are routine finance skills widely available. Standard budgeting practice reasonably flags when requests exceed available funding before authorization.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among the scope, risk, and economic assumptions used by decision stakeholders during capital authorization
rows.plain: Make sure decision-makers use the same understanding of the work involved, its risks, and its financial effects when approving major spending. This helps them approve funding with a shared view of the choice.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent authorization assumptions are a governance-alignment issue, not core to release prevention; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Different stakeholders routinely carry divergent assumptions into authorization, undermining decision consistency. Reconciling assumptions across stakeholders slows authorization but consistency is the goal. Aligning scope, risk, and economic assumptions requires facilitation and analytical skill. Inconsistent assumptions cause misfunding, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time the capital request remains pending in required governance reviews before authorization
rows.plain: Keep a request for money to buy equipment from waiting too long for the required management reviews and approval. Faster approval helps the purchase move forward when it is needed.
rows.imp_rat: Capital request pending in governance is a schedule concern unrelated to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Queue times vary with committee cadence, making pending duration unpredictable. Requests routinely sit in governance queues longer than planned, delaying authorization. Expediting through governance needs relationship and process knowledge. Pending time defers benefit, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the authority required to release approved capital for the containment capability
rows.plain: Make clear who has the authority to release the approved money for the containment equipment. This prevents delays or unauthorized spending.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in capital-release authority is a governance concern unrelated to physical containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Authority to release capital is usually documented, so ambiguity is limited but not eliminated. Ambiguity over release authority can delay capital deployment. Understanding delegation-of-authority matrices is standard finance knowledge. Ambiguity delays spend rather than wasting it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that implementation cannot fit approved shutdown and qualification windows after capital authorization
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the project cannot be completed during the approved equipment shutdown and testing periods after the money is approved. This matters because missing these limited time periods could delay production.
rows.imp_rat: Implementation not fitting shutdown windows is a schedule concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Whether implementation fits approved windows is uncertain because timelines slip after authorization. Fit to shutdown windows is inherently a scheduling problem. Planning implementation into narrow qualification windows needs experienced project planning. Missing the window can idle capital and force rescheduling costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material hazardous-drug release consequences lacking defensible economic valuation in the investment case
rows.plain: Make sure the financial plan puts a trustworthy dollar value on the important harm that could result if hazardous medicine escapes. This helps people judge the investment using the full possible impact.
rows.imp_rat: Release consequences lacking economic valuation is a business-case defect not tied to physical containment (relevance low) with decision-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Most release consequences lack any credible dollar figure today, so the economic case is chronically thin and inconsistent. Economic valuation work is time-consuming but the missing valuation itself is the core problem. Assigning defensible economic value to exposure consequences requires rare health-economics and actuarial skill. Without monetized consequences the investment case cannot justify spend, weakening every cost argument.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material lifecycle cost elements omitted from the hazardous-drug containment investment case
rows.plain: Make sure the investment plan includes all important costs of keeping hazardous medicine contained from purchase through the end of its use. This prevents the real cost from being understated.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted lifecycle cost elements in the investment case is a business-case defect not tied to physical containment (relevance low) with decision-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle models vary widely in completeness, so omissions recur across cases. Lifecycle costing takes effort but omission, not time, is the loss. Building a complete lifecycle cost model needs specialized TCO skill unevenly present. Material lifecycle cost elements are often omitted, understating true ownership cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of baseline release and exposure records with missing, inconsistent, or unverifiable data in the investment case
rows.plain: Make sure the starting information about product release and exposure records is complete, consistent, and verifiable in the investment case. This helps decision-makers rely on the analysis.
rows.imp_rat: Baseline records with unverifiable data is a business-case data defect not tied to physical containment (relevance low) with decision-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Baseline release and exposure records are chronically incomplete, inconsistent, or unverifiable, giving the investment case an unreliable factual foundation. Reconciling records is time-consuming but data quality is the core issue. Verifying baseline exposure data requires data-governance and IH skill rarely applied to capital cases. Bad baseline data undermines every economic figure, indirectly wasting capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of error in projected fill-finish downtime and throughput disruption during implementation of the containment capability
rows.plain: Make the estimate of lost final product filling, sealing, and production capacity during installation as accurate as possible. This helps show the true effect of putting the new containment equipment in place.
rows.imp_rat: Error in projected downtime is a forecasting concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Downtime and throughput-disruption estimates are frequently wrong, making implementation impact uncertain. Downtime estimation errors surface during implementation, not as delay per se. Forecasting fill-finish downtime accurately needs production-engineering expertise. Downtime forecast error causes budget surprises.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation among acquisition, qualification, and operating cost estimates from commercial proposals in the investment case
rows.plain: Keep the purchase, approval-testing, and operating-cost estimates from different suppliers reasonably consistent in the investment plan. This makes the financial calculation more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Variation among cost estimates is a business-case rigor issue peripheral to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Commercial proposals vary in what they include, so estimate consistency is moderate. Reconciling proposals takes some time but variance is the issue. Normalizing cost estimates across proposals needs procurement analytical skill. Wide estimate variation makes the budgeted cost basis unreliable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among finance, procurement, quality, safety, and production stakeholders about investment assumptions in the financial case
rows.plain: Make sure Finance, Procurement, Quality, Safety, and Production agree on the assumptions used to estimate the investment's costs and benefits. This makes the financial case more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Stakeholder disagreement on assumptions is a governance-alignment concern unrelated to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Stakeholders commonly disagree on financial-case assumptions, undermining consensus. Unresolved disagreement delays the case secondarily. Aligning finance, quality, safety, and production on assumptions needs facilitation skill. Assumption disagreement can misfund the case.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of projected economic benefit counted more than once in the hazardous-drug containment investment case
rows.plain: Avoid counting the same expected financial benefit more than once in the investment case for containing hazardous drugs. This gives a more trustworthy estimate of the project’s value.
rows.imp_rat: Double-counting economic benefit is a business-case error unrelated to containment performance; ranking caps it at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit double-counting slips through when models are built ad hoc, reducing case reliability. Double-counting checks add little to timelines. Detecting double-counted benefits needs disciplined financial-modeling skill. Overstated benefits inflate the case rather than waste cash directly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material economic assumptions not evaluated across plausible operating conditions in the investment case
rows.plain: Check important money-related assumptions under a reasonable range of operating conditions. This shows whether the investment still makes sense when circumstances change.
rows.imp_rat: Assumptions not stress-tested is a business-case rigor issue peripheral to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Most cases evaluate assumptions at a single point rather than across plausible conditions, weakening robustness. Sensitivity analysis takes time but its absence, not delay, is the failure. Testing assumptions across operating conditions requires modeling expertise many cases lack. Untested assumptions can hide cost exposure, an indirect risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of financial-case delays caused by missing cost, benefit, risk, or production evidence before submission
rows.plain: Spend less time delaying the financial case because important information about cost, benefits, risks, or production is missing. This helps the buyer submit a complete case for approval sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Financial-case delays from missing evidence is a schedule concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence-gap delays recur because evidence sources are fragmented and slow. Missing evidence chronically delays financial-case submission. Assembling cost, benefit, risk, and production evidence needs cross-functional expertise. Delay defers benefit rather than spending cash.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unplanned capital required for hazardous-drug transfer points omitted from the acquisition scope
rows.plain: Include the cost of transfer points needed to move hazardous medicines, even if they were missed in the original purchase plan. Adding them later can require unexpected investment money.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned capital for omitted transfer points is a scoping/budget error peripheral to the CFJ (relevance low) with financial consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: The recurrence of such omissions shows the scoping process is unreliable at catching all transfer points. The rush to fund omitted transfer points causes delay but the pain is the unplanned spend. Spotting every transfer point up front needs operational expertise not held by the funder. Omitted transfer points routinely force unplanned supplemental capital requests after the fact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unbudgeted capital required for facility modifications discovered after budget approval
rows.plain: Include the cost of changes to the building or site that the equipment will need. Discovering these changes later can require more money than was approved.
rows.imp_rat: Unbudgeted facility-modification capital is a financial-estimate error unrelated to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Early scoping frequently misses facility impacts, so post-approval discoveries are common. Discovering facility mods late delays projects secondarily. Anticipating facility modifications requires facilities-engineering assessment often skipped early. Facility mods discovered post-approval force sizable unbudgeted capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of capital rendered unusable by transfer interfaces incompatible with existing fill-finish assets
rows.plain: Avoid spending money on transfer connections that cannot work with the filling and finishing equipment already in place. Otherwise, part of the investment may be unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Capital lost to incompatible interfaces is a procurement/compatibility waste unrelated to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility is checkable but not always verified up front, so mismatch surprises still occur. Compatibility problems surface late, but the loss is stranded capital not delay. Verifying transfer-interface compatibility with fill-finish assets needs engineering expertise. Incompatible interfaces render purchased capital unusable, a direct waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unbudgeted capital required for qualification deliverables omitted from the approved estimate
rows.plain: Include the cost of all required checks, tests, and records in the estimate. If they are left out, extra funding may be needed later.
rows.imp_rat: Unbudgeted qualification capital is a financial-estimate error unrelated to release prevention; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification scope is inconsistently captured, so unbudgeted deliverables recur. Qualification omissions surface late but the loss is unbudgeted spend. Knowing which qualification deliverables to budget needs validation expertise the funder lacks. Omitted qualification deliverables force unbudgeted capital after approval.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which supplier pricing exceeds the basis approved for the containment investment
rows.plain: The supplier’s price should stay close to the amount approved for this protective equipment investment. Any extra charge should be kept very small.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier pricing exceeding basis is a cost concern unrelated to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Contract pricing controls make basis breaches fairly detectable. Not a timing need. Enforcing pricing against approved basis is standard procurement practice. Supplier pricing can exceed the approved basis, causing overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of capital added by late changes to containment and sterile-processing requirements after budget approval
rows.plain: Prevent late changes to requirements for keeping materials contained and handling them safely without germs from adding to the approved budget. This helps keep the investment affordable after approval.
rows.imp_rat: Capital added by late requirement changes is a rework/cost concern unrelated to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement churn after budget approval remains common, adding cost unpredictably. Late changes delay projects secondarily. Freezing containment and sterile-processing requirements needs strong requirements discipline. Late requirement changes add unbudgeted capital directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of capital committed to transfer capacity beyond forecast production demand
rows.plain: Do not spend money on more transfer capacity than the expected production volume needs. This avoids paying for equipment that will not be used.
rows.imp_rat: Capital committed beyond demand is a capacity-waste concern unrelated to the CFJ; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Demand forecasts are uncertain, so capacity over-commitment recurs. Not a timing need. Sizing capacity to demand needs demand-forecasting skill. Over-provisioned transfer capacity ties up capital beyond need.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unbudgeted capital caused by omitted freight, duties, taxes, and installation charges in the approved estimate
rows.plain: Include delivery, government charges, taxes, and setup in the approved estimate. Leaving them out can create unexpected extra investment costs.
rows.imp_rat: Unbudgeted capital from omitted charges is a financial-estimate error peripheral to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Landed-cost omissions are common but well-understood, so mature procurement teams catch them more reliably than most gaps. Not a timing issue. Estimating freight, duties, taxes, and installation is a standard procurement competency. These charges are frequently omitted from estimates, causing predictable overruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of added capital caused by quoted pricing expiring during authorization of the containment investment
rows.plain: Avoid extra investment costs caused by supplier prices expiring while approval is still being completed. This helps keep the corrective project within its planned budget.
rows.imp_rat: Capital added by expired quotes is a cost/schedule concern peripheral to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Quote-expiry surprises happen when authorization drags, but are largely manageable. Quote expiry is tied to authorization timing. Managing quote validity windows is routine procurement work. Expired quotes force re-pricing at higher cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of potential loss from hazardous-drug exposure incidents left uninsured or unreserved after investment authorization
rows.plain: Make sure money or insurance is set aside for possible losses from workers being exposed to hazardous medicines after the investment is approved. This reduces the financial impact of such an incident.
rows.imp_rat: Uninsured exposure loss is a financial-risk concern; though exposure-adjacent, ranking caps it at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Residual exposure is partly covered by standard insurance and reserves, but gaps remain uncertain. Not a timing need. Quantifying uninsured exposure loss needs actuarial and risk-financing skill. Uninsured or unreserved exposure loss is a direct financial risk left on the table.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which projected incident and compliance savings are overstated in the investment risk assessment
rows.plain: Expected savings from fewer incidents and fewer rule-related costs should be based on realistic estimates. Overstating these savings could hide the true financial risk of the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Overstated savings in risk assessment is a business-case error peripheral to preventing release; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Incident and compliance savings are commonly overstated because they rest on optimistic, hard-to-verify assumptions. Not a timing-driven need. Calibrating savings estimates needs actuarial and safety judgement in short supply. Overstated savings misjustify spend, an indirect capital risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between forecast and actual hazardous-drug transfer demand over the investment horizon
rows.plain: Make sure the predicted need to move hazardous drugs is close to the amount that will actually be needed during the planned investment period. This helps reduce the risk of spending too much or too little money.
rows.imp_rat: Transfer-demand forecast deviation is a forecasting concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Long-horizon transfer demand is inherently uncertain, so deviation is common. Not a timing need. Forecasting transfer demand over the horizon needs planning expertise. Demand-forecast deviation causes over- or under-investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of fill-finish output lost through implementation-related disruption of the containment capability
rows.plain: Avoid losing production of medicines during the work needed to install or change the system that keeps them protected from contamination. This helps prevent the project from reducing the amount that can be made and sold.
rows.imp_rat: Output lost through implementation disruption is a production-waste concern unrelated to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: How much output is lost is uncertain and depends on execution quality. Output loss stems from downtime, a timing effect secondarily. Protecting output during implementation needs experienced operations planning. Lost fill-finish output is a direct economic loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in recurring per-transfer costs over the investment horizon
rows.plain: Keep the cost of each transfer from changing unpredictably over the planned life of the investment. This makes future spending easier to predict and lowers financial risk.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in per-transfer costs is a cost concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Recurring per-transfer costs vary with usage patterns, giving moderate consistency. Not a timing need. Forecasting per-transfer cost variation needs consumable-usage analysis skill. Per-transfer cost variation erodes budget predictability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material payment, service, liability, and termination obligations unresolved at authorization
rows.plain: Resolve important questions about payments, services, responsibility for losses, and ending the agreement before approval. This limits unexpected financial problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contract obligations is a commercial-terms concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Established contracting practice resolves most terms, though some reach authorization open. Resolving contract terms adds time secondarily. Negotiating payment, service, liability, and termination terms is standard procurement/legal work. Unresolved obligations can create later cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from funding authorization until the hazardous-drug containment capability is usable in production
rows.plain: Make it faster to put protection against dangerous medicines into use after the money has been approved. This helps protect workers and allows production to begin sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time to usable capability is a schedule concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Lead times vary, so time-to-usable is only moderately predictable. Time from authorization to production use is often long due to procurement, install, and qualification lead times. Compressing this timeline needs experienced implementation management. Extended time-to-use defers benefit, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the funded containment capability becomes unusable or noncompliant before payback
rows.plain: The funded equipment for containing hazards should remain usable and meet required rules until it has paid for itself. Otherwise, the investment could become a loss.
rows.imp_rat: Funded capability becoming unusable before payback is an investment-risk concern not tied to live containment (relevance low) with financial consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory shifts and product changes make it moderately uncertain whether the funded capability stays compliant through payback. Obsolescence before payback is a long-horizon issue, not immediate delay. Forecasting compliance longevity of a CSTD needs regulatory foresight that is scarce. A capability going unusable before payback strands invested capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of investment exposed to supplier insolvency, discontinuation, or service failure over the investment horizon
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of money at risk if the supplier goes out of business, stops making the product, or can no longer provide support during the planned period of use. This protects the investment over time.
rows.imp_rat: Investment exposed to supplier insolvency is a commercial-risk concern peripheral to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier due diligence reduces but does not eliminate insolvency and discontinuation risk over the horizon. Supplier failure impacts are long-horizon, not immediate delay. Assessing supplier viability is a standard supply-risk competency. Supplier failure can strand investment, a direct exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material lifecycle cost categories omitted from the ownership model for the containment capability
rows.plain: Make sure the ownership estimate includes all important costs over the full life of the containment equipment. This prevents the long-term cost from being understated.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted lifecycle cost categories are a financial-modeling error with no direct bearing on containment during transfer; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Category completeness depends on the analyst, making the ownership model inconsistently reliable. Assembling the ownership model is not primarily time-bound. Identifying all cost categories requires procurement expertise in consumable-heavy devices. Omitted cost categories understate ownership cost, causing later budget overruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between forecast and actual containment-device use across fill-finish transfers
rows.plain: Make sure the predicted use of sealed devices that keep drugs contained during transfers is close to actual use. This helps avoid unexpected costs while the equipment is owned and operated.
rows.imp_rat: Device-use forecast deviation is a planning concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Device-use forecasts deviate from actuals fairly often. Not a timing need. Forecasting device use across transfers needs usage analytics. Forecast-actual deviation drives inventory and spend waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of recurring consumable spending above the approved lifecycle budget for hazardous-drug transfers
rows.plain: Keep ongoing spending on disposable supplies for moving hazardous medicines within the approved budget for the equipment’s full life. This prevents long-term costs from exceeding the plan.
rows.imp_rat: Consumable spending above budget is a cost-control concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Consumable overruns recur because usage forecasts are imprecise. Not a timing need. Controlling consumable spend needs usage-tracking discipline. Consumable spending routinely exceeds the lifecycle budget for high-volume transfers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage by which acquisition and recurring prices exceed lifecycle projections for the containment capability
rows.plain: Keep the equipment’s purchase price and ongoing costs from rising too far above the amount expected over its entire life. This helps the containment system remain financially worthwhile.
rows.imp_rat: Prices exceeding projections is a cost-control concern unrelated to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Price-forecast accuracy varies, so overruns against projections recur. Not a timing need. Forecasting acquisition and recurring prices accurately needs market and TCO expertise. Actual prices often exceed lifecycle projections, causing overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of fill-finish production disruption during implementation of the containment capability
rows.plain: Keep production interruptions as short as possible while putting the new protection against contamination into place. This helps limit the full cost of buying and using it.
rows.imp_rat: Production disruption during implementation is a schedule concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Disruption duration varies with implementation execution, giving moderate predictability. Fill-finish disruption during implementation directly consumes production time. Minimizing disruption needs skilled changeover and project planning. Lost production time carries real revenue cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer components repurchased because they are incompatible with fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: The person handling the purchase wants to avoid buying replacement transfer parts because the first parts do not work with the final filling and packaging process. This reduces waste and the total cost of ownership.
rows.imp_rat: Repurchased incompatible components is a procurement waste unrelated to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Component-compatibility verification is uneven, so repurchases recur. Repurchase cycles add delay secondarily. Matching components to fill-finish requirements needs technical procurement skill. Incompatible components get repurchased, a direct duplicated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification activities repeated after post-authorization requirement changes to the containment capability
rows.plain: Avoid repeating tests after approval because the required ability to keep harmful material contained has changed. This saves time and testing costs.
rows.imp_rat: Repeated qualification activities is a rework concern unrelated to the containment CFJ; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Post-authorization requirement changes still trigger qualification repeats fairly often. Repeated qualification consumes time secondarily. Managing requirement stability needs strong change-control discipline. Repeated qualification wastes labor and materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unused transfer inventory discarded after expiry or fill-finish requirement changes
rows.plain: Throw away less unused material that must be moved between steps because it expires or because production requirements change. This reduces waste and its cost.
rows.imp_rat: Unused inventory discarded is a supply-waste concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement changes and expiry cause recurring discard losses. Not a timing need. Managing inventory to expiry needs inventory-control skill. Expired or obsolete transfer inventory is discarded, a direct waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of maintenance, training, and support expense above lifecycle projections for the containment capability
rows.plain: Keep the cost of upkeep, training, and support for the containment system from exceeding the amount expected over its life. This helps the system remain affordable to operate.
rows.imp_rat: Support expense above projections is a cost-control concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Support-cost projections are moderately reliable but often optimistic. Not a timing need. Projecting maintenance, training, and support costs needs lifecycle costing skill. These recurring expenses frequently exceed projections, causing overspend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of competing investment cases using noncomparable economic, risk, and planning assumptions during capital prioritization
rows.plain: Make sure competing investment proposals use the same kinds of financial, risk, and planning assumptions. This makes it fairer to decide which needs should come first.
rows.imp_rat: Noncomparable competing investment cases are a prioritization concern unrelated to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Competing cases commonly use noncomparable assumptions, making prioritization unreliable. Not primarily a timing need. Enforcing comparable assumptions across cases needs portfolio-level analytical discipline. Noncomparable cases misallocate capital, an indirect loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of decision-critical exposure-reduction claims unsupported by evidence when ranking competing capital needs
rows.plain: Support with evidence the claims that a proposed purchase will reduce important risks when comparing competing major needs. This helps leaders rank projects on facts rather than unsupported promises.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure-reduction claims unsupported when ranking needs is a business-case defect not tied to physical containment (relevance low) with prioritization-quality consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Competing capital needs are routinely ranked on unsupported exposure-reduction claims, making prioritization unreliable. Ranking needs is not primarily a timing issue. Judging exposure-reduction claims across competing needs requires analytical rigor that is scarce. Unsupported claims misallocate capital, a downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unresolved disagreement over investment priority among finance, safety, quality, and fill-finish stakeholders
rows.plain: Help finance, safety, quality, and medicine-packaging teams agree quickly on which investment should come first. This ensures limited funds go to the most important need.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved priority disagreement is a governance-decision concern unrelated to the CFJ; capped.
rows.sat_rat: How long disputes persist is unpredictable, given shifting priorities. Priority disputes among stakeholders drag on, delaying funding decisions. Resolving priority disputes needs executive facilitation skill. Prolonged disputes defer containment benefit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of authorization delay beyond the applicable capital-planning window for the containment investment
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for approval when a containment project misses the period in which it was planned for funding. This helps the project compete for funding before planning decisions are closed.
rows.imp_rat: Authorization delay beyond the planning window is a schedule concern, not physical containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Whether authorization lands within the window is inconsistent given competing agenda items. Authorization frequently slips past the capital-planning window because reviews and evidence gaps stall approval. Navigating the approval calendar needs process savvy that experienced sponsors hold. Delay defers containment benefits rather than directly wasting cash.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required containment and implementation scope left unfunded after capital prioritization
rows.plain: Provide funding for all required work to contain the problem and put the solution into place, even after choosing which investments come first. This prevents important work from being left without money.
rows.imp_rat: Unfunded containment scope after prioritization is a budgeting error not the containment mechanism itself (relevance low); its harm is deferred capability rather than immediate release (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because prioritization is inconsistent across cycles, whether required scope gets funded is unpredictable. Prioritization cycles are lengthy but the core loss is unfunded scope, not time. Deciding which containment scope survives prioritization needs finance and clinical judgement that is unevenly available. Capital prioritization frequently leaves required containment scope unfunded, forcing the role to absorb gaps or return for more money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of reserved capital diverted to unplanned mandatory facility or compliance needs before authorization
rows.plain: Keep money set aside for this purchase from being used for unexpected required building or compliance work before the purchase is approved. This protects the planned funding.
rows.imp_rat: Reserved capital diverted to other needs is a budget-instability concern not tied to physical containment (relevance low) with funding consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Whether earmarked capital survives to authorization is unpredictable given competing mandatory demands. Diversions happen at any time, not a timing failure. Protecting reserved capital requires political and financial savvy that varies by sponsor. Reserved containment capital is frequently raided for unplanned mandatory needs, a direct monetary loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of material demand, compliance, or facility assumption changes during capital prioritization
rows.plain: They want to avoid frequent changes to the material needs, rule-related needs, or building assumptions while deciding which major investments matter most. This makes it easier to compare priorities and choose wisely.
rows.imp_rat: Assumption changes during prioritization is a planning-instability concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Material assumptions shift during prioritization fairly often, undermining case stability. Assumption changes disrupt schedules secondarily. Stabilizing demand and facility assumptions needs planning expertise. Assumption churn can invalidate the cost basis.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the funding request is rejected for failing governance thresholds for the containment investment
rows.plain: The funding request should meet all the required approval rules for the safety investment. This helps prevent it from being rejected for a formal reason.
rows.imp_rat: Funding request rejected for governance thresholds is a budget/process defect not tied to physical containment (relevance low) with authorization consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Governance thresholds are known, so well-prepared cases pass, but containment cases still fail when evidence is thin, giving moderate reliability. Rejection forces re-cycling that costs time, but the failure is the rejection itself. Crafting a case that clears governance thresholds needs experienced capital-planning judgement. A rejected request delays containment spend rather than directly wasting money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required exposure, quality, compliance, and economic evidence missing from the approval package
rows.plain: Include all required information about worker exposure, product quality, rule-following, and financial impact in the funding request. This gives approvers enough information to make a sound decision.
rows.imp_rat: Missing evidence from the approval package is a documentation-completeness defect remote from the physical job (relevance low) with authorization consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approval packages routinely arrive with sizable evidence gaps because no unified standard defines what 'complete' means. Gathering the full evidence package takes long, but incompleteness is the dominant failure. Compiling exposure, quality, compliance, and economic evidence spans multiple specialties the purchaser cannot cover alone. Missing evidence leads to rejected or under-funded cases, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among finance, quality, safety, and fill-finish leaders about governance criteria for funding approval
rows.plain: Finance, quality, safety, and final-production leaders should agree on the rules used to decide whether funding is approved. Clear shared rules make funding decisions more consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement on governance criteria is a process-alignment concern peripheral to release prevention; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Governance-criteria disagreement among leaders is frequent, reducing decision consistency. Disagreement over criteria delays approval secondarily. Aligning leaders on governance criteria needs cross-functional negotiation skill. Criteria disagreement risks funding wrong scope.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the funding request is routed to an incorrect governance authority during approval
rows.plain: The funding request should go to the right decision-makers for approval. Sending it to the wrong authority could delay or derail the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Funding request mis-routed in governance is a process concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Governance routing is generally well-defined, so misrouting is relatively uncommon. Misrouting causes rework delay secondarily. Knowing the correct governance authority is routine for experienced sponsors. Misrouting wastes effort rather than money directly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of required investment left unfunded after governance review of the containment capability
rows.plain: Make sure all the money needed for the required protection against harmful exposure is funded after the review. This prevents an approved project from being left short of money.
rows.imp_rat: Unfunded investment after governance review is a budgeting error remote from live containment (relevance low) with deferred-capability consequence (severity low). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Outcomes of governance review vary case to case, making residual unfunded amounts hard to predict. Governance review adds delay, but the felt loss is the funding shortfall itself. Anticipating what governance will trim requires seasoned insight into board priorities. Investment required for containment is often left partially unfunded after governance review, a direct monetary shortfall.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the funding request waits for required governance reviews before approval
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for the required internal reviews of a funding request before it can be approved. This helps the purchase move forward sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Funding request waiting for reviews is a schedule concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Review scheduling is fairly predictable even if slow, giving moderate consistency. Waiting for scheduled governance reviews is a recurring, largely fixed delay. Little special skill mitigates the waiting itself. Waiting defers containment value rather than spending money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of investment-scope changes that invalidate governance approvals already obtained for the containment investment
rows.plain: Make it less likely that changes to the investment plan will cancel or reopen approvals that were already granted. This helps the project keep its funding and move forward.
rows.imp_rat: Scope changes invalidating approvals is a governance-rework concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Late scope changes that invalidate approvals occur regularly, hurting reliability. Rework from invalidated approvals costs time secondarily. Locking scope before approval needs disciplined governance skill. Invalidated approvals waste prior effort and can add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting financial, procurement, safety, and scope data elements across approval documents
rows.plain: Make the approval papers agree about the money, purchasing details, safety, and work that will be included. This helps decision-makers approve the same plan rather than conflicting versions.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting approval data elements are a document-consistency issue peripheral to containment; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Approval documents frequently contain conflicting data because they are compiled from disconnected sources without reconciliation. Reconciling documents adds effort but the defect is contradiction, not delay. Cross-checking financial, procurement, and scope data needs meticulous coordination skill. Conflicting data can trigger wrong funding decisions, an indirect loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about continuity of supply after reviewing the source and commercial terms
rows.plain: The buyer should quickly know whether supplies will continue to arrive after reviewing the source and business terms. This supports confidence in a dependable supply.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about supply continuity is a commercial-risk concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of anxiety while service, recovery, and support commitments remain unresolved before sign-off
rows.plain: The buyer wants less worry while waiting for the supplier to settle its promises about service, repairs, and help before approval. This matters because the buyer needs confidence that support will be available after the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over unresolved support commitments is an emotional concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that the commercial terms protect against component obsolescence and discontinuation over the investment horizon
rows.plain: Make sure the agreement protects the buyer if parts are later discontinued or become unavailable during the years the investment is expected to last. This supports a reliable supply of replacement parts.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about obsolescence protection is a commercial-terms concern unrelated to the CFJ; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to explain who bears supply, service, or obsolescence risk before authorization
rows.plain: The person wants to know who would be responsible if supplies, repairs, support, or outdated equipment become a problem before approving the purchase. This matters because responsibility for these risks should be clear in advance.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over risk-bearer ambiguity is an emotional/commercial concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether the authorized scope satisfies hazardous-drug containment and sterile-manufacturing requirements
rows.plain: Know sooner whether the approved work will safely contain dangerous drugs and support making germ-free products. This matters so problems are found before the work moves ahead.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty whether scope meets requirements is a decision-confidence concern unrelated to physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt caused by conflicting interpretations of requirements governing hazardous-drug transfers in sterile fill-finish
rows.plain: Make sure everyone agrees on the rules for safely moving dangerous medicines during sterile drug production. This helps prevent unsafe handling and questions about whether the work was authorized.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting requirement interpretations is an ambiguity concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence that the authorized investment can withstand audit scrutiny
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while waiting for proof that the approved spending can stand up to an audit. This matters because they need confidence that no problems will be found with their authorization.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting audit-resilience evidence is an emotional concern peripheral to release prevention; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to explain residual audit exposure before authorizing the investment
rows.plain: The person wants to know and explain what inspection problems could still remain before approving the investment. This matters because they do not want to authorize a purchase that leaves them responsible for unresolved issues.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over residual audit exposure is an emotional concern unrelated to the CFJ; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of confidence erosion when acquisition cost and lifecycle economics support conflicting authorization conclusions
rows.plain: Reduce loss of confidence when the purchase price and the long-term costs and savings point to different funding decisions. A clear explanation of both views helps support a sound authorization.
rows.imp_rat: Confidence erosion over conflicting economics is a decision-confidence concern peripheral to containment; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty about the funding decision while finance and fill-finish leaders await direction
rows.plain: Make the funding decision clear quickly while finance and medicine-packaging leaders are waiting for direction. This lets them act without guessing about the available money.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty about the funding decision is a demeanor concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when stakeholders challenge budget headroom or omitted lifecycle costs before authorization
rows.plain: The buyer needs to stay calm when others question whether the budget has enough room or whether future costs were left out before approval. This helps them present a complete and trustworthy cost picture.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over budget headroom questions is a poise concern unrelated to containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes when late cost or utilization changes alter the expected economics before authorization
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries when late changes in costs or usage make the expected financial value look different before approval. This helps them feel sure they are still making a sound purchase decision.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over late economics changes is an emotional concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a hazardous-drug containment audit finding is attributed to the authorizer's decision before regulators or senior leadership
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an audit finds a problem with controls for containing dangerous medicines and publicly blames the person who approved the investment. The approval should be supported by careful review of the safety protections.
rows.imp_rat: Audit finding attributed to authorizer is a reputational concern; ranking caps it at 1 below the prior batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility loss with quality, safety, and sterile-manufacturing leaders when the funded capability is judged insufficient
rows.plain: Protect trust with quality, safety, and germ-free manufacturing leaders by ensuring the approved equipment and process can actually do what was promised. The funded solution must be sufficient for the intended work.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when capability judged insufficient is reputational, not physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the authorizer is bypassed in later hazardous-drug safety investment decisions after the authorization is questioned
rows.plain: The person who approved the spending should not be excluded from later safety investments involving dangerous medicines just because the earlier approval was questioned. This keeps responsibility and safety judgment clear.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizer bypassed in later safety decisions is a reputational/org concern peripheral to the containment CFJ; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholders portray the authorization as favoring acquisition cost over hazardous-drug containment and worker protection
rows.plain: The person approving the purchase wants to avoid being seen as choosing a cheaper option at the expense of safely containing dangerous medicines and protecting workers. The decision should show that worker safety and containment were properly considered.
rows.imp_rat: Portrayal of favoring cost over protection is a reputational concern peripheral to physical containment; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership attributes a containment-investment budget breach to the authorizer's judgment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that senior leaders blame the person who approved the spending if the containment project goes over budget. The investment decision should be financially well supported.
rows.imp_rat: Budget breach attributed to authorizer is a reputational/cost concern unrelated to release prevention; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility loss with finance governance when claimed lifecycle economics prove inaccurate after authorization
rows.plain: Avoid losing finance leaders’ trust when the promised total cost over the equipment’s life turns out to be wrong after the purchase is approved. The expected spending and savings should be realistic.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when economics prove inaccurate is a reputational concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership portrays cost discipline as compromising required hazardous-drug containment in the funded scope
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that top leaders think saving money weakened the required protection against exposure to dangerous medicines. The funded equipment must control costs without leaving workers or products inadequately protected.
rows.imp_rat: Leadership portraying cost discipline as compromising containment is a reputational framing concern; capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the authorizer is bypassed in future capital decisions after prior stewardship is judged unsound
rows.plain: Future major spending decisions should continue to include the person responsible for approving them, even if earlier money-management decisions were criticized. This preserves accountability and avoids making decisions without the right expertise.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizer bypassed in future decisions is an org-dynamics/reputational concern unrelated to release prevention; capped.
